Nell and Saf hide inside the festival’s temporary “Vampire Crypt,” a fibreglass coffin positioned beneath the pier. From inside, they overhear Councillor Vale arguing with a security contractor about keeping teenagers away from the restricted maintenance area until after the dawn ceremony. Vale refers to “the Grant business” and says the council cannot survive another scandal attached to the pier. Nell records only the final, ambiguous seconds because her wet screen refuses to cooperate. Once Vale leaves, Saf reveals that the real blood on her sleeve came from finding a bloody strip of fabric snagged on the locked maintenance gate. It resembles Jamie’s school scarf. Nell wants to call the police; Saf insists they first need to know whether Jamie is actually there. They realise 317 may refer to a locker in the seafront arcade where Milo worked weekends. As they leave, Nell receives another message from Milo’s number: **TWO OF THREE. LATE AS USUAL.** ## Chapter 3: Locker 317 Nell and Saf cross the deserted high street towards the closed arcade. The journey provides space to reveal the fracture in their friendship. Nell blames Saf’s dares and appetite for trouble for Milo being beneath the pier; Saf counters that Nell knew Milo was investigating something and kept quiet because she wanted no part of it. They break into the arcade through a window that has remained unrepaired for months. The darkness is punctuated by malfunctioning machines, recorded laughter, and an animatronic fortune-teller repeatedly announcing bankruptcy. Locker 317 contains Milo’s stolen traffic cone, a disposable camera, a memory card, and an envelope labelled: **FOR WHEN ST MERCY FINALLY FALLS INTO THE SEA.** Before they can inspect everything, a festival security volunteer enters. Nell and Saf hide among the horror attractions while arguing in whispers. Their escape involves triggering every machine at once and convincing the volunteer that a papier-mâché corpse is a drunk tourist. Outside, they review the memory card on Saf’s phone. It contains photographs taken beneath the pier before Milo died: cracked joints, corroded supports, and pages from an engineering report. One photograph shows Vale meeting a council surveyor. Another shows Milo pointing at a warning notice later removed from the site. The report pages are incomplete, but the visible language recommends “immediate restrictions” and warns against “concentrated public loading.” Nell understands enough to realise the dawn festival crowd could be dangerous. The final file is a short video. Milo jokes about uncovering “the least glamorous conspiracy in British history” and says he has hidden the full report somewhere adults never willingly look. Behind his voice is a faint electronic chime. Nell recognises it as the hourly announcement from the town’s memorial garden. The chapter ends when they hear a crash from inside the locked garden and discover a trail of blood leading through its gates. ## Chapter 4: The Third of Three Nell and Saf follow the blood trail through the memorial garden, passing plaques for town benefactors and an embarrassingly misspelled bench dedicated to Milo. The scene looks ominous, but Nell notices that much of the blood contains glitter from festival supplies. They find Jamie hiding inside the small maintenance shed. He has a genuine cut above one eye, a festival blood pouch in his pocket, and Milo’s old phone in his hand. Jamie admits sending the messages. The phone was not lost in the sea; Milo left it at home on the night he died because he was using an old action camera. Jamie recently found Milo’s notes while clearing his room. He contacted officials, but Vale dismissed the photographs as old and accused him of exploiting the anniversary. Jamie staged the bloody clues to force Nell and Saf to follow Milo’s trail. He also used the phrase “come alone” because he did not want them involving adults who might confiscate the evidence. Saf is furious at being manipulated. Nell is more disturbed by how quickly she believed, however briefly, that Milo might be communicating with them. Jamie reveals that he entered the restricted area earlier and was chased by security. His cut came from falling against a gravestone, while the bloody scarf was deliberately planted. He claims the complete report is hidden in the memorial garden, based on Milo’s clue about somewhere adults never look. They decode the clue as Milo’s own memorial bench, whose misspelled plaque has never been corrected because nobody from the council has inspected it. Beneath the plaque they find a waterproof envelope. It contains the full engineering report and Milo’s action-camera memory card. The report confirms that the pier’s safe capacity was drastically reduced eleven months before Milo’s death. The dawn ceremony expects nearly a thousand attendees. A message from Vale appears on the festival volunteers’ group chat: all staff are to report early because the ceremony has been moved forward by forty-five minutes to avoid worsening weather. They now have less than two hours. ## Chapter 5: Proper Channels Nell insists they take the report to someone official. Saf and Jamie expect it to disappear again, but Nell argues that charging onto the pier with stolen paperwork will only make them look unstable. They go to the temporary festival control room in the community hall. It is filled with fake cobwebs, blood dispensers, stressed volunteers, and laminated safety procedures. Nell attempts to explain the danger to the duty manager while Saf antagonises him by filming. Councillor Vale arrives. He does not deny the report but claims it is obsolete, preliminary, and superseded by later verbal advice. He frames Milo as a thrill-seeker whose trespassing caused his own death. He also reveals that Jamie broke into a restricted site and that Nell and Saf stole property from the arcade. Vale takes the physical report “for verification” and instructs security to contact their parents. Nell’s partial recording from beneath the pier proves nothing. Saf attempts to upload the photographed pages, but the hall’s network is overloaded and Vale has her removed from the volunteer system. The confrontation fractures the group. Saf accuses Nell of handing Vale the only evidence. Nell accuses Saf and Jamie of turning everything into another Milo-style stunt. Jamie says both girls abandoned Milo’s family after his death and have no right to decide how his evidence is used. In the argument, the truth about the fatal night begins to emerge. Saf says she told Milo that Nell thought his investigation was pathetic. Nell says Saf had promised to stop him going beneath the pier. Neither knows what Milo actually did after their last messages. Security locks them inside a backstage costume room while waiting for parents and police. Through the wall, they hear Vale order the festival technicians to begin the dawn sequence early. Nell spots that the room contains the controls and spare equipment for the festival’s projection system. She also realises Milo’s action-camera card is still hidden inside her bicycle helmet. ## Chapter 6: The Worst Available Plan Nell, Saf, and Jamie review Milo’s action-camera footage on a projector. It shows the underside of the pier on the night he died. Milo speaks directly to the camera, explaining that both Nell and Saf told him not to go, though for different reasons. He went anyway because he wanted proof dramatic enough to force the council to act. The footage records Milo finding a visibly damaged maintenance walkway. It ends before his fall, but it disproves the story that either girl dared him there. It also captures Vale’s voice ordering a worker to remove a closure notice until after the previous year’s festival weekend. Nell and Saf confront the guilt they have displaced onto each other. Saf admits she blamed Nell because anger was easier than acknowledging she could not control Milo. Nell admits she let people think Saf was responsible because it protected her own reputation. They do not fully reconcile, but they agree to stop using Milo as a weapon. Nell devises the “worst available plan”: use the festival’s own audiovisual system to project the report and Milo’s footage onto the large gauze screen above the pier entrance. Saf can access the stage rigging through volunteer routes. Jamie knows where Vale’s confiscated documents are being kept. Nell will reach the control booth and interrupt the ceremony. They escape the costume room using a collapsible ceremonial coffin and a poorly secured fire door. The sequence is comic but tense, with Nell forced to remain inside the coffin while volunteers wheel it towards the pier. Nell secretly sends the footage to her night-shift journalist aunt, Mara, with instructions to publish it if Nell does not call within twenty minutes. This provides insurance without transferring control of the climax to an adult. At the pier, crowds are already assembling in rain ponchos and vampire costumes. Vale begins his opening speech. Saf and Jamie split off. The chapter ends when the coffin containing Nell is carried onto the pier rather than towards the control booth. ## Chapter 7: The Bleeding Sunrise Nell emerges from the coffin in the middle of the ceremony, creating a distraction Saf uses to enter the projection gantry. Tourists applaud because they assume Nell is part of the performance. Jamie retrieves the report from Vale’s office but is caught by a security contractor. Vale continues the ceremony while privately ordering Nell removed. Nell grabs a live microphone and warns the crowd that the pier is unsafe. Her panicked delivery and blood-streaked clothes make the warning sound like scripted horror. Saf successfully interrupts the promotional film. Milo’s photographs appear on the main screen, followed by the engineering report. Vale cuts the sound and claims the material is a malicious anniversary prank. Nell refuses to flee. She speaks plainly about Milo’s death, the report, and the council’s choice to delay action. Jamie escapes and restores Milo’s action-camera audio. Vale’s recorded instruction to remove the closure notice plays over the loudspeakers. The crowd begins to understand, but movement towards the exit creates the concentrated load the report warned about. A corroded railing gives way near Jamie, and part of the maintenance walkway collapses beneath Saf. Nell must cross a narrow service platform to reach Saf. She is terrified and moves slowly rather than performing an implausible leap. Jamie and several festival workers help redirect the crowd while Nell secures Saf with decorative stage rope. The damaged section falls into the sea, spattering the pier with red festival dye from ruptured ceremonial containers. The limited collapse confirms the danger without causing fatalities. Vale can no longer dismiss the evidence. Emergency services arrive as Mara publishes the footage and report online. Vale attempts to describe the evacuation as a planned precaution, still wearing plastic fangs. Nell, Saf, and Jamie reach solid ground together as dawn breaks behind the closed pier. ## Chapter 8: Low Tide The immediate aftermath unfolds in the grey morning. The pier is closed indefinitely. The council announces an independent inquiry using language designed to suggest nobody has yet done anything wrong. Vale resigns from the festival committee “to prevent distraction” but does not confess or apologise. Milo’s death will be formally re-examined. The press initially portrays Nell, Saf, and Jamie as heroic whistle-blowers, then misquotes them and publishes an old school photograph in which they all look twelve. Nell is uncomfortable with becoming part of a cleaner public story than the truth deserves. At the hospital, Jamie receives stitches. He apologises for using Milo’s phone and the emergency code. Nell tells him the manipulation was cruel even though the danger was real. Saf makes him promise never to use fake blood as a navigational aid again. Nell and Saf talk privately outside, watching red dye wash through the gutters towards the sea. They acknowledge that neither caused Milo’s decision and that neither can reduce him to a victim or a saint. Nell apologises for allowing Saf to carry the town’s blame. Saf apologises for cutting Nell off rather than telling her how frightened and angry she was. They do not promise to become best friends again. Saf asks whether Nell wants tea after they finish giving statements. Nell says yes, provided the café has passed a structural inspection. At home, Nell’s mother is furious, relieved, and unsure which emotion should be expressed first. Nell’s abandoned tracking-phone deception has failed because she accidentally left her phone’s location attached to the family dog, creating a record that suggests the dog spent the night sending messages from the pier. Nell finally sleeps. Near midday, her phone buzzes with a message from Saf: a photograph of her white trainers, permanently stained red by festival dye. Saf sends: **🩸** Their old code for a minor disaster. After considering the weight the symbol carried only hours earlier, Nell replies: **🩸🩸🩸** The story ends with Saf typing back, restoring a small, ordinary connection without pretending that everything has been repaired.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Writer Might Approach the Piece A human writer is likely to begin less from plot machinery than from the peculiar emotional charge of the opening: > The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸 The line combines menace with bathos. Three blood emojis could announce murder, menstruation, melodrama, a private joke, or somebody being an idiot. The distinctly YA energy lies in that uncertainty. A human writer may ask not merely, “What does the code mean?” but: - Who is the one person the protagonist least wants to hear from? - What private history could make three silly symbols genuinely frightening? - Why send emojis instead of words? - What does the protagonist do in the first ten seconds that reveals who they are? - What embarrassing explanation would they prefer to the frightening one? - What frightening explanation would they prefer to the true one? The best version may grow from a small, specific emotional contradiction. Nell might desperately hope the text is a prank because a prank would mean Milo’s number is being used by someone living. Or she might know immediately that Jamie sent it but resist admitting this because responding would drag her back into a family she has avoided. Such reactions can be more human than a carefully staged sequence of mystery clues. A human approach would also be likely to protect the story’s oddness. It might allow the code’s origin to be embarrassingly trivial, the town festival to be badly organised rather than neatly thematic, and Milo’s evidence to be incomplete or compromised. Real memories and private jokes rarely possess perfect symbolic unity. The three drops need not correspond elegantly to three friends, three clues, three acts, and three stages of grief. One strong meaning is enough. The writer may also start with voice. Before devising the engineering report or festival schedule, they might draft Nell lying awake, seeing Milo’s name on her screen, and reacting in a way that is defensive, petty, frightened, and funny. If Nell’s voice works, she can carry a simpler plot. If it does not, no amount of structural planning will make the story feel fully alive. For an 8,000-word story, a human writer may distrust the urge to include every attractive idea. The emotional premise already contains: - A dead friend. - A broken friendship. - A younger brother using the dead friend’s phone. - A suspicious death. - Civic negligence. - An unsafe pier. - A festival. - A public climax. - A partial collapse. - A media aftermath. That is enough material for a novel or novella. A human writer may choose to leave some causes unexplained, compress the antagonist’s role, or make the physical danger much smaller. Short fiction often feels larger when it withholds rather than when it resolves every strand. A human writer would also pay close attention to local texture. “British” should not be delivered chiefly through rain, councils, weak tea, plastic fangs, and officious volunteers. Those are useful, but they can become a kit of recognisable signifiers. A convincing St Mercy-on-Sea would emerge from details that do not seem chosen to prove Britishness: - The one bus shelter whose roof has been missing since Easter. - A chip shop still using an old town slogan on its napkins. - The smell beneath the pier: salt, fryer oil, wet rope, and somebody’s strawberry vape. - A cash machine that charges £1.99 and is treated as a personal betrayal. - A community Facebook argument about whether the festival counts as satanic. - A memorial bench installed facing a public bin. - A parent who calls every energy drink “Red Bull.” - The specific way teenagers know which alley is safe, which camera is fake, and which adult will tell their mum. These details should arise through use rather than decorative description. Finally, a human writer might let the story be morally untidy. Milo may have discovered real danger and still exaggerated it. Vale may be dishonest and still sincerely believe cancelling the festival would ruin local businesses. Jamie may be manipulative in a way that cannot be absolved by being right. Nell and Saf may not exchange balanced apologies. One may have behaved worse. Their friendship may resume for reasons that are not wholly healthy. That lack of symmetry is often where human truth enters. # Common Telltale LLM Approaches ## 1. Over-Engineering the Premise LLM-generated plans often make every element perform several perfectly coordinated functions. Here: - Three drops represent three friends. - The blood emojis connect to a blood-themed festival. - Fake blood conceals real blood. - Red corrosion echoes blood. - Red dye enters the sea at dawn. - The private code returns in the final text. - The “bleeding sunrise” becomes the climax. - The friendship and pier both need repair. This is elegant, but the elegance risks becoming visible as design. Human stories frequently contain motifs, but they do not always line up so comprehensively. If every red object is meaningful, the reader may feel the author arranging symbols behind the scenery. For this piece, the central image is already strong. Repetition should be selective. A single ugly red tide of festival dye may be powerful; repeated red imagery in every chapter could make the symbolism feel manufactured. ## 2. Excessive Causal Cleanliness LLMs favour plots in which: - Every clue points somewhere. - Every object returns. - Every misunderstanding is explained. - Every character receives an arc. - The climax settles the internal and external conflicts simultaneously. - The denouement addresses all consequences. This can create satisfaction, but it can also feel like a screenplay outline generated from structural doctrine. The current plan is exceptionally orderly. Each chapter has a location, clue, reversal, and hook. The action proceeds from pier to arcade to memorial garden to community hall to control room to pier. Nothing is wasted, but little is allowed to breathe. A human-feeling story might contain: - A clue that turns out to be Milo showing off. - A location they visit for the wrong reason. - A conversation that changes their understanding but not the investigation. - An interruption that is socially important but structurally inconvenient. - Evidence that raises questions rather than answering them. - A plan that fails for an unanticipated mundane reason. Messiness should not mean arbitrary plotting, but causality need not resemble a polished chain of escape-room puzzles. ## 3. “Theme Delivery” Dialogue LLM characters often state the emotional thesis in balanced, articulate terms: - “I blamed you because anger was easier.” - “You used Milo as a weapon.” - “Neither of us caused his decision.” - “Grief did not give you the right to manipulate us.” These are coherent and psychologically plausible, but teenagers in crisis may not speak with therapeutic precision. They may: - Focus on one apparently trivial detail. - Revive an old insult. - Deny what is obvious. - Make a joke at the worst moment. - Apologise for the wrong thing. - Say “Fine” when nothing is fine. - Communicate through remembered facts rather than emotional labels. - Understand each other without achieving a polished verbal resolution. The planned emotional revelations should be translated into behaviour, evasions, half-sentences, and specific accusations. ## 4. Symmetrical Character Arcs Nell is cautious and must learn to act. Saf is reckless and must learn to accept help. Each begins at one pole and moves towards balance. This is sound craft, but it is also recognisably schematic. People do not always possess complementary flaws arranged for mutual correction. Nell’s caution might be situational rather than a defining trait. Saf’s recklessness might be partly a story Nell tells about her. Perhaps Saf has been more responsible since Milo’s death, while Nell still treats her as the reckless one. That discrepancy would add depth. Likewise, the ending need not demonstrate equal growth. One girl may reach for reconciliation while the other cannot yet manage it. One may understand the past first. Imbalance can feel more honest than mutual, simultaneous insight. ## 5. Safely Calibrated Darkness The plan takes care to ensure: - Milo’s death is accidental. - No one deliberately killed him. - The collapse causes no fatalities. - The antagonist is culpable but not monstrous. - The injury is minor. - The friendship is damaged but hopeful. - The ending is unresolved but reassuring. Each choice is defensible. Collectively, they can produce a conspicuous safety rail around the story. “Darkly comic” does not require cruelty or mass casualties, but it does benefit from emotional risk. The current version repeatedly promises danger and then limits it to a manageable level. The larger risk need not be physical. A bolder version might allow: - The evidence to show Milo caused some of the damage he documented. - Nell to have betrayed him before his death in a way that genuinely mattered. - Saf to publish something private that cannot be taken back. - Jamie’s manipulation to fracture the family’s trust. - The exposure to harm local workers alongside guilty officials. - The council to escape meaningful consequences. - Nell and Saf not to reconcile that morning. Darkness can come from irreversible choices rather than dead bodies. ## 6. Wrapping Everything With a Bow While Claiming Not To LLM stories often signal sophistication by saying the ending is “hopeful but incomplete,” then still provide: - Public vindication. - A closed dangerous location. - An inquiry. - Reconsideration of the dead friend’s reputation. - The antagonist’s resignation. - Mutual apologies. - Tea together. - A comic parental beat. - A motif callback. - Renewed texting. That is, in practice, a considerable bow. The plan explicitly rejects a perfectly tidy reconciliation but supplies almost every other form of closure. A more human ending may choose only one or two consequences. Perhaps the pier closes, but the girls do not know whether the report will change anything. Perhaps they sit outside A&E and fail to talk about Milo. Then the final text arrives days later, not immediately. Or perhaps the text callback is omitted because it is too neat. ## 7. The Antagonist as an Institutional Spokesman Councillor Vale is designed as a plausible, bureaucratic antagonist, yet he risks becoming a familiar composite: - Smarmy public figure. - Protects tourism. - Minimises a report. - Discredits grieving teenagers. - Orders evidence contained. - Uses procedural language. - Wears comic plastic fangs. - Is captured on a recording saying the incriminating thing. - Resigns without admitting fault. This is less cartoonish than a murderer, but still convenient. He personifies an entire system and gives the climax a face. The story may become more unsettling if responsibility is dispersed. Vale might not have personally removed the warning. He may have received a sanitised summary from somebody else. He could be guilty of refusing to ask questions rather than issuing a clearly incriminating order. The more quotable his wrongdoing, the more easily the story can defeat him. Institutional failure is usually harder to expose because nobody says, “Remove the closure notice until after the festival.” ## 8. The Convenient Recording LLM mysteries frequently rely on: - A hidden camera. - An accidentally captured confession. - A saved voice note. - A livestream. - A public projection. - A journalist receiving a backup copy. The current climax uses several of these at once. Milo’s camera captures Vale’s voice; the protagonists project it; Nell sends it to a journalist; the crowd sees it; the media publishes it. This solves evidentiary and publicity problems efficiently, but it feels engineered. A recording can still work, especially in YA, but it should be partial, ambiguous, badly framed, damaged, or emotionally uncomfortable. The teenagers should need to interpret it, and adults should be able to dispute it. The structural failure itself might be more persuasive than a perfect audio clip. ## 9. The Public Spectacle Climax Hijacking a festival’s sound and projection system is energetic, visual, and familiar. It appears frequently in contemporary capers and YA mysteries because it allows private truth to become public in one cinematic sequence. The danger is genericity. Once a story reaches “the protagonist takes the microphone while evidence appears on the big screen,” the reader may predict the rest. The crowd first thinks it is a performance; the villain denies it; proof plays; danger confirms the warning. A less polished climax might be smaller and stranger: - Nell pulls a fire alarm, but half the crowd assumes it is part of the event. - Saf cuts power to the pier and plunges everyone into darkness. - Jamie steals the ceremonial key, physically preventing a gate from opening. - They sabotage the festival’s insurance compliance rather than publicly expose the truth. - Nell makes an anonymous bomb threat, creating a morally troubling evacuation. - A local vendor recognises Milo’s photographs and begins moving people before officials do. - They force closure by causing a minor, controlled failure—raising questions about whether they have become reckless themselves. The climax should emerge from these particular teenagers, not merely from the available audiovisual technology. ## 10. “Quirky British” Ornamentation The plan contains many comic touches: - Plastic fangs. - A fibreglass coffin. - Weak systems and laminated procedures. - A malfunctioning fortune-teller. - A traffic cone. - A misspelled memorial bench. - A dog-location app error. - Red-stained white trainers. - A ceremonial coffin escape. Individually, these are amusing. Together, they may create a curated “British quirk” atmosphere. The risk is that each scene arrives with an accessory intended to produce charm. Human comedy is often less ornamental. It can come from: - The exact thing somebody refuses to admit. - A social hierarchy among volunteers. - A mother texting increasingly restrained messages. - Nell knowing Saf is lying because of an old verbal habit. - The humiliating practical consequences of wet clothing. - An adult using a child’s old nickname at a devastating moment. - Somebody still owing somebody £6.40. - The town’s residents recognising the girls and making the wrong assumptions. The comic world should not feel stocked with props. ## 11. Trying to Be Impressive at the Expense of Readability The intention is articulate and thematically comprehensive, but there is a risk the eventual prose will attempt to fulfil every announced purpose. If each chapter carries: - Blood symbolism. - Social satire. - Engineering clues. - Grief subtext. - British local colour. - Banter. - A reversal. - A cliffhanger. - Character development. the prose may become dense and self-conscious. The reader needs a clean surface: a girl gets a frightening text and goes outside. Complexity should accumulate beneath that line of action. The first-person narration should not be witty in every paragraph. Constantly polished observations make a teenage narrator sound authored rather than alive. Some sentences should be plain, especially when Nell is frightened. ## 12. Generic Contemporary YA Voice A common synthetic YA voice combines: - Self-deprecation. - Hyperbolic metaphors. - Lists of unlikely disasters. - Snark about adults. - Pop-cultural comparisons. - Abrupt emotional sincerity. - Repeated constructions such as “Because apparently…” - Parenthetical jokes. - Statements like “This was fine. It was not fine.” Nell’s dry voice could easily drift into this register. Avoiding it will require specificity in syntax and worldview. Her humour should reflect her own obsessions, education, family, and relationship with Saf. If she is rule-bound, perhaps she notices signage, opening hours, insurance wording, and exits. Her metaphors might come from risk assessments and school bureaucracy rather than generic internet sarcasm. # Deep Critique of the Intention ## The Core Premise Is Strong but Potentially Overloaded The best element is the emotional hook: a message from a dead friend’s phone using a private emergency code. It creates immediate dread, history, and obligation. The damaged trio gives the three emojis meaning without requiring exposition at the start. The difficulty is that the plan then adds a civic thriller large enough to compete with that premise. The unsafe-pier plot is thematically appropriate, but the story may split into two different experiences: 1. A psychologically eerie story about grief, blame, and a dead friend’s phone. 2. An adventure about exposing a suppressed engineering report before a festival. The first is intimate and emotionally strange. The second is procedural and cinematic. A human-feeling version should decide which experience dominates. At present, the plan tries to give both equal weight, and the second may swallow the first after the midpoint. The strongest question may not be “Can they evacuate the pier?” but “What did Milo ask of them, and what did they fail to do?” The pier danger should intensify that question rather than replace it. ## Nell Risks Being a Functional Protagonist Nell is described through familiar useful traits: - Intelligent. - Anxious. - Observant. - Rule-bound. - Dryly funny. - Afraid but capable. These make her ideal for a YA mystery, but they do not yet make her singular. The plan specifies her arc more clearly than her ordinary life. What does she want before the text? What has she been doing in the year since Milo’s death? What is shameful about her that has nothing to do with the mystery? Possibilities include: - She has begun using Milo’s death in her university application. - She compulsively reads the public comments about his accident. - She secretly visits the pier webcam. - She has become friends with someone who openly dislikes Saf. - She lied at the inquest. - She resents Milo for dying and resents everyone for making that unsayable. - She is planning to leave St Mercy and has not told her mother. - She has kept an object that belongs to Saf and pretends she threw it away. - She has deleted almost all of Milo’s messages except one unkind exchange. Such details would make her feel less like “the cautious one” and more like a person. ## Saf Risks Existing as Nell’s Foil Saf’s function is currently to: - Act where Nell hesitates. - Distrust authority. - Provoke conflict. - Carry visible anger. - Need to learn cooperation. - Receive Nell’s apology. This makes her legible but perhaps too instrumental. Her life since Milo’s death should exist beyond the night’s plot. Why is she volunteering at a festival associated with the pier? That could be a rich contradiction. Maybe: - Her mother runs a food stall and needs the money. - She volunteered to monitor the event because she does not trust the organisers. - Vale recruited her as a piece of “youth engagement” optics. - She wants to reclaim the pier from Milo’s death. - She has become obsessive about first aid and crowd safety. - She is not actually reckless anymore; Nell simply refuses to update her image of Saf. The last option is especially useful. Saf might be the one who has brought gloves, a charger, and a first-aid kit, while Nell expects chaos from her. That would quietly undermine the planned complementary archetypes. ## Milo Is at Risk of Becoming a Device The intention says Milo will not be idealised, which is wise. Yet he still performs a remarkable number of posthumous functions: - He invented the code. - He connected the trio. - He discovered the danger. - He took the photographs. - He stole the report. - He hid the evidence. - He recorded jokes. - He recorded exculpatory statements. - He captured Vale’s incriminating voice. - His death motivates the plot. - His final footage resolves the girls’ guilt. Even if he is described as reckless and vain, this degree of usefulness may turn him into a plot-delivery system. His clues also make him seem uncannily prepared for a story that occurred after his death. To humanise him, some evidence should be useless, embarrassing, or wrong. He might have misunderstood part of the report. His video might show that he was attempting to frighten the council with exaggerated claims. He may say something cruel about Nell or Saf. The final recording should not conveniently release both girls from guilt. It could instead reveal that he lied to each of them. The surviving characters should have to construct meaning from incomplete material rather than receive Milo’s posthumous verdict. ## Jamie’s Motivation Is Plausible but His Plotting Is Too Expert Jamie is fourteen and grieving, yet he: - Understands the code’s power. - Sends carefully timed messages. - Plants fake blood. - Leaves a scarf clue. - Directs Nell and Saf through Milo’s evidence trail. - Lures them to multiple locations. - Enters a restricted site. - Locates evidence adults overlooked. - Anticipates official interference. This is a polished mystery-game operation. It risks making him feel like a screenwriter inside the story. A more human Jamie would make mistakes: - He sends the text impulsively and has no full plan. - The photograph is old, not taken that night. - The blood trail is unnecessary and embarrasses him when challenged. - He loses Milo’s phone. - He expects Nell and Saf to arrive together but accidentally worsens their conflict. - He misunderstands one of Milo’s notes. - His attempt to recreate Milo’s style is transparently bad. - He lies about being chased because he wants to seem less frightened. His manipulation should create chaos rather than an efficient trail. ## The Engineering Threat May Be Too Abstract Until the Climax “Concentrated public loading,” “capacity restrictions,” corrosion, and reports can be credible, but they are not automatically vivid. In 8,000 words, too much explanation will slow the story. Too little will make the danger feel like a generic excuse for action. The story needs one or two concrete physical signs: - A bolt turning freely beneath Nell’s fingers. - A support vibrating when a delivery cart crosses above. - Rust falling like grit into Jamie’s hair. - A gap widening between boards. - A railing shifting under the pressure of one person. - A worker quietly telling them not to stand together. This sensory evidence may be more effective than pages of reports. The report can confirm what the characters and reader have already felt. ## The Moral Framing of Negligence Is Almost Too Well Judged The plan carefully avoids a cartoon villain and identifies “institutional cowardice” as the antagonist. This is thoughtful, but perhaps over-formulated. Fiction can become didactic when its moral complexity is announced in advance. The town’s economic dependency should be felt through people, not explained as a theme. For example: - Saf’s mother will lose a week’s takings if the festival is cancelled. - Nell’s mother works at a hotel that is full for the first time in months. - The arcade owner knew the pier was unsafe but feared closure would kill the seafront. - Milo’s family accepted council compensation that included a confidentiality clause. - The festival paid for Milo’s memorial bench. These complications would make exposure cost something. At present, stopping the festival is clearly correct and the only meaningful obstacle is official dishonesty. If innocent people have understandable reasons to resist cancellation, the choice becomes more human. ## The Intended Britishness Needs Deeper Social Specificity The current intention promises British voice but remains socially broad. A seaside town is not a neutral background. The story should know: - Whether St Mercy is in Kent, Sussex, Dorset, Lancashire, Yorkshire, Wales, or elsewhere. - What its local economy is. - Whether Nell and Saf attend an academy, sixth-form college, grammar school, or comprehensive. - Their class positions. - Their ethnic and religious family contexts. - How local power operates. - How easy it is to reach the next town. - Whether tourism is resented, needed, or both. - What the pier means to residents beyond the festival. Safiya Hussain should not function merely as an energetic British Muslim-coded friend with a food-stall family unless her identity is rendered with care. Nell’s whiteness or class position should also not remain unmarked. Their falling-out may have been interpreted differently by school staff or the town because of those differences. The story need not become sociological, but the relationships will feel more real if social context creates friction. # Deep Critique of the Chapter Plan ## Chapter 1: Three Drops ### What Works - The opening message is immediate. - Milo’s old number creates a powerful second hook. - Nell’s reluctance to respond can reveal character. - The nighttime departure gives the story momentum. - Ending on the blood-soaked figure is an effective chapter turn. ### Risks The chapter packs in too many hooks: - Festival anniversary. - Dead friend. - Impossible phone. - Private code. - Mysterious call. - Pier photograph. - Bloody handprint. - Ominous caption. - Secret departure. - Blood-soaked figure. This intensity may feel mechanically suspenseful. The line “BEFORE THE SUNRISE BLEEDING. COME ALONE” is especially theatrical. Jamie, a fourteen-year-old trying to imitate Milo, might write something overdramatic, but if so the awkwardness should be detectable. As written, it sounds like a thriller clue. The phone not being recovered is also later contradicted by the revelation that Milo left it at home. If Nell believes it was never recovered, she must have misunderstood what happened, or the family must have concealed the truth. Either possibility requires explanation. It may feel contrived that Milo’s phone sat at home for a year while everyone believed it was lost. Nell’s location-sharing trick with a second phone risks becoming gadgety. It may be funnier and more believable if she simply leaves her phone at home, then has to return for it, or if she forgets about tracking entirely and receives a call from her mother at the worst moment. The ending image of Saf in a blood-soaked white dress leans towards cinematic staging. Why is she wearing a costume after midnight? A volunteer shift can explain it, but the image feels selected for effect before it feels lived. ### Humanising Possibilities - Keep the first message alone on the page and allow silence around it. - Make the second message less polished: a photo with no caption, or “pier. now.” - Let Nell recognise Jamie’s spelling or Milo’s punctuation without consciously admitting it. - Give Nell a petty reason for being awake, such as checking whether Saf has posted about the anniversary. - Have her send an ordinary response—“who is this”—and hate herself for it. - Let the fear come from the sender name, not added gothic language. - End with Saf saying something banal such as, “You took your time,” while visibly injured. ## Chapter 2: No Questions Asked ### What Works - Bringing Saf in early prevents the story from becoming a solo puzzle. - Their suspicion of each other is emotionally useful. - Hiding while overhearing Vale creates tension. - The old rhythm resurfacing involuntarily could be poignant. ### Risks The chapter relies on several familiar devices: - Fake blood mixed with real blood. - A symbolic signature. - A numbered clue. - Hiding in a coffin. - Overhearing the antagonist discussing the cover-up. - Recording only an ambiguous fragment. - Another teasing text at the end. This resembles an adventure serial. The clues are conspicuously authored, and Vale’s conversation arrives at exactly the right time. His mention of “the Grant business” sounds like dialogue written so hidden protagonists can hear relevant exposition. The fibreglass coffin is a comic prop rather than an inevitable location. If the story contains many such devices, it may feel staged. ### Humanising Possibilities - Nell and Saf do not overhear Vale; instead, he encounters them and behaves solicitously, which is harder to interpret. - “317” could be a number Nell misreads, sending them to the wrong place. - Milo’s gull drawing might be an old tag already present, not a fresh clue. - Saf might not have received the same message, making her presence more suspicious. - The blood on Saf’s clothing might have a mundane explanation she refuses to give because it is humiliating. - Their first interaction should include a concrete leftover grievance unrelated to Milo’s death. - Let Vale remember Nell’s name and not Saf’s, or vice versa, revealing social dynamics without exposition. ## Chapter 3: Locker 317 ### What Works - The arcade is a strong nocturnal location. - Milo’s stored objects can reveal personality. - The investigation gains substantive evidence. - Nell and Saf’s disagreement develops alongside action. ### Risks The locker is an “evidence box” containing exactly what the story needs: camera, memory card, envelope, photographs, report pages, and a video clue. It is a classic LLM bundle of plot utility. The malfunctioning fortune-teller announcing bankruptcy and papier-mâché corpse escape are clever but may be trying too hard. They signal “darkly comic British mystery” rather than arising naturally. Milo’s phrase about “the least glamorous conspiracy in British history” is a polished line designed to define tone. It could work, but if all his recordings contain such lines, he will sound like an authorial mouthpiece. The chapter also ends with another blood trail. Reusing the same cliffhanger weakens it. ### Humanising Possibilities - The locker contains mostly rubbish and one useful item. - The disposable camera has only six surviving photographs, several obscured by Milo’s thumb. - The memory card does not work immediately. - The report pages are mixed with schoolwork or humiliating personal material. - One item proves Milo lied to both girls. - The number 317 refers not to a locker but to £3.17, a debt or private joke. - The arcade sequence can be funny through the owner’s strict, absurd routines rather than malfunctioning horror props. - End with Jamie calling Nell from Milo’s phone and saying nothing, or with Nell realising somebody is physically close enough to watch them. ## Chapter 4: The Third of Three ### What Works - Revealing Jamie at the midpoint is structurally sensible. - His use of Milo’s phone is emotionally provocative. - The story’s objective shifts clearly. - The misspelled memorial plaque has the potential to express civic neglect. ### Risks Jamie’s confession risks becoming an exposition dump explaining every earlier mystery. The planned sequence resolves: - Who sent the text. - Why the phone exists. - Why fake blood was used. - Why the scarf was planted. - Why he wanted no adults. - What he found. - Where the report is hidden. - Why officials ignored him. - What the deadline is. This is extremely efficient and therefore potentially artificial. The complete report hidden beneath Milo’s memorial plaque is almost too symbolically perfect. It suggests Milo predicted his own memorial or that Jamie hid it there. If Milo hid it before dying, the bench did not exist. If Jamie hid it, the clue structure becomes even more staged. The moved-forward ceremony is a standard ticking-clock escalation. Useful, but obvious. ### Humanising Possibilities - Jamie admits only part of the truth; Nell and Saf catch him in further lies later. - He did not plant every clue. Some were left by Milo, some by Jamie, and he no longer knows which. - The report is not beneath Milo’s memorial but in an unglamorous municipal location. - The misspelled bench can remain as an emotional detail without holding evidence. - Jamie found the report in Milo’s room and brought them out because he wants help interpreting it. - His injury should affect the story—headache, nausea, fear—not merely authenticate the fake blood. - The festival deadline might already have been known, avoiding a convenient last-minute schedule change. ## Chapter 5: Proper Channels ### What Works - Attempting official channels is sensible and prevents the teenagers from behaving idiotically for plot purposes. - Vale’s use of their prior behaviour against them is plausible. - The group’s internal conflict peaks at an appropriate point. - Losing control of the evidence creates urgency. ### Risks The community hall scene may become a concentrated display of obstructive-adult tropes. Vale conveniently appears, takes the evidence, threatens parents, controls the network, undermines their credibility, and locks them in a room. The teenagers are proven right while the adult is wrong in every respect. Locking minors in a costume room may be legally and practically odd. It is done to create an escape sequence and place them near audiovisual equipment. The argument also threatens to become too explicit. Each character states the moral grievance required for the arc. The line about abandoning Milo’s family may be powerful, but it risks feeling strategically inserted. ### Humanising Possibilities - An adult believes them but cannot close the pier alone. - A police officer takes them seriously but must verify the report, creating delay rather than outright dismissal. - Vale does not confiscate the report; Nell voluntarily hands it over and then regrets it. - The group is not locked in—they are told to wait, and choosing to leave has consequences. - Their parents arrive, complicating rather than ending the story. - Saf’s mother needs the festival to continue, making the conflict personal. - Jamie’s accusation lands because it concerns a specific act: Nell stopped replying to his messages, or Saf skipped Milo’s birthday memorial. - The girls’ fight could focus on one message sent before Milo died, not broad statements of guilt. ## Chapter 6: The Worst Available Plan ### What Works - Watching Milo’s footage together creates an intimate emotional opportunity. - The characters regain agency. - Nell devising the plan demonstrates growth. - Sending a backup to Mara is sensible. ### Risks This chapter contains the most conspicuous plot convenience. Milo’s video: - Explains why he went. - Exonerates both girls. - Demonstrates the danger. - Captures Vale’s culpability. - Supplies publishable evidence. It is essentially a posthumous witness statement crafted to resolve the story. This risks draining the girls’ reconciliation of difficulty. The “worst available plan” is in fact a familiar and reasonably competent plan. Escaping inside a ceremonial coffin compounds the prop-driven tone. Mara the journalist is a safety mechanism. Her existence ensures the truth cannot be suppressed, reducing suspense. She also appears late as a useful profession rather than a fully realised relationship. ### Humanising Possibilities - The footage makes matters worse before it helps. - Milo says he told Saf he was going but lied to Nell, or vice versa. - His audio is mostly wind; one phrase can be interpreted several ways. - Vale is not recorded at all. - Nell and Saf choose to act without knowing whether either bears some responsibility. - Mara may be a local blogger nobody respects, not a journalist able to secure publication. - Nell sends evidence to a class group chat and immediately regrets surrendering control of it. - Their plan might be improvised in stages rather than announced as a complete strategy. - Remove the coffin escape unless it is indispensable. ## Chapter 7: The Bleeding Sunrise ### What Works - The setting provides strong visuals. - The crowd mistaking danger for performance suits the dark comedy. - A structural failure validates the threat. - Nell’s rescue can show courage without erasing fear. ### Risks This chapter is highly cinematic and highly familiar: - Hero emerges during ceremony. - Crowd applauds by mistake. - Hero grabs microphone. - Villain dismisses evidence. - Video plays. - Crowd believes. - Structure fails. - Friend hangs in danger. - Hero crosses narrow platform. - Emergency services arrive. - Villain makes ridiculous statement. The climax may feel like a streaming-series finale rather than short fiction. It also attempts to resolve truth, public perception, physical danger, friendship, and antagonist exposure simultaneously. Saf hanging from a collapsing walkway places her in a damsel-like position so Nell can complete her arc. Even with stage rope, it risks turning the friendship into an action metaphor. Vale wearing plastic fangs after the collapse is an obvious visual joke. It may be funny, but it is almost too perfectly satirical. ### Humanising Possibilities - The climax happens below the pier with only a handful of people, while the crowd remains oblivious. - The teenagers prevent access by jamming the turnstiles or cutting power. - The public never hears Milo’s evidence that morning. - Nell’s act of courage is social—making a serious accusation under her own name—rather than a physical rescue. - Saf saves Nell, complicating the planned arc. - Nobody hangs from a ledge; the structural failure is a sickening movement that forces evacuation. - Vale is absent from the climax, making the teenagers fight procedure rather than a person. - The crowd reacts in mixed ways: some leave, some film, some complain, some refuse to believe. - The protagonists’ intervention could cause a crush risk, making their righteous action morally complicated. ## Chapter 8: Low Tide ### What Works - The aftermath includes bureaucratic self-protection. - Jamie’s apology is necessary. - The location-app mistake offers a release of tension. - The final emoji callback is clear and accessible. ### Risks The chapter contains too many closing notes: - Pier closure. - Inquiry. - Vale resignation. - Re-examination of Milo’s death. - Media satire. - Jamie’s apology. - Rule about fake blood. - Nell and Saf’s mutual understanding. - Tea invitation. - Structural-inspection joke. - Mother reunion. - Dog-tracking joke. - Sleep. - Stained-trainers callback. - Three-drop reply. This is a textbook example of wrapping every thread. The final emoji exchange is especially neat. It transforms the symbol’s meaning, confirms renewed contact, and ends on a humorous image. That may satisfy, but it may also feel designed rather than discovered. The dog-tracking error is a sitcom beat that could trivialise the emotional aftermath. It also relies on technology behaving in an implausibly convenient way. ### Humanising Possibilities - End earlier, perhaps outside the hospital or on the closed promenade. - Leave official consequences mostly unknown. - Let Jamie apologise indirectly by returning an object. - Let Nell and Saf fail to articulate what happened. - If retaining the emoji callback, do not also include tea, mutual apologies, and media consequences. - Saf might send one drop and Nell leave it unanswered for several hours. - Nell may type three drops and delete them. - The final message could come from Milo’s number because Jamie has not yet changed the contact, producing discomfort rather than easy reassurance. - The stained trainers could be seen in person, avoiding a deliberately circular text ending. - End on a concrete image that does not explain its own symbolism. # Brainstorming Ways
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention The story will be a darkly comic British YA mystery unfolding over one wet night in St Mercy, a run-down seaside town on the south coast. It will remain tightly focused on the promise of its opening: > The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸 The three drops are a private emergency code once used by three friends: sixteen-year-old Nell Price, her estranged former best friend Safiya Hussain, and Milo Grant, who died after falling from beneath St Mercy Pier eleven months earlier. The message arrives from Milo’s old number. The story’s primary question will be emotional as well as practical: why has someone used a dead boy’s phone to summon the two girls who have spent almost a year blaming each other for his death? ## Plot and Scope The mystery will remain smaller and less polished than in the earlier plan. There will be no elaborate town-wide trail, perfect cache of evidence, public confession, or cinematic hijacking of a giant screen. Milo’s younger brother, fourteen-year-old Jamie, sent the message impulsively from Milo’s phone after finding a photograph and part of an old structural survey among Milo’s things. He believes they show that the pier was known to be unsafe before Milo died. Jamie has no master plan. He assumes the old code will make Nell and Saf come to him. When he panics and hides, the girls misinterpret the limited evidence he has left behind. This keeps the opening sinister without turning Jamie into an implausibly sophisticated puzzle-maker. St Mercy Pier is due to host the dawn finale of the town’s annual Night of the Revenant festival. The event’s blood-and-vampire decorations provide dark comic contrast and occasional confusion between fake and real blood, but the festival will not dominate every scene. It is a shabby, economically necessary local event rather than an extravagant gothic spectacle. Milo had photographed corrosion beneath the pier and obtained part of a preliminary survey recommending reduced capacity. The evidence is incomplete. It does not establish a murder or a grand conspiracy, nor does it wholly explain his death. Councillor Crispin Vale, the festival organiser, approved continued use of the pier after receiving a softened summary of the survey. His wrongdoing lies in choosing not to ask difficult questions because cancellation would hurt the town. The immediate danger is plausible and concrete: hundreds of people are due to gather on the oldest section of the pier for sunrise. Nell, Saf, and Jamie must persuade someone to restrict access or stop the ceremony. Their eventual success will come through a messy combination of partial evidence, direct physical warning signs, an adult willing to listen, and an improvised disruption—not through a perfect recording or public confession. The climax will be an evacuation rather than a spectacular collapse. A section of railing and several boards will give way after most of the crowd has been kept back. No one will hang dramatically over the sea, and Nell will not prove her growth through an action-film rescue. Her decisive act will be publicly making an accusation she cannot fully prove, knowing it may expose her own lie about the night Milo died. ## Characters and Emotional Conflict ### Nell Price Nell will narrate in first person. She is observant and careful, but these traits will not be treated as a generic anxiety archetype. She has spent the year since Milo’s death managing how others see her. During the inquest, she said she had not known Milo intended to go beneath the pier. In fact, he had texted her that evening. She did not encourage him, but she deleted the message because she feared being blamed. Nell has privately allowed the town to assume Saf was the reckless influence in the trio. Her central flaw is therefore not simply cowardice; it is her capacity to appear honest while arranging the truth into a safer shape. Her humour will be precise, occasionally unkind, and rooted in what she notices: signs, official wording, small hypocrisies, the difference between what people say and what they mean. When frightened, her prose will become plainer rather than more extravagantly witty. ### Safiya Hussain Saf will not merely be Nell’s reckless opposite. Since Milo’s death, she has become more practical: she carries a first-aid kit, knows the festival’s safety routes, and is volunteering because her mother’s café depends on festival trade. Nell continues to interpret her through an outdated image of the impulsive girl she used to be. Saf has her own fault. Milo told her he was planning to photograph the pier, and she sent him an angry voice note daring him to prove he was not bluffing. She tried to retract it, but she does not know whether he heard the retraction. She has carried the more visible portion of the town’s blame and resents Nell for remaining respectably silent. Saf’s family’s reliance on festival income complicates the decision to stop the event. She has a material reason to hesitate, even while believing the pier may be dangerous. ### Milo Grant Milo will appear only through old messages, one damaged camera card, and the surviving characters’ contradictory memories. He will not function as a posthumous detective who left a flawless solution. He was funny, attention-seeking, inventive, and careless with other people’s fear. His photographs are badly framed. His notes contain exaggerations. He did identify a real problem, but he also went beneath the pier partly because he wanted dramatic footage. Nothing he left behind fully absolves Nell or Saf. ### Jamie Grant Jamie uses Milo’s old phone because he knows Nell and Saf will respond to Milo’s code when they have ignored his earlier attempts to speak to them. This is manipulative, but not calculated beyond that first choice. He has a minor head wound from entering the restricted area, uses festival blood to make it look worse, and then becomes frightened by the consequences. Jamie is angry that the girls have converted Milo into a private tragedy while avoiding his family. He is also trying to imitate his older brother’s flair and discovering that dramatic gestures are harder to control than they look. ### Councillor Vale and the Adults Vale will be evasive rather than openly villainous. He has not issued a conveniently recorded order to conceal danger. He accepted the interpretation that allowed the festival to proceed and now resists anything that might force him to reconsider. Other adults will not all be useless. A council duty officer will listen but require evidence. Saf’s mother will initially resist cancellation because her café needs the money, then help once she sees the pier move under pressure. Adult responses will vary according to fear, money, loyalty, and limited knowledge. ## Voice and Style The prose will aim for clarity, immediacy, and specificity. It will avoid a relentlessly polished “YA snark” voice. Nell will not produce a clever comparison in every paragraph, nor repeatedly announce that things are “fine” when they are not. Humour will come from uncomfortable interactions and local realities: - Festival volunteers arguing about whether fake blood stains council tabards. - Saf using her first-aid kit while Nell realises she has brought deodorant as a weapon. - A café till still running while everyone discusses possible structural failure. - Vale speaking about “managing access” because he will not say “closing the pier.” - Tourists complaining that the safety barrier spoils their photographs. Britishness will emerge through social and geographical detail rather than a collection of quirky props. Relevant details will include the seafront café, unreliable buses, volunteer hierarchies, wet school shoes, local Facebook rumours, and the economic dependence of a fading resort on one profitable weekend. The blood motif will be used sparingly: - The opening emojis. - Festival stage blood on Jamie and Saf. - Jamie’s genuine cut. - Rust staining Nell’s hand beneath the pier. - A restrained final echo, if it feels earned. The story will avoid explaining every symbol. Red corrosion will not be repeatedly described as the town “bleeding,” and the pier will not become an overt metaphor for the friendship. ## Structure and Pacing Each chapter will cover one major movement and end on a natural shift in understanding rather than a sensational cliffhanger. With only around 1,000 words per chapter, the cast and locations will remain limited: 1. Nell’s home and the promenade. 2. The festival café and pier entrance. 3. Beneath the pier. 4. The closed arcade storeroom. 5. The café. 6. The pier office and entrance. 7. The dawn ceremony. 8. The immediate aftermath. The first four chapters will identify the sender and establish the danger. Chapters five and six will expose the characters’ lies and force a decision. Chapter seven will contain the attempted evacuation. Chapter eight will remain intimate, concentrating on what the night has changed rather than cataloguing every official consequence. ## Ending The ending will not provide total vindication. The pier will be closed pending inspection, but no inquiry results or resignation will be shown. Milo’s death will not be neatly reclassified before the story ends. Vale will remain capable of presenting the closure as routine caution. Nell will admit that she deleted Milo’s message and gave an incomplete account at the inquest. Saf will reveal the voice note she sent him. These admissions will not establish whether either action affected Milo’s decision. The uncertainty remains. Nell and Saf will not exchange matching therapeutic apologies or promise to become best friends again. They will choose one small, practical act of renewed contact. The final callback to the emojis will be understated: after they separate, Saf will send Nell one drop—the trio’s old signal for “I’m alive, stop fussing.” Nell will begin to type three drops, reconsider the melodrama, and send a plain “OK.” This reflects movement without converting the symbol into an overly neat emblem of healing. # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1: The Message At 00:08, Nell lies awake checking posts about the Night of the Revenant festival while pretending she is only charging her phone. She sees pictures of Saf volunteering at the seafront café and is annoyed by how normal Saf appears. A text arrives from Milo’s saved contact: **🩸🩸🩸** Nell explains only what is immediately necessary: three drops meant all three of them, now, no questions. They invented it years ago after an emergency that turned out to be Milo trapping his finger in a folding chair. Since his death, nobody has used it. She stares at the message long enough for the screen to dim. Her first response is anger rather than fear. She types “Not funny,” deletes it, then calls. Someone answers. Nell hears wind, breathing, and the repetitive electronic music used along the festival route. The call ends. A photograph follows: wet paving, the lower gate of the pier, and the toe of a blood-smeared trainer. There is no gothic caption, only: **come** Nell suspects Jamie but tells herself he would not do this. She also fears Saf may be injured. Without waking her mother, she puts on yesterday’s clothes, takes a torch and a can of deodorant, and walks to the seafront. Near the shuttered café, she sees Saf kneeling beside a streak of blood. Saf looks up and says, “Tell me that wasn’t you.” ## Chapter 2: Two of Three Nell and Saf establish that both received the same message. Their reunion is brittle. Nell notices Saf now carries a proper first-aid pouch and wears a festival volunteer tabard; Saf notices Nell has brought deodorant as a weapon. The blood is mostly sticky theatrical syrup from a damaged festival sachet, but there are several real drops beside it. A trail leads towards the locked lower access gate beneath the pier. Saf explains that her mother’s café is supplying the dawn ceremony. She stayed late to help with preparation, received the text, and found the blood. She has already called Jamie, who did not answer. Nell realises Saf has Jamie’s current number while she does not. They find Milo’s old phone lying just inside the gate, its screen cracked. Beside it is Jamie’s inhaler and a scrap of paper bearing a locker number from the amusement arcade’s staff storeroom. The objects are not arranged as a clever clue; they appear to have fallen from Jamie’s pocket. A festival security volunteer catches them at the gate. He is less sinister than inconvenienced. He says he chased a boy out of the restricted area earlier and assumed the blood was part of the event. He refuses to unlock the gate and tells them Councillor Vale does not want “another Grant incident” beneath the pier. Saf wants to retrieve the locker key she believes Jamie took from Milo’s room. Nell wants to call Jamie’s mother. Saf tells her that Jamie’s mother stopped accepting Nell’s calls months ago. They head towards the arcade because it is the only concrete lead and because neither wants to explain the message to a parent yet. ## Chapter 3: Underneath Before reaching the arcade, they hear coughing from beneath the pier. Saf finds a gap in the temporary fencing and goes through. Nell follows, partly because letting Saf go alone feels too much like repeating the past. They find Jamie crouched beside a support column. He has a cut above his eyebrow, smeared with exaggerated quantities of festival blood. He admits sending the text but becomes defensive when challenged. He wanted them both there, used the old code, and expected them to know it was him. He dropped Milo’s phone while hiding from security. Jamie has been trying to photograph a joint Milo marked in an old picture. The physical danger becomes tangible: bolts are deeply corroded, rust flakes onto Nell’s hand, and the walkway overhead shifts when a delivery trolley crosses the pier. Jamie shows them a creased photocopy of one survey page recommending capacity restrictions. He found it with Milo’s photographs. It lacks the report’s date, author, and conclusion, so it is alarming but not definitive. Saf cleans Jamie’s cut and tells him the fake blood was idiotic. Jamie replies that ordinary messages did not work. He accuses both girls of ignoring him since the funeral. A security torch approaches. The three leave through the gap and go to the arcade storeroom, where Jamie says Milo kept the rest of his photographs. ## Chapter 4: Milo’s Locker The arcade storeroom belongs to Saf’s uncle, who allows her access because she is collecting café supplies. This avoids an elaborate break-in while adding social risk: Saf must lie about why Nell and Jamie are with her. Milo’s old locker contains mostly rubbish—uniform shirts, dead batteries, receipts, a mouldy packet of sweets—and a damaged camera card taped inside a notebook. The notebook mixes observations about the pier with jokes, guesses, and plans for a dramatic video. The card yields only a handful of images on the storeroom computer. Several are blurred. One clearly shows severe corrosion. Another includes part of a council survey cover sheet with a date preceding Milo’s death. A final image shows that a warning sign was present beneath the pier on the afternoon he died, though it was gone by the time his body was found. There is no recording exonerating anyone and no direct evidence that Vale ordered concealment. Milo’s notes state that he intended to return “when it’s busy enough for them to care,” suggesting he wanted to film the structure moving during an event. Jamie assumes this proves Milo died exposing a cover-up. Saf says it proves Milo had another dangerous plan. Nell recognises one of Milo’s handwritten notes as a reference to the message he sent her that night. Before she can explain, Saf receives a volunteer alert: the sunrise ceremony will admit attendees in forty minutes. The old section of the pier is already being dressed for the crowd. ## Chapter 5: The Things They Said The three return to Saf’s mother’s café, where staff are preparing food while discussing the pier as if it were both a possible death trap and a scheduling problem. This grounds the economic cost: cancelling the ceremony would leave Saf’s family with stock they cannot afford to waste. Saf’s mother listens but says the photocopy and blurred pictures are not enough to accuse the council publicly. She agrees to contact the overnight council duty officer and keep Jamie inside. Jamie refuses, insisting adults have had eleven months. While waiting, Nell and Saf argue in the café’s cramped stockroom. Saf reveals that Milo told her he was going beneath the pier. She sent him a furious voice note saying, “Go on, then. Prove it.” She followed it with another telling him not to, but the second message remained unread. Nell admits Milo also texted her. He said he had found something and asked her to come. Nell replied that she was done cleaning up his disasters. After his death, she deleted the exchange and told the inquest she had not known his plans. Saf’s anger is not simply that Nell lied; it is that Nell remained the sensible, grieving friend while Saf became the town’s obvious bad influence. Nell cannot offer an adequate defence. The council duty officer calls back. She has located only a later summary describing the pier as suitable with “managed access.” She says she cannot close it on photographs alone but will send someone to inspect the affected section. Outside, a queue is already forming. ## Chapter 6: Managed Access Nell, Saf, and Jamie take the photographs to the pier office. Vale is there with volunteers and the duty officer. He recognises the preliminary survey page but argues that later advice superseded it. He does not deny the corrosion; he insists the ceremony can proceed if numbers are controlled. Vale’s position is made uncomfortable rather than absurd. Cancelling will cost local businesses money, and the town has already taken deposits it cannot repay. Saf’s mother, standing nearby, visibly feels the force of this. Nell asks why the warning sign in Milo’s photograph was removed. Nobody provides a clear answer. Vale says temporary signs are moved constantly. The duty officer asks for ten minutes to examine the lower support. Vale begins admitting attendees onto the pier in small groups. The decision is not presented as obviously insane, but Nell feels movement through the boards and sees a gap opening beside a corroded bracket. Jamie tries to run onto the pier to stop people himself. Saf catches him. In the struggle, Milo’s notebook falls open, and Nell sees that his notes overstate measurements and misidentify one support. His evidence is real but unreliable. Nell decides they cannot win by presenting Milo as an infallible investigator. She tells the duty officer and Vale that she lied at the inquest and will say so publicly, along with what Milo showed her before he died. She cannot prove a cover-up, but she can make it impossible for the council to claim nobody warned them. Vale asks whether she understands what that allegation will do to Milo’s family and to her own. Nell says she does, although she does not. ## Chapter 7: Sunrise Nell steps onto the low temporary platform used for announcements and takes the unattended microphone. She gives no polished speech. She says her name, says she lied about Milo’s final message, and says the old section of the pier may be unsafe. The result is confusion rather than instant belief. Some people leave. Others film her. Several tourists complain that they have paid for the sunrise ceremony. Vale tries to take the microphone without turning the interruption into a public confrontation. Saf makes the practical move: using her volunteer authority, she closes the inner access gate and redirects the queue towards the café, offering free tea that her mother has not authorised. Other volunteers follow because Saf gives them concrete instructions. Jamie shows Milo’s photographs to people waiting nearest the entrance. Saf’s mother sees the visible gap between boards shift when a supply trolley rolls past. She backs her daughter and tells Vale she will accept the financial loss. The duty officer returns from below and orders the old section cleared pending inspection. Vale calls it a precaution, avoiding any admission of wrongdoing. The evacuation remains untidy, full of arguments, phones, and people attempting to collect prepaid drinks. Once the pier is nearly empty, a railing near the ceremony platform tears loose under a volunteer’s hand. Two boards drop into the water. Nobody is hurt, but the sound silences the remaining objections. Nell looks for Saf, expecting relief or reconciliation. Saf is busy helping her mother carry trays of unsold food back into the café. ## Chapter 8: One Drop The chapter stays close to the immediate aftermath. The pier is closed. Police take names. The duty officer collects copies of the photographs and survey page but promises nothing beyond an inspection. Vale tells a reporter the closure demonstrates the council’s commitment to safety. Jamie’s mother arrives and is furious with him, then with Nell and Saf, then too exhausted to decide whom to blame. Jamie apologises for using Milo’s phone, but he also says it was the only thing that made them come. Nobody tells him he is entirely wrong. Nell gives a corrected statement about Milo’s final text. Doing so brings no cleansing sense of relief. It may reopen the inquest; it may merely change how people discuss the girls online. Saf submits both of her voice notes rather than playing only the second. Outside the café, Nell and Saf share a tray of food that can no longer be sold. Their conversation remains indirect. Nell says Saf seems different. Saf replies that Nell never asked. Nell attempts an apology, but Saf stops her from turning it into a speech. Saf does not invite Nell to resume their friendship. She asks whether Nell still has the same number. Nell says yes, though Saf has known this all night. They separate when Saf returns to help her family and Nell walks home along the wet promenade. The festival’s fake blood is being hosed from the pavement, leaving pink water around the drains. Nell’s phone vibrates. Saf has sent: **🩸** One drop—their old shorthand for “alive.” Nell types three drops, looks at them, and deletes them. She sends: **OK** Saf reads it. For the moment, that is all.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Nell Price Nell is sixteen and in Year 12 at St Mercy Sixth Form, studying English literature, history, and geography. She lives with her mother, Ruth, in a narrow terraced house ten minutes uphill from the seafront. Ruth manages bookings and payroll at the Miramar Hotel, despite the Miramar having neither a view of the sea nor anything resembling a mirror. Nell’s father moved to Bristol when she was ten and now conducts much of his parenting through forwarded articles and unusually large birthday bank transfers. Money is not constantly discussed in the Price household, but it shapes daily decisions. Ruth keeps the hotel’s complimentary tea bags in a kitchen jar and refuses to put the heating on before November. Nell is embarrassed by this mainly because she knows Saf notices and does not care. Nell was the academically reliable member of the former trio. Teachers trusted her with keys, registers, and explanations. If Nell said Milo and Saf had not meant to set off the science-block alarm, adults tended to believe her. She once enjoyed being the person who could translate their chaos into an acceptable account. Since Milo’s death, she has come to understand that credibility is a form of power, but she has used that power mostly to protect herself. She describes herself as honest because she dislikes direct lies. In practice, she edits. She omits. She answers the precise question asked. At Milo’s inquest, she said she had not known he would be beneath the pier. Milo had texted her that he had “found something properly rotten” and asked her to come. Nell deleted the exchange before the police examined her phone. She tells herself this did not alter the facts of his death. It only altered who had to answer for them. Her carefulness is genuine rather than wholly cowardly. Nell reads instructions before using things. She checks cinema fire exits. She photographs parking signs for her mother in case of appeals. She knows the last bus is scheduled for 23:12 and generally arrives at 23:26. She dislikes crossing roads away from pedestrian lights, although she will do it if someone is watching because she dislikes appearing frightened even more. Nell’s wit is observant, dry, and occasionally cruel. She notices when an adult substitutes a euphemism for an answer. She enjoys official language because it reveals what institutions want to avoid saying. She has a private collection of photographs of contradictory council signs: **PROMENADE OPEN AT ALL TIMES** beside **GATES LOCKED AT DUSK**, or **TEMPORARY CLOSURE** on a door that has been boarded up for six years.
