She once planned to make a social-media account for them but worried nobody would follow it. She dislikes broad emotional declarations and distrusts people who cry attractively. When distressed, she becomes practical in unhelpful ways. At Milo’s funeral, while everyone else was gathering outside, she reorganised a tray of triangular sandwiches according to filling. Saf saw her do it and understood; an aunt praised Nell for being “so composed.” Her small habits include: - Saving important contacts under full names, including people she has known since primary school. - Keeping receipts in her phone case until the ink wears off. - Biting the inside of her left cheek when she is concealing something. - Saying “Technically” just before making a morally dubious distinction. - Refusing to send voice notes because she hates the sound of herself inhaling. - Reading local Facebook comments despite claiming they are bad for her. - Sleeping with one foot outside the duvet. - Carrying a cheap black biro that frequently leaks onto her fingers. - Deleting messages during arguments, then remembering their wording almost exactly. - Correcting spelling in her head while someone is insulting her. Nell was once affectionate in an unobtrusive way. She brought people the food they had mentioned wanting and remembered which lies they had told their parents. Since Milo’s death, she has withdrawn from this role and allowed classmates to interpret her reserve as maturity. She has become closer to a polite sixth-form crowd who discuss universities and never ask why she and Saf no longer speak. Her major flaw is not fear but self-protective moral tidiness. She wants to be innocent more than she wants to be truthful. She resents Saf for making this difficult simply by existing as the person everyone finds easier to blame. She also has a buried, shameful anger at Milo. She misses him, but she is furious that his last act left her with a question she cannot answer and a version of herself she dislikes. She cannot say this aloud because dead boys are permitted flaws in anecdotes, not in grief. ## Physicality Nell is average height and narrow-shouldered, with pale skin that flushes visibly when she lies or becomes angry. Her straight brown hair reaches her jaw because she cut it herself after Milo’s funeral and has maintained it badly ever since. One side tends to tuck neatly behind her ear while the other swings into her face. She dresses to avoid attracting comment: dark jeans, school jumpers, plain trainers, a waterproof coat chosen by her mother. Her clothes are clean but rarely new. She wears no jewellery except a cheap digital watch she trusts more than her phone. Nell holds herself still around adults, making eye contact and keeping her hands quiet. Around Saf, old physical habits return without permission: leaning closer to hear her, turning her body towards her, passing objects without looking. When frightened, Nell presses her tongue against the back of her teeth and becomes unnaturally formal. When genuinely amused, she snorts once and immediately looks offended with herself. She interacts with unfamiliar spaces by locating exits and reading signs. She tests loose boards with the toe of her shoe. She avoids touching dirty surfaces but will forget this when concentrating, then become preoccupied with what is on her hand. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships At the beginning, Nell’s immediate motive is to establish who used Milo’s number and stop them from doing it again. Underneath this is a more urgent need: to find Saf before Saf can tell anyone about the message Milo sent Nell on the night he died. Nell’s first allegiance is habitually to her mother, though she often expresses it by concealing problems Ruth cannot afford to address. She loves Ruth and is embarrassed by how carefully Ruth manages money and reputation. Her relationship with Saf is built from years of intimacy buried beneath resentment. Nell knows Saf’s expressions, family routines, and evasions better than she knows those of her current friends. She still measures events against what Saf would say about them. She wants Saf’s forgiveness but also wants Saf to admit she was more responsible for Milo’s behaviour. These desires cannot coexist cleanly. Nell was drawn to Milo because he treated her caution as useful rather than boring. He made her an accomplice, not an audience. She also sometimes felt he used her as free crisis management. Her grief is tangled with relief that she no longer has to wait for his next emergency, which she considers unforgivable. She has kept her distance from Jamie because he resembles Milo most when angry. She tells herself his family needed space. In truth, she could not tolerate the possibility that he might ask what she knew. By the climax, her motive shifts from proving herself uninvolved to making a truthful warning, even though the truth does not guarantee that anyone will forgive or believe her. ## Voice Experiments > The council called the pier “subject to ongoing maintenance,” which was the same phrase Mum used for the shower when it had been growing mushrooms for six weeks. > I had not lied at the inquest. Not exactly. They had asked whether I knew where Milo was going, and knowing is a strong word. Knowing implies certainty. I had possessed information and behaved as if I hadn’t, which was apparently different enough to let me sleep for nearly a year. > “That is not blood,” I said. > Saf looked down at the red coating her fingers. “Brilliant. Tell Jamie’s forehead.” > If Milo had come back from the dead, I was going to kill him. This was not logical, but neither was Milo, historically. # Safiya “Saf” Hussain Saf is sixteen, in the same sixth form as Nell, and takes biology, psychology, and art. She lives above the Mercy Café with her mother, Farah, her maternal grandmother, Nani Salma, and her eight-year-old brother, Sami. Her father, Imran, lives in Southampton and visits irregularly but warmly; he and Farah are separated without turning this into a public feud, which disappoints the town’s appetite for explanation. The café has belonged to Farah’s family for twenty-two years. It serves fried breakfasts, tea, chips, jacket potatoes, and a recent range of expensive coffees introduced because tourists kept asking. Saf has worked there since she was tall enough to reach the card machine. She can carry four mugs in one hand, identify regular customers by how they knock on the window, and calculate whether a complaint is worth a free slice of cake. The Hussains are not destitute, but a bad winter matters. The Night of the Revenant weekend brings in roughly what the café makes during several ordinary November weeks. Saf understands this in specific terms: the broken freezer, the supplier’s overdue invoice, Sami’s new school shoes. Stopping the festival is not an abstract sacrifice. Before Milo’s death, Saf was the trio’s obvious troublemaker. She climbed fences first, challenged teachers openly, and once replaced the school’s motivational posters with notices reading **YOUR BEST MAY BE STATISTICALLY MEDIOCRE**. Nell translated her provocations into jokes adults could tolerate; Milo escalated them. Since the accident, Saf has changed in ways Nell has not noticed. She completed a first-aid course, joined the festival volunteer team, and now checks doors behind her. She is still confrontational, but less impulsive. She hates being called reckless because it freezes her at fifteen, on the worst night of her life. Saf’s anger is quick, but not simple. She often uses it to avoid showing fear or requesting tenderness. If someone disappoints her, she prefers to act as though she has judged them contemptible rather than admit they have hurt her. She blocked Nell after seeing Nell leave the inquest with a teacher’s arm around her while reporters photographed Saf’s family arguing with the coroner’s officer. Nell never asked why. Saf is socially confident but privately superstitious. She claims not to believe in ghosts, then refuses to whistle beneath the pier because Nani says it calls things that drown. She says this is cultural respect, not fear. She does not step on memorial plaques and becomes genuinely angry when tourists sit on Milo’s bench. She is visually minded. She sketches people on till receipts and remembers colour more reliably than wording. Her A-level portfolio consists largely of empty seaside interiors: laundrettes, amusement arcades before opening, the café kitchen after midnight. Her teachers praise their “liminality,” a word Saf uses only sarcastically. Her minor habits include: - Carrying plasters, antiseptic wipes, safety pins, painkillers, and a tampon in a mint tin. - Testing tea by smell rather than tasting it. - Drawing eyes in the margins of notes and then blacking them out. - Leaving voice notes instead of typing, even when the message could be one word. - Using someone’s full name when she is about to be particularly rude. - Pocketing loose screws and washers she finds on the floor. - Eating chips with far too much vinegar. - Cutting labels from clothes because they “feel judgemental.” - Never sitting with her back to the café door. - Keeping her phone screen cracked because replacing it feels like rewarding the phone. - Calling Nell “Price” when angry and “Nell” only when she forgets to maintain distance. Saf is good in practical emergencies. She becomes quieter, gives short instructions, and deals with what is in front of her. Afterwards, she shakes and complains about something unrelated. She is less good with prolonged uncertainty, which makes grief particularly intolerable. Her major flaw is that she knows how to wound people accurately. When frightened or cornered, she identifies the statement the other person least wants to hear and says it. She later regrets the damage but resents having to apologise for something she still partly believes. On the night Milo died, she sent him a voice note saying, “Go on, then. Prove it.” It followed an argument in which he accused her of becoming boring and scared. Less than a minute later, she sent another message telling him not to go and tried to call. The retraction remained unread. Saf does not know which message he heard, and this uncertainty has become the central fact of her private life. ## Physicality Saf is slightly shorter than Nell, broad-shouldered, strong through the arms from café work, and difficult to overlook. She has warm brown skin, dark eyes, and thick black hair usually tied in a low, untidy plait. She sometimes wears a hijab for family or religious occasions but does not wear one daily; she dislikes strangers treating this as an invitation to assess her faith. She dresses in practical layers: cargo trousers, old trainers, oversized sweatshirts, and the café apron or festival tabard. Her fingers are often marked with pen, charcoal, coffee, or food colouring. She has a faint pale scar across one knuckle from punching a glass-fronted fire alarm at school during an actual fire. Saf occupies space directly. She leans on counters, sits backwards on chairs, and steps closer when arguing. Around adults she mistrusts, she crosses her arms and smiles without warmth. Around children or injured people, her movements become careful and economical. Her face is expressive despite her efforts. One eyebrow rises before she challenges someone. She chews the side of her thumb when afraid. When she is trying not to cry, she looks furious and speaks more quietly. Saf touches people more readily than Nell does, but after the estrangement she becomes conspicuously careful not to touch Nell. This makes the first accidental moment of old familiarity—a hand at Nell’s elbow, perhaps—more charged than an explicit declaration. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships Saf’s strongest allegiance is to her family and the café. She knows Farah is under financial strain and wants the festival to succeed. At the same time, she cannot tolerate another person being injured because local adults decided money justified delay. Her immediate motive is to find Jamie and prevent him from repeating Milo’s mistake. She feels protective of him but also angry that he has inherited Milo’s talent for forcing other people into crises. Saf’s relationship with Nell remains emotionally central despite nearly a year of silence. She believes Nell abandoned her when the town needed a person to blame. She also misses Nell’s steadiness, memory, and ability to understand what Saf means before Saf says it. She does not trust Nell’s apologies because Nell is skilled at producing the version of events adults reward. Saf loved Milo without romanticising him. He made her laugh, competed with her, and often treated her boundaries as challenges. She is furious with him for dying after she told him to prove himself. Her family is one of the few places where this anger has been allowed. Saf likes Jamie but refuses to indulge him. She recognises that grief has made adults permissive with him and suspects he is beginning to confuse being hurt with being entitled. Saf’s eventual choice to stop access to the pier threatens her family’s income and her mother’s trust. Her loyalty is not simply transferred from family to truth; she tries to protect both and discovers she cannot. ## Voice Experiments > “I’m not reckless. I’m standing in a volunteer tabard with a first-aid kit while you’ve brought aerosolised deodorant to a possible murder.” > Nell always went quiet before she said something unforgivable. It was how you knew she had checked the grammar. > “He wasn’t some tiny doomed prince, Nell. He was a pain in the arse who thought consequences were what happened to other people.” > “I did tell him not to go.” Saf rubbed her thumb against the edge of the table. “Just not first.” # Jamie Grant Jamie is fourteen, in Year 10, and lives with his mother, Helen, in a council maisonette several streets inland. His father left when Jamie was six and sends Christmas messages with the wrong age in them. Milo, five years older, occupied an unstable mixture of brother, babysitter, bully, hero, and substitute parent. Since Milo’s death, adults have handled Jamie either too carefully or not carefully enough. Teachers allow missed homework if he looks tired. Neighbours ask how his mother is while he is standing in front of them. People lower their voices when saying Milo’s name, then post photographs of the pier online. Jamie has become skilled at appearing unaffected because visible grief causes adults to speak to him as though he is six. He is bright but uneven at school. He enjoys design technology and physics when they involve objects, but resents showing his calculations. He has a talent for repairing old electronics and an impatience that ruins half his repairs. Milo’s phone works because Jamie replaced its charging port with a part ordered online and held the casing together with black electrical tape. Jamie learned the three-drop code by watching the older trio use it. They never formally included him. Once, when he sent one drop to Milo after falling off his bike, Milo replied with a photograph of a plaster and the words **apply this emotionally**. Jamie remembers laughing. He also remembers that Milo did not come to collect him. His decision to send the opening text is impulsive. He has tried to contact Saf twice and Nell once about the material he found. Saf told him not to go beneath the pier and promised to look later. Nell left his message unread for three days. Jamie interprets both responses as proof that they prefer grieving Milo to dealing with anything he left behind. He takes Milo’s phone, types the code, and sends it before deciding what he wants Nell and Saf to do. When they respond, he panics. He smears theatrical blood over his genuine cut because he wants the situation to look as urgent as it feels. He is immediately ashamed of this but continues lying because admitting the first lie would make everything else easier to dismiss. Jamie shares Milo’s appetite for drama but not his social confidence. Milo enjoyed being watched; Jamie becomes awkward and hostile under attention. He often performs indifference, then checks obsessively whether anyone reacted. His habits include: - Taking broken disposable vapes apart for their batteries. - Wearing one earbud with nothing playing so adults hesitate to talk to him. - Cracking his knuckles before telling a lie. - Keeping loose screws in an old mint tin, unaware Saf does the same. - Photographing things instead of writing notes, then never labelling the photographs. - Refusing to tie his shoelaces properly. - Using full stops in texts when angry because Milo said they looked threatening. - Carrying an inhaler but pretending he rarely needs it. - Drinking tea with five sugars when his mother is not watching. - Saying “Whatever” when he means several distinct things. - Correcting people who say Milo “passed away”: Milo died, and Jamie sees euphemism as theft. - Sleeping in Milo’s old hoodie but never wearing it outside. His major flaw is emotional coercion. Jamie believes that if ordinary requests are ignored, he is justified in creating an emergency. He has learned this partly from Milo and partly from the way adults only became attentive after catastrophe. He does not yet understand that being dismissed does not make every escalation fair. He is also possessive of Milo’s memory. He resents Nell and Saf for having private stories about his brother, yet hoards material they might need. He sometimes treats biological kinship as giving him the final word on who Milo was. At other times, he is painfully aware that Nell and Saf knew parts of Milo that he never did. ## Physicality Jamie is small for fourteen and reacts badly to anyone mentioning it. He has Milo’s dark blond hair but keeps it too long over his forehead, partly because Helen once said the resemblance was strongest around the eyes. He is pale, freckled, and usually looks as though he slept in his clothes. He wears battered trainers, black jeans, and oversized hoodies inherited from Milo. His asthma makes him careful about showing breathlessness; he will stop and pretend to inspect something rather than use his inhaler in front of other teenagers. Jamie’s body language alternates between sprawl and retreat. He occupies chairs carelessly when he feels safe, but folds inward when confronted. He avoids eye contact while telling the truth and stares directly at people when lying. His anger reddens his ears before it reaches his face. He picks at scabs and electrical tape. Around Nell and Saf, he imitates Milo’s crooked smile without knowing he is doing it. The resemblance unsettles both girls, which Jamie notices and sometimes exploits. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships Jamie wants acknowledgment more than justice in the abstract. He wants somebody to confirm that Milo saw a real danger and was not merely a stupid boy trespassing for attention. He wants Nell and Saf to explain what happened, though he suspects any answer will hurt. His strongest allegiance is to his mother, but he conceals his actions because he believes she cannot survive another crisis. This belief is both loving and patronising. Helen would rather be frightened than excluded. Jamie idealises Milo and resents him. He misses being tormented, borrowed from, and occasionally chosen. Finding Milo’s notes gives him a task that feels like continued contact. He sees Saf as the more honest of the girls because she is visibly angry and has remained in sporadic contact. He sees Nell as someone adults protect. At the same time, he contacted Nell because Milo trusted her with practical problems. Jamie’s hostility towards her contains disappointed expectation. His alliance with the girls is unstable. He needs their knowledge but does not want them to control Milo’s story. By the end, he can admit that using the phone was cruel, but he still believes the message achieved what ordinary requests could not. ## Voice Experiments > “I did message you normally. You did what everyone does with normal. You left it there until it stopped being your problem.” > Milo’s phone had been dead for eleven months. Getting it on again took two replacement parts, three tutorials and Mum leaving for work. Jamie tried not to think of this as resurrection. Resurrection probably had fewer adverts. > “It’s not fake blood. There is real blood under it.” > “That does not improve it,” Saf said. > “It improves the accuracy.” > “He died. You can say it. It doesn’t make him do it again.” # Milo Grant Milo died at seventeen, eleven months before the story begins. He had left sixth form during his first year and worked irregular shifts at the amusement arcade, café kitchens, and a bicycle hire kiosk. He told people this was temporary while cycling through possible futures: documentary filmmaker, marine engineer, urban explorer, stand-up comedian, electrician, and owner of a bar that would not admit anyone over thirty. He was not secretly destined for greatness. He was clever, charismatic, and unreliable. He had an intuitive understanding of people’s attention and almost no understanding of their limits. He could make a dull afternoon feel like the opening of a criminal enterprise. He could also make his friends responsible for finishing whatever he began. Milo invented private games and codes constantly. The three blood drops began after he trapped his finger in a folding chair at a youth-club first-aid course and sent Nell and Saf an image of the injury. Nell said one drop meant “mild concern,” two meant “ambulance,” and three meant “you are already dead.” Milo declared three the highest emergency level. The code endured because it was stupid, not because it was solemn. He liked filming neglected parts of St Mercy: closed hotels, storm drains, boarded kiosks, and the underside of the pier. His interest combined genuine curiosity with an attraction to trespassing. He wanted the town to admit what it allowed to rot. He also wanted strangers online to think he was fearless. Milo noticed the pier’s corrosion while retrieving a dropped camera from a maintenance ledge. He later found pages of a preliminary survey in an unlocked office bin and convinced himself he had uncovered deliberate corruption. He was partly right. The danger had been minimised and responsibility dispersed. However, he exaggerated measurements, misread technical terms, and planned to obtain dramatic footage during a busy event because static photographs seemed insufficient. On the night he died, he contacted both Nell and Saf. He gave each a different version of his plan, knowing which argument would provoke which response. He wanted Saf to dare him and Nell to follow him. Neither did. His final actions cannot be assigned to one message or person. Milo’s flaws include: - Treating “no” as the start of a negotiation. - Assuming charm constituted an apology. - Inventing details to improve stories, then half-believing them. - Borrowing money in amounts too small to confront him over. - Making serious discoveries sound like jokes until nobody knew when to worry. - Needing an audience. - Testing affection by creating situations in which people had to come and get him. - Being kinder to strangers than to people whose loyalty he trusted. - Leaving projects nearly finished so someone else would rescue them. His kindness was real but inconsistent. He spent an afternoon repairing Nani Salma’s radio and then forgot Jamie’s school play. He knew when Nell was overwhelmed and could quietly redirect attention away from her. He also used her competence without asking. He defended Saf from teachers, then accused her of cowardice when she refused a dangerous plan. Trivial details kept in others’ memories include: - He hated bananas but liked banana milkshake. - He could not wink and instead closed both eyes. - He stole hotel pencils. - He claimed never to be cold and constantly borrowed coats. - He knew every free public toilet in St Mercy. - He sang advertising jingles while concentrating. - He always read the final page of a book first. - He took photographs with his thumb over the lens. - He wrote important information on his hands, then washed it off. - He called the sea “the Channel” only around tourists. - He owed Nell £6.40 and Saf one replacement mug. ## Physicality Milo was tall and still growing into himself, with long limbs, freckled skin, dark blond hair, and a smile that usually arrived before he had decided whether a situation was funny. He moved quickly and carelessly, stepping onto walls or kerbs when flat ground was available. He touched objects constantly: testing doors, pressing buttons, picking at paint, spinning coins. In photographs, he leaned into other people’s space or appeared blurred at the edge. When lying, he rubbed the side of his nose. Nell knew this; Saf claimed he did it whether lying or not. His physical confidence was partly performance. He disliked heights when standing still but managed them by continuing to move. He had once admitted this to Nell, then denied the conversation later. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships Before his death, Milo wanted to prove the town’s adults had concealed danger and to prove he himself was not merely an unemployed nuisance. Those desires became indistinguishable. He cared about public safety, but he also wanted the discovery to belong to him. He depended on Nell to make his plans workable and on Saf to make them exciting. He resented both when they tried to withdraw from those roles. His allegiance to the trio was genuine, but he often assumed it guaranteed their availability. Milo loved Jamie but treated him as a junior audience rather than an equal. He protected Jamie from their father’s unreliability while repeating smaller versions of it himself. Because Milo exists through fragments, no single account of him will be definitive. Nell remembers the burden; Saf remembers the provocation; Jamie remembers the hero. The truth lies uneasily among all three. ## Voice Experiments > “Three drops means emergency.” > “You trapped your finger in a chair,” Nell said. > “The chair attacked first.” > “It’s not trespassing if the fence is already broken. That is the infrastructure extending an invitation.” > On the damaged video, Milo’s face filled the frame for half a second. “If this is blurry, it’s atmospheric. If it’s completely black, Nell touched the settings.” > **Found something properly rotten. Not metaphorically for once. Come down.** # Farah Hussain Farah is forty-two and has run the Mercy Café since her father’s health declined eight years ago. She is practical, sociable, and less emotionally transparent than Saf believes. She can welcome a difficult customer while calculating how quickly they can be persuaded to leave. She was born in Portsmouth and moved to St Mercy at twelve. She knows the town’s families across several generations and distrusts people who arrive with schemes for “revitalising the seafront.” She also knows the café needs those schemes to attract customers. Her relationship with local tourism is therefore neither cynical nor sentimental: she needs people to come, spend money, complain about the weather, and leave. Farah has spent the year since Milo’s death protecting Saf from gossip without encouraging Saf’s version of events. She knows about the angry voice note but not the deleted retraction. She believes Saf was not responsible for Milo’s choices, yet worries Saf’s anger makes her vulnerable to being represented as responsible. Her kindness tends to be logistical. She sends food home with people, remembers allergies, and knows who cannot pay until Friday. She is less comfortable with direct consolation. When Saf cries, Farah offers tasks before hugs. Saf sometimes interprets this as indifference, though it is how Farah keeps from becoming overwhelmed herself. Farah’s minor traits include: - Distrusting contactless payments while using them hundreds of times a day. - Calling every streaming service “Netflix.” - Keeping a pencil behind her ear and searching for it. - Refusing to waste food even when it is burnt. - Memorising customers’ orders but forgetting their names. - Reheating the same cup of tea until it is undrinkable. - Writing supplier calculations on paper napkins. - Using “my love” both affectionately and as a warning. - Treating chairs scraped across the café floor as a personal insult. Her flaw is that she often converts moral problems into practical calculations because those are easier to survive. When told the pier may be unsafe, her first thoughts are about proof, lost income, and unsold stock. This is not greed; it is pressure. However, Saf experiences the hesitation as another adult deciding that danger can wait. ## Physicality Farah is compact, quick-moving, and usually dressed in black trousers, trainers, and a café polo shirt beneath a cardigan. Her dark hair is cut to her chin and increasingly threaded with grey, which she dyes irregularly. She rarely stops moving during work. She talks while wiping tables, counts money while listening, and expresses anger through increasingly precise arrangements of objects. When truly frightened, she becomes still. Farah’s face is open with customers and guarded with officials. She has a broad, convincing service smile that Saf can distinguish instantly from a real one. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships Farah’s primary allegiance is to her family, expressed through the café that supports them. She wants Saf safe, Jamie returned to his mother, and the festival weekend to proceed. The discovery that these aims conflict forces her to choose without certainty. She likes Nell but has not forgiven her silence after Milo’s death. She suspects Nell’s composure concealed something, though she does not know what. She will not attack a sixteen-year-old for this, which makes her restrained disappointment harder for Nell to face. Farah cared for Milo while distrusting him. She fed him, employed him briefly, and banned him from repairing café equipment after he made the toaster eject bread onto the floor. She saw how he could lead Saf into trouble, but also how willingly Saf followed. Her eventual decision to back the closure matters because it costs her directly. She does not become an unquestioning ally; she sees the boards move and chooses the immediate safety of strangers over money her family genuinely needs. ## Voice Experiments > “You have brought me one photocopy, three wet teenagers and a possible structural emergency at half one in the morning. Let me put the kettle on before anyone develops a fourth problem.” > “People say money isn’t everything when they mean it isn’t currently their thing.” > “Safiya, I believe you. Believing you and closing a pier are, inconveniently, different activities.” > “Free tea?” Farah repeated. > Saf looked at the queue. “Emergency measure.” > “Then they can have emergency-sized cups.” # Councillor Crispin Vale Crispin Vale is fifty-four, a local councillor and chair of the St Mercy Tourism Partnership. He grew up in the town, left for university, worked in regional marketing, and returned after inheriting his mother’s house. He considers himself one of the few people willing to do the unglamorous work of keeping St Mercy economically alive. He is not wealthy, aristocratic, or secretly criminal. “Crispin” sounds grander than he is; his father sold carpets. He has spent years being mocked for earnest regeneration campaigns and has developed a thick professional skin over a thin personal one. Vale helped create the Night of the Revenant festival from a local legend about a drowned smuggler. The legend is mostly invented, which Vale regards as normal branding rather than dishonesty. Festival income has kept several businesses open through winter. He can name those businesses and the families behind them. He received a summary of the pier survey describing the old section as usable with restricted access and continued monitoring. He knew an earlier draft was more severe but did not read it closely. He told himself engineers disagreed routinely and that closure would cause damage more certain than the risk. His culpability lies in cultivated ignorance. After Milo’s death, Vale publicly called it a tragic trespassing accident. He genuinely believed this was likely. He also knew that asking whether the pier contributed would threaten funding, insurance, and his own reputation. He allowed the simpler version to harden. Vale’s habits include: - Saying “Let’s be proportionate” when he means “Do not make this larger.” - Remembering business owners’ names and forgetting their children’s. - Wearing festival merchandise over formal clothes. - Replying to emails at 5 a.m. to demonstrate commitment. - Straightening signs while speaking. - Disliking being recorded and becoming more formal when a phone appears. - Carrying mints in a rattling metal tin. - Referring to social-media criticism as “noise.” - Keeping old local newspaper clippings about successful events. - Using first names aggressively during conflict. His major flaw is that he confuses being burdened with being right. Because he knows the town’s financial pressures, he believes frightened teenagers cannot understand the consequences of cancellation. He does not think of himself as choosing money over safety; he thinks he is balancing competing harms. This self-conception allows him to dismiss evidence that would make balance impossible. He is not wholly unsympathetic towards Milo’s family. He helped arrange the memorial bench and secured a small hardship payment for Helen. Part of his defensiveness comes from believing these acts demonstrate that he behaved decently. ## Physicality Vale is tall, slightly stooped, and heavier around the middle than his carefully fitted jackets allow. He has thinning silver hair and a ruddy face that looks jovial in photographs and strained at close range. He moves with practised public confidence, offering handshakes and lowering his voice to create an impression of seriousness. When challenged, he smooths his tie or straightens nearby objects. His expressions are controlled until someone questions his motives; then his mouth tightens before his language does. During the festival, he wears a black raincoat over a burgundy waistcoat chosen to suit the Revenant branding. The outfit is faintly ridiculous but not full costume. He removed the plastic fangs after the first year because he could not speak clearly in them. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships Vale’s allegiance is to St Mercy as an institution, which means he often sacrifices individual voices to the version of the town he believes can survive. He wants the festival to proceed safely enough, preserve confidence, and avoid another public association between the pier and Milo’s death. He views Nell as credible until she admits lying. He sees Saf as intelligent but confrontational and believes her family’s dependence on the festival will eventually make her reasonable. He treats Jamie as a grieving child whose claims are best managed rather than answered. Vale does not want anyone hurt. Once the duty officer confirms movement in the structure, he accepts closure. His failure is that he requires official confirmation after receiving sufficient reasons to stop. He will not be defeated or exposed in a dramatic sense. By the end, he has already reframed events as evidence that council safeguards worked. Whether this is conscious deceit or professional instinct should remain uncertain. ## Voice Experiments > “Nobody is dismissing you, Nell. I am asking you to distinguish between a serious concern and a proven immediate danger.” > “If I close the pier every time somebody photographs rust, we may as well tow the town into the Channel.” > He did not say the festival mattered more than a person. He said six businesses had taken deposits, three hundred advance tickets had been sold, and panic had consequences. Adults liked to put numbers around a choice until the choice disappeared. > “We are not concealing anything,” Vale said. “We are managing information responsibly.” > Saf glanced at Nell. “You two should start a club.”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter One ## The Message The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸 It appeared under Milo Grant’s name. For a few seconds, I watched the screen as if one of us might become embarrassed and admit we had made a mistake. The time changed from 00:08 to 00:09. Milo remained dead. Outside, the wind pushed rain against my window in handfuls. A car hissed along Mercy Road, heading towards the seafront, and somebody shouted something cheerful and unintelligible. The Night of the Revenant festival had officially begun eight minutes ago. Apparently this required drunken screaming. I had not been looking at festival posts. I had been checking the time. The fact that Saf Hussain featured in several of the posts was coincidental. In the latest picture she stood outside the Mercy Café wearing a burgundy volunteer tabard over a black sweatshirt. Her hair was in a plait, and she held a tray of paper cups while a plastic skeleton leaned against her shoulder with more familiarity than seemed appropriate. Saf was looking away from the camera, laughing at someone beyond the frame. She looked normal. I had spent eleven months trying to look normal, and it was irritating to discover she could do it accidentally. My phone buzzed again, although no second message appeared. Just the first one sitting there. Three blood drops had once meant all three of us, now, no questions. We had invented the code at thirteen, after Milo trapped his finger in a folding chair during a youth-club first-aid course. He sent a photograph of the injury to Saf and me from three metres away. One drop, I told him, meant mild concern. Two meant an ambulance. Three meant he was already dead and we could stop rushing. Milo immediately decided three drops meant a maximum emergency. It was typical of him to choose the only option that removed any obligation to help. After that we used it for actual emergencies, or what passed for them. Saf stranded on the last bus without money. Me locked in a hotel laundry cupboard. Milo seeing his ex-girlfriend while wearing the jumper he had stolen from her brother. Nobody had used it since he died. I pressed my thumb to his name and opened the contact information. The number was the same. I knew it was the same because I had never deleted it and had looked often enough to recognise the final digits. I had also never changed the photograph: Milo at fourteen, holding a crab close to the camera while Saf pretended to eat it behind him. His phone had not been in the bag the police returned to his mum. I remembered Helen Grant saying so in our kitchen. She had been staring into a mug of tea Mum had made and forgotten to drink. The sea took things, people kept saying. People enjoyed giving the sea agency. It saved everyone else paperwork. I typed: **Who is this?** That looked frightened. I replaced it with: **Not funny.** That looked more frightened. I deleted the whole thing and pressed call. The phone rang once. Someone answered. I sat up so quickly the charging cable came out of the wall. There was no voice. Only wind, close breathing and a thin, repetitive tune in the background. Four electronic notes, then four more. “Hello?” The breathing stopped. “Milo?” I did not mean to say it. The name came out small and automatic, like a word spoken while falling. The call ended. For a moment I could hear nothing except the rain and the Miramar Hotel clock in Mum’s room chiming twelve minutes late. Then I recognised the tune. The council had installed speakers along the festival route, playing the same thirty-second piece of “atmospheric music” between announcements. Saf had sent a recording of it to our old group chat last year with the caption **local man discovers keyboard’s minor setting**. The old group chat was still on my phone. I never opened it. A picture arrived. Wet paving filled most of the frame. At the top was the lower gate beneath St Mercy Pier, its bars furred orange with rust. At the bottom, partly cut off, was the toe of a white trainer smeared dark red. Underneath, one word: **come** My scalp prickled. Jamie, I thought. It had to be Jamie. Milo’s little brother knew the code. He had spent years hovering outside bedrooms and behind café counters while we discussed things he was supposedly too young to hear. I searched my messages for him. The latest was from nine days ago. **found some of milos pier stuff. do you want it** I had read it three days later, during history, and then the lesson had started. Afterwards, replying felt rude because of the delay. After another day, it felt worse. Eventually I had decided no answer was kinder than an obviously guilty one. This was a system I used often. It had not yet made anybody feel better. I called Jamie’s number. It went straight to voicemail. Then I called Saf. Her name vanished before the first ring and was replaced by **Call Failed**. I tried again. The same thing happened. Blocked. Fair enough, technically. Though blocking somebody for eleven months and then allowing yourself to appear in public photographs looking cheerful seemed unnecessarily aggressive. I enlarged the picture. The trainer might have belonged to Jamie. It might have belonged to anybody. The red could have been festival blood; cardboard boxes of it had been stacked outside the café all week. It could also have been actual blood, which was the difficulty with blood-themed community events. I could wake Mum. Mum would call the police. The police would ask why I thought the photograph showed an emergency. I would explain that a dead boy’s phone had sent me emojis based on a folding-chair incident from 2022. We would all enjoy that. I pulled on yesterday’s jeans and a sweatshirt. My waterproof was hanging over the banister, which meant getting it without waking Mum. I put my cheap torch in one pocket and, after considering the possibility of being murdered beneath the pier, took a can of deodorant from my desk. There was probably no official guidance recommending deodorant for self-defence. There was official guidance recommending people not go alone to closed piers after midnight, but we had moved beyond guidance. Downstairs, Mum’s bedroom door stayed shut. I eased the front latch up, caught it before it clicked, and stepped into the rain. St Mercy sloped towards the sea, so every gutter was overflowing in the same direction. Festival bunting snapped above the road. Red plastic drops, meant to resemble blood, slapped one another in the wind. The promenade was brighter than usual but nearly empty. Music leaked from the speakers. Four notes, then four more. Outside the shuttered Mercy Café, someone was kneeling on the pavement. Saf wore the burgundy tabard from the photograph. One knee of her cargo trousers was soaked. In front of her, a dark red streak ran towards the pier. She looked up. “Tell me,” she said, “that wasn’t you.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Character** The first-person narration from Nell is immediately distinctive and consistent with her profile. The voice is dry, observant, and self-aware without being performatively quirky. Lines like "I had not been looking at festival posts. I had been checking the time" establish her self-deception economically. The detail about reorganising sandwiches at funerals isn't here, but the same psychological texture is present throughout: Nell managing appearances, editing her own behaviour, noticing small hypocrisies. The humour lands consistently. "Milo remained dead" is a perfect beat — flat, dark, and character-revealing. The deodorant-as-weapon gag is set up and paid off without being laboured. The observation about the sea being given "agency" to "save everyone else paperwork" is genuinely sharp and fits Nell's voice precisely. The internal logic of the three-drop code is explained naturally through memory rather than exposition dump. The folding-chair origin story is both funny and characterising of Milo. **Dialogue** There is very little dialogue, but what exists is excellent. Saf's closing line — "Tell me that wasn't you" — is perfectly calibrated: it establishes the dynamic, the suspicion, the brittleness of their reunion, and the dark comedy of the situation in six words. The brief phone exchange ("Hello?" / "Milo?") is handled with restraint; the narrator's acknowledgment that saying Milo's name was involuntary is honest and affecting. **Plot and Structure** The chapter follows its plan faithfully. Nell receives the message, investigates, calls, receives the photograph, and arrives at the pier to find Saf. The pacing is well-managed — the chapter doesn't rush to the reunion but earns it. The detail about Jamie's unanswered message nine days prior is planted cleanly and reveals Nell's character flaw (the "system" of non-response) without authorial underlining. **Emotional Engagement** The chapter is emotionally effective without being sentimental. The moment Nell says "Milo?" into the phone is genuinely affecting — the narrator's own commentary ("I did not mean to say it. The name came out small and automatic, like a word spoken while falling") is one of the few more poetic moments, and it earns its place. The grief is present but not performed. **Potential Weaknesses** The "like a word spoken while falling" simile is the one moment that edges toward the slightly overwrought, though it's defensible. The chapter is perhaps slightly long for what it accomplishes — some of the middle section (the deliberation about calling Mum, the deodorant) could be tighter — but none of it is wasted, as it all characterises Nell. The festival atmosphere is present but not overdone. The "four notes, then four more" motif is used twice, which is exactly right. **Consistency with Plan and Profile** The chapter follows the plan almost exactly: the timing, the message, the call, the photograph, the deodorant, the arrival at the pier, Saf's line. Nell's character is rendered faithfully — her self-protective editing, her dry wit, her physical carefulness, her complicated feelings about Saf. The Milo contact photograph detail (him at fourteen with the crab) is a lovely addition not in the plan but entirely consistent with the character profiles. **Overall Assessment** This is a confident, well-executed opening chapter. The voice is distinctive and consistent, the dark comedy works, the emotional undercurrent is present without being forced, and the chapter accomplishes its structural goals efficiently. It avoids the trap of YA snark-overload while maintaining genuine wit. The prose is clean and specific. Minor quibbles about one slightly strained simile and marginal pacing don't significantly diminish what is a genuinely accomplished piece of writing. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Two ## Two of Three Saf stood. She had a first-aid pouch clipped to her waist. An actual one, with a white cross and compartments. I became aware of the deodorant in my pocket. “Was what me?” I said. “The text.” “No.” “Good start. Very convincing.” She picked up her phone from the café windowsill and showed me the screen. Milo Grant: 🩸🩸🩸 Underneath was the same photograph of the gate, except Saf’s message said **outside** rather than **come**. “He used a capital letter for you,” I said. Saf stared at me. “Milo’s phone texts us from beyond the grave, and you’re annoyed the ghost respects me more?” “I’m not annoyed.” “You came out in the rain to correct punctuation?” “I came because I thought you might be hurt.” The words landed between us with more force than intended. Saf looked down at the red streak. “Well. Sorry to disappoint.” Close up, she did look different from her festival photograph. There were grey hollows beneath her eyes and a wet curl stuck to her cheek. Her tabard said **ASK ME ABOUT THE REVENANT**, which was unfortunate in the circumstances. I crouched beside the streak. It shone under the café light, too bright and gelatinous to be real. “Stage blood,” I said. “I know.” “You touched it?” “I smelled it.” “That seems less sensible.” “It’s sugar syrup and food colouring.” “You can identify blood by smell now?” “I work in catering.” Saf rubbed her fingers together. The red stretched between them in a sticky string. “One of the sachets has burst. They’re using them in the procession tomorrow. Today.” A cardboard box sat by the café door, one bottom corner sagging. Several plastic sachets inside had split. Red syrup had leaked across the paving. I let out a breath. “Except those,” Saf said. Past the glossy spill, three smaller drops darkened the concrete. They were thinner, almost brown at the edges. “Those are real.” “How do you know?” She gave me a look. “Right,” I said. “Catering.” “First aid.” “I was going to say that next.” Saf unzipped the pouch at her waist and took out gloves. She put them on with quick, practised snaps. “When did you start carrying that?” “When did you start bringing Impulse to knife fights?” My hand closed around the deodorant through my pocket. “It’s Sure.” “Oh, good. Clinical grade.” She followed the drops towards the lower entrance to the pier. Temporary metal barriers had been wired across the opening, and a laminated sign read: **RESTRICTED AREA AUTHORISED REVENANT PERSONNEL ONLY** Somebody had crossed out **REVENANT** and written **EVENT** above it. The real blood stopped near the locked gate. Saf swept her phone torch over the ground. “Jamie’s not answering.” “You called him?” “Obviously.” “I did too.” That made her glance at me. “You’ve got his number?” “He messaged me.” “When?” “Recently.” “When recently?” “Nine days ago.” “And you didn’t think to mention that?” “We haven’t spoken for eleven months, Saf.” “That hasn’t stopped you tonight.” She faced the gate before I could answer. One bar had been bent away from the frame, leaving a gap too narrow for either of us but wide enough to reach through. Something lay inside. Saf put an arm through. Her fingers closed around a phone held together with strips of black electrical tape. When she turned it over, Milo’s crab photograph lit the lock screen. For one second neither of us spoke. “You knew he had it,” I said. “No.” “You didn’t look surprised.” “I’m wearing a tabard that asks strangers about a drowned revenant. My face has used up its reactions.” She pressed the side button. The phone demanded a passcode. “Jamie repairs old phones,” she said. “Milo must’ve left it at home.” “I thought it was lost.” “So did I.” “Why would Helen say it wasn’t returned with his things?” “Because that’s true.” “That’s not the same as saying it was at home.” “Do you want to ring his mum at half twelve and complain that she phrased grief ambiguously?” I did not. Saf crouched again. “There.” Near the phone was a blue inhaler. Beyond it lay a torn scrap of yellow card. Saf stretched farther, shoulder pressed between the bars, and caught both with her fingertips. The inhaler had **J. GRANT** written on it in black marker. The card was an old arcade locker ticket: **STAFF 16**. A brass key had torn away from its punched hole, leaving a jagged circle. “Sixteen,” I said. “Milo’s locker?” “Maybe. Uncle Tariq hasn’t cleared the staff room since about 2009.” The pier groaned overhead. It was only timber responding to the wind. I knew this because the council signs explained that unusual noises were a normal feature of historic structures. The sign was reassuring if you did not ask why reassurance had been required. Saf shone her light through the bars. “Jamie?” No answer. She gripped the bent section of gate. “Don’t,” I said. “I can fit.” “You absolutely cannot.” “I can get my shoulders through.” “Your head is also part of you.” “Still a delight, Price.” The surname struck harder than it should have. A torch beam swept across us. “What do you two think you’re doing?” A man in a fluorescent jacket approached from beneath the pier. I recognised Mr Eames, who drove the festival minibus and once banned Milo from the arcade for attempting to repair a coin pusher with a magnet. Saf stood, keeping Jamie’s inhaler behind her back. “We’re looking for a boy,” she said. “At this hour?” “We thought midnight would make him easier to distinguish from all the other children under the pier.” Mr Eames stopped at the gate. Rain shone on his bald head. “Nobody’s under there now. I chased one out twenty minutes ago.” “What did he look like?” I asked. “Dark hoodie. Trainers. Ran off towards the arcade.” “Was he bleeding?” Mr Eames glanced at the red pavement. “I assumed that was your lot.” “Our lot?” Saf said. “The festival. All this vampire business.” “It’s a revenant,” she said. “Completely different department.” He took out a ring of keys but did not approach the lock. “You need to move away from here. Councillor Vale’s been very clear.” “Why?” I said. “Restricted access.” “Because of what happened to Milo?” Saf turned sharply towards me. Mr Eames’s expression changed—not guilt, exactly. Irritation forced into sympathy. “We’re not having another Grant incident,” he said. “Not tonight.” He told us to leave and waited until we did. At the promenade corner, Saf held up the locker ticket. “The arcade,” she said. “We should call Jamie’s mum.” “You call her, then.” I looked at Milo’s phone in Saf’s hand. Helen Grant had once sat in our kitchen without drinking her tea. After the funeral, she had stopped returning Mum’s messages. I had stopped sending mine first. Saf knew this. “You’re crueler than you used to be,” I said. “No.” She slipped Milo’s phone into her first-aid pouch. “You just used to find it useful.” She started towards the arcade. After a moment, I followed.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Two of this story demonstrates considerable craft in executing a difficult tonal balancing act: darkly comic British YA fiction that must simultaneously establish genuine menace, reintroduce an estranged friendship, and advance a mystery plot. Let's examine it across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND DIALOGUE The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. The exchange between Nell and Saf crackles with authentic tension—the banter is sharp without being performatively witty, and crucially, the humour emerges from character rather than authorial cleverness. Lines like "I'm wearing a tabard that asks strangers about a drowned revenant. My face has used up its reactions" feel genuinely earned. The rhythm of their exchanges captures two people who know each other's patterns intimately but are performing distance. The deodorant/first-aid kit contrast is deployed efficiently and lands well. The dialogue avoids the trap of constant quipping. When Nell says "I came because I thought you might be hurt" and it lands with "more force than intended," the chapter earns its emotional beat without announcing it. The surname "Price" striking harder than it should is handled with restraint. Mr. Eames functions well as a minor character—his "another Grant incident" line does real work, revealing institutional memory and defensiveness without requiring him to be a villain. The final exchange—"You're crueler than you used to be" / "No. You just used to find it useful"—is the chapter's best moment. It's precise, character-revealing, and genuinely stings. This is the kind of line that justifies the whole enterprise. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY Both Nell and Saf are rendered faithfully to their profiles. Nell's observational wit (noting the capital letter, the sign corrections), her self-protective instincts, and her physical awareness of the deodorant in her pocket all track. Her internal admission that the words about Saf being hurt "landed with more force than intended" shows her emotional suppression without stating it. Saf's practical competence (gloves, first-aid knowledge, smell-testing the blood), her confrontational warmth, and her use of "Price" as a distancing mechanism are all consistent. The detail of Saf keeping Jamie's inhaler behind her back—a small, instinctive protective gesture—is excellent characterization without commentary. PLOT EXECUTION The chapter follows its plan closely: establishing both received the message, the brittle reunion, the theatrical blood mixed with real drops, the discovery of Milo's phone, Jamie's inhaler, and the locker ticket. Mr. Eames's appearance and his "another Grant incident" line are handled well. The chapter ends with them heading to the arcade, exactly as planned. The objects (phone, inhaler, locker ticket) are presented as having "fallen from Jamie's pocket" rather than arranged as clever clues—consistent with the plan's insistence on avoiding implausible puzzle-making. SHOWING VS. TELLING The chapter is largely show-don't-tell. Emotional states are conveyed through physical detail and dialogue rather than exposition. The grey hollows under Saf's eyes, the wet curl on her cheek, Nell's hand closing around the deodorant—these do the work efficiently. The one slight weakness is the pier groaning followed by the parenthetical about council signs; this is a bit too neat in its irony, though it's brief. TONAL BALANCE The darkly comic tone is well-calibrated. The humor doesn't undercut the genuine unease—the real blood drops beside the theatrical syrup, the bent gate bar, the inhaler with "J. GRANT" in marker. The chapter maintains the sense that something is genuinely wrong while allowing the characters to be funny about it. WEAKNESSES The chapter is not without flaws. The council sign observation ("unusual noises were a normal feature of historic structures") is slightly over-explained—the irony is spelled out where it could be left implicit. The line "I was going to say that next" is a minor false note, slightly too sitcom-y. The pacing in the middle section (examining the gate, finding the objects) is competent but less distinctive than the dialogue sections. The chapter doesn't quite achieve the density of implication that the best chapters of this genre manage—it's executing its plan efficiently rather than transcending it. The "Saf knew this" paragraph near the end is a small tell-don't-show moment, though it's brief and the surrounding context makes it forgivable. OVERALL This is a confident, well-executed chapter that serves its story faithfully. It's not a masterpiece—it doesn't surprise us with unexpected depths or images that linger—but it's genuinely good work: the dialogue is sharp, the characters are consistent and nuanced, the tonal balance is maintained, and the plot advances cleanly. The final line is strong enough to send the reader forward. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Three ## Underneath We had reached the old bingo hall when Saf stopped. “Did you hear that?” Rain ticked against the metal shutters. Somewhere along the promenade, the festival speakers began their four electronic notes again. “No.” A cough came from beneath the pier. It was low and tight, followed by a breath that whistled at the end. Saf turned and ran back. Mr Eames had gone. His torch moved farther along the beach, a pale circle among the supports. Saf slipped behind a stack of temporary barriers and found where the wire fencing had pulled loose from a concrete block. “I thought he ran towards the arcade,” I whispered. “He saw a dark hoodie. He didn’t ask it to complete a questionnaire.” Saf lifted the fence. “Wait.” She looked down at my hand on her wrist. I let go. “You cannot go under there alone,” I said. “Then come.” This was how it had always worked. Saf went first. Milo called it leadership. I called it evidence. We squeezed through sideways and descended the wet steps to the beach. The tide was out, leaving ribbed sand littered with seaweed, bottle caps and a single glove flattened like roadkill. Above us, St Mercy Pier became something else. From the promenade it was lights, striped kiosks and posters promising **THE SOUTH COAST’S MOST HAUNTING SUNRISE**. Underneath, it was black beams, damp concrete and ironwork thick with rust. Water dripped steadily through the boards. The cough came again. Saf swept her torch between the supports. “Jamie?” “Go away.” Saf’s shoulders dropped by half an inch. He crouched behind a concrete column with his hood pulled up. One hand was pressed to his forehead. The other held a small square of paper. Saf reached him first and pulled his hand down. Blood covered the left side of his face. Real blood is darker than stage blood, except when someone has spread stage blood over it in a layer thick enough to ice a cupcake. “Oh, you absolute little—” “I’m fine.” “You’ve painted your head.” “It was already bleeding.” Saf opened her first-aid pouch. Jamie looked at me over her shoulder. He had grown since the funeral, though not enough to fill Milo’s old black hoodie. The sleeves swallowed his hands. His dark blond fringe was wet and stuck to the blood. “You came, then,” he said. I wanted to hug him. I wanted to shake him. Instead, I said, “You sent it.” “No. Milo did.” Saf pressed gauze to his eyebrow. He flinched. “Ow.” “Ghosts don’t repair charging ports with electrical tape,” she said. “Hold this.” Jamie held the gauze. “You used his number,” I said. “You wouldn’t have come for mine.” “You didn’t give me a chance.” “I gave you nine days.” Saf’s eyes flicked towards me. “I was going to answer,” I said. Jamie snorted, then winced because snorting moved his eyebrow. “Where did you get the blood?” Saf asked. “Festival box.” “So you stole it from my mum.” “I borrowed one sachet.” “You used fake blood on a real head wound. Do you know how stupid that is?” “It made it easier to see.” “It made it look like your brain had come out.” Jamie considered this. “You came faster.” Saf went very still. I recognised that stillness. It meant she was either about to cry or say something designed to make somebody else do it. “What are you doing under here?” I asked quickly. Jamie lifted the square of paper. It was a photograph, its surface freckled by rain. One of the pier’s iron joints filled the frame. A circle had been drawn around a line of corrosion in red pen. In the corner, Milo’s thumb covered part of the lens. “I found it in his room,” Jamie said. “There’s a notebook too. Numbers and stuff about the supports.” “And that meant you should crawl under the pier at midnight?” “I wanted to find which one.” “You could have shown us the photograph.” “I tried showing Saf.” Saf peeled open an antiseptic wipe. “You sent me a picture of a rusty bolt during the breakfast rush.” “You said later.” “It was six forty in the morning.” “Later was nine days.” Saf cleaned the cut. Underneath the stage blood, it was short but deep enough to keep leaking. “How did you do this?” “Hit the bracket.” “While being chased?” “Wasn’t chased.” “Mr Eames said—” “He shouted. I ran.” This distinction seemed important to Jamie. I took the photograph. Behind the red circle, the metal had split into scaly layers. I shone my torch along the nearest column until I found a matching joint. A handwritten red sixteen marked the concrete. Milo’s writing. The bracket was worse than in the picture. One bolt had almost disappeared beneath orange crust. I touched the edge and a flake came away into my palm, soft as burnt paper. “Don’t touch it,” Saf said. “I barely did.” “That’s not reassuring.” A rumble passed overhead. The boards trembled, then settled with several small knocks. Fine rust sifted into Jamie’s hair. We looked up. “A delivery trolley,” Saf said. “They’re taking the urns over.” Jamie held out another folded page. “It says they shouldn’t.” The photocopy had been creased into quarters. Most of the heading was missing, but a table listed support numbers and conditions. Sixteen was marked **ADVANCED SECTION LOSS**. Below that, a paragraph recommended “immediate restriction of concentrated loading pending intrusive examination.” “Is there a date?” Saf asked. Jamie shook his head. “Who wrote it?” “Don’t know.” “Where’s the rest?” “In Milo’s locker. I think.” “You think?” “He wrote sixteen on the photo and kept an arcade ticket with it.” I read the paragraph twice. The words were careful, which made them worse. “It could be old,” Saf said. “It could already have been repaired.” Jamie pointed at the bracket. It had not been repaired. Mr Eames’s torch swung towards us from farther down the beach. Saf folded the photocopy and shoved it into her tabard. “We’re leaving.” “I’m getting more pictures,” Jamie said. “No.” “You don’t get to tell me.” “I’m the one currently stopping your forehead falling off.” Jamie pulled the gauze away. Fresh blood spotted it. His breathing had begun to whistle again. “Use your inhaler,” I said. He glared at me. “It’s by the gate.” His glare became less effective. Saf caught his chin and made him look at her. “You sent the code. We came. That means you don’t get to keep inventing the emergency.” The torch beam struck the next support. This time Jamie did not argue. We ducked between the columns and hurried for the gap. As I squeezed beneath the fence, the pier shifted overhead with a long wooden groan. It was probably normal. I had always been good at probably.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Three of this story demonstrates considerable craft and discipline. The model has executed the chapter plan faithfully while maintaining a distinctive voice that avoids the pitfalls of generic YA snark. The chapter opens with economy and precision. The sensory details are specific without being overwrought: "Rain ticked against the metal shutters," "ribbed sand littered with seaweed, bottle caps and a single glove flattened like roadkill." The glove simile is the kind of casual, slightly grim observation that feels genuinely Nell's rather than performed. It lands. The transformation of the pier from tourist attraction to industrial menace is handled well: "From the promenade it was lights, striped kiosks and posters promising THE SOUTH COAST'S MOST HAUNTING SUNRISE. Underneath, it was black beams, damp concrete and ironwork thick with rust." This contrast is earned and not over-explained. The model resists the temptation to make it a heavy metaphor. The Jamie reveal is managed with appropriate restraint. The stage blood over real blood is a good comic-dark beat, and the exchange "You've painted your head" / "It was already bleeding" / "I was going to answer" / "I gave you nine days" is sharp, economical dialogue that reveals character without exposition. The nine-days detail is particularly effective: it's specific enough to feel real and damning enough to sting. Character consistency is strong throughout. Nell's observational precision ("Real blood is darker than stage blood, except when someone has spread stage blood over it in a layer thick enough to ice a cupcake") is exactly the voice established in the profiles—dry, precise, occasionally unkind. Saf's practical competence with the first-aid kit, her quick anger at the fake blood, and her eventual firm authority ("You sent the code. We came. That means you don't get to keep inventing the emergency") are all consistent with her established character. Jamie's defensive hostility, his use of Milo's hoodie, his inhaler pride, and his emotional coercion are all present and correctly calibrated. The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. It does real work: it reveals the nine-day gap, establishes the power dynamics, conveys Jamie's grief-anger, and moves the plot forward simultaneously. The exchange about the breakfast rush photograph is particularly good—it's funny, it's specific, and it illuminates both characters without authorial intrusion. The structural evidence (the photocopy, the bracket, the rust flaking into Nell's palm) is introduced with appropriate ambiguity. The model correctly follows the plan's instruction that the evidence be "alarming but not definitive." The missing date and author are noted. The physical danger is made tangible without melodrama—the trolley rumble, the rust sifting into Jamie's hair, the long wooden groan at the end. The closing line—"I had always been good at probably"—is the chapter's best moment. It's a perfect encapsulation of Nell's self-protective rationalization, delivered with exactly the right lightness. It earns its place. There are minor weaknesses. "Saf's shoulders dropped by half an inch" is a nice detail, but the chapter occasionally relies on small physical tells (the stillness, the shoulder drop) that, while effective, are used frequently enough to become slightly formulaic. The line "It meant she was either about to cry or say something designed to make somebody else do it" is good but slightly over-explains what the physical detail already conveyed. The chapter follows its plan almost exactly: the cough, the gap in the fencing, finding Jamie with the theatrical blood over a real wound, the photocopy without date or author, the physical evidence of corrosion, the inhaler, the security torch, and the departure. The plan's instruction that "the walkway overhead shifts when a delivery trolley crosses the pier" is executed precisely. The locker is established as the next destination. The dark comedy is well-earned rather than imposed. The humor emerges from character and situation rather than from authorial cleverness. The Britishness is present in texture (the festival volunteer, the delivery urns, the specific social dynamics) rather than as a collection of props. One small concern: the chapter is perhaps slightly too smooth in its execution. Every beat lands, every line does work, but there's a slight sense of controlled competence rather than genuine surprise. The model is executing a plan very well rather than discovering something unexpected. This is a minor criticism of an otherwise strong chapter. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Model Output:
# Chapter Four ## Milo’s Locker Saf’s uncle Tariq was asleep in a plastic chair behind the arcade prize counter. The machines were off, but the carpet retained enough colour for the entire building. Yellow stars and pink planets glowed beneath the emergency lights. Even after closing, the place smelled of warm dust, old chips and the coconut air freshener Tariq hung above the change machine. Saf knocked on the counter. Tariq opened one eye. “Fire?” “No.” “Robbery?” “No.” He closed it again. “Then morning.” “I need the storeroom key.” Both eyes opened. He looked at Saf’s tabard, then Jamie’s bloody forehead, then me. His face paused on mine. “Nell Price.” “Hello.” “You’ve grown.” “I haven’t.” “Then I’ve shrunk.” He pushed himself upright. “What happened to him?” “Festival decoration,” Jamie said. Saf said, “Shelf.” I said nothing. It was the only answer not currently making matters worse. Tariq stared at us until Saf added, “Mum needs the spare cup sleeves.” “At half past one?” “Dawn ceremony.” “There are sleeves in the café cellar.” “Compostable ones.” This apparently settled it. Tariq took a key from the drawer and handed it over. “No touching the machines,” he said. “No food. No ghosts.” “Specific,” I said. “Last year was busy.” The storeroom occupied the back of the arcade, beyond a door marked **STAFF ONLY** and another marked **THIS DOOR ALARMED**, although the alarm had been removed, leaving two pale screw holes. Jamie led us to a row of dented metal lockers. Number sixteen had a sticker on it: **MILO – OWES £6.40**. I had written that. The sight of my own handwriting made the room tilt slightly. Saf noticed. She did not say anything. “There’s no key,” Jamie said. “The ticket was sixteen,” I said. “Not a key.” “I know.” “You said Milo kept things here.” “He did.” Saf pulled a butter knife from a box of café cutlery. I looked at her. “Why do you have experience opening lockers?” “I have experience working with Tariq.” She pushed the knife into the gap beside the latch and struck the door once with her palm. It opened immediately. “Security here is mostly emotional,” she said. Milo’s locker did not contain a complete engineering report, a signed confession or anything else likely to make the night simpler. It contained two faded uniform shirts, one trainer without its partner, a dead power bank, three hotel pencils, receipts, a bicycle light, a paperback with the final page folded over, and a packet of sweets furry with green mould. Saf picked up a mug printed with **WORLD’S OKAYEST EMPLOYEE**. “He stole that from the café.” “He said you gave it to him,” I said. “He also said seals were legally fish.” Jamie began emptying everything onto the floor. “Careful,” Saf said. “He’s dead. He won’t tell Tariq.” Saf’s hand tightened around the mug. “That doesn’t mean wreck his stuff.” “He left it in a locker for a year.” “He didn’t exactly choose storage.” Jamie looked at her, face closing. I crouched and examined the notebook beneath the shirts. The cover had swollen with damp. Milo’s handwriting filled half the pages, leaning uphill as if trying to escape. There were lists of support numbers, sketches of brackets, arrows pointing to words like **BAD**, **VERY BAD** and **COUNCIL-GRADE BAD**. Some measurements had units; others ended in question marks. Between them were potential titles for a video: **ST MERCY PIER: COME FOR THE VIEWS, STAY BECAUSE THE FLOOR COLLAPSED** **LOCAL COUNCIL HATES THIS ONE WEIRD STRUCTURAL DEFECT** Saf read over my shoulder. “He thought he was hilarious.” “He frequently was.” “Frequently is not always.” Jamie searched the seams of the locker, then reached inside the notebook’s back cover. His fingernail caught on black tape. A memory card came away with it. The storeroom computer took seven minutes to start and made a noise like a refrigerator losing an argument. Saf stood watch by the door while Jamie held his inhaler between his teeth and clicked through error messages. “There,” he said. The card contained nineteen files. Eleven would not open. Three were photographs of Milo’s shoes. One was entirely black. “Atmospheric,” I said. Saf’s mouth twitched before she stopped it. The first useful picture showed the bracket we had found, taken in daylight. The next showed a row of supports farther out, orange streaks running into the water. In another, Milo held a ruler against a gap in a joint, though he had photographed it at an angle that made the measurement useless. Then came a document on a desk. Only part of the cover was visible: **ST MERCY PIER PRELIMINARY CONDITION— 17 NOV—** The year matched the autumn before Milo died. Jamie leaned so close his breath fogged the screen. “See?” “It says preliminary,” Saf said. “It says condition.” “It doesn’t say death trap.” The next image showed a paragraph recommending restrictions. Half of it was obscured by a takeaway carton. Milo had underlined **concentrated loading** in red. The final photograph was taken beneath the pier on the afternoon he died. I knew the date because it was printed in the corner. Beside support sixteen stood a yellow warning board: **ACCESS PROHIBITED STRUCTURAL INSPECTION** By the time the police searched beneath the pier that night, the sign had gone. Nobody spoke for several seconds. Jamie broke the silence. “They knew.” “We don’t know who put the sign there,” Saf said. “They took it away.” “Signs get moved.” “Before or after he fell?” Saf turned from the screen. “I’m not defending them.” “Sounds like it.” “I’m trying not to pretend a blurry photograph says whatever I want.” Jamie opened Milo’s notebook again and found a page headed **REV NIGHT**. Beneath it Milo had written: **Need footage when busy enough for them to care. Static rust = boring. Movement = proof.** Below that was a list of names. Saf. Nell. My name had a line beneath it. Beside it, Milo had written: **She’ll say no. Ask anyway.** My phone vibrated before either of them could look at me. Saf checked hers at the same time. A volunteer alert glowed across the screen. **SUNRISE ACCESS OPENS 02:20. ALL STAFF TO POSITIONS.** She swore. “How long?” Jamie asked. “Forty minutes.” Saf looked from the warning sign on the computer to Milo’s note in my hand. “What did he ask you?” she said. From the arcade, Tariq called, “Saf? Your mother says the urns are already going over.” Above us, faint through the ceiling, something heavy rolled onto the pier.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of YA fiction that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTERISATION Nell's narration is consistently rendered. The dry, observational wit comes through without being overdone: "Security here is mostly emotional" lands cleanly, as does the exchange about seals being "legally fish." The moment where Nell sees her own handwriting on the locker sticker ("MILO – OWES £6.40") and the room tilts slightly is one of the chapter's best beats — it's economical, specific, and emotionally true without being announced. Saf's practicality (the butter knife, the first-aid instincts, the careful parsing of what the photographs actually prove) is consistent with her profile. Jamie's grief-anger, his possessiveness over Milo's things, and his tendency to leap to conclusions are all faithfully rendered. The Tariq scene is a small gem. The rhythm of the exchange — "Fire?" / "No." / "Robbery?" / "No." — is perfectly calibrated, and "Last year was busy" earns its laugh without straining. This is the kind of dark comedy the plan called for: local, specific, and not trying too hard. DIALOGUE The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. It's terse, character-specific, and does real work. The argument between Jamie and Saf over Milo's things ("He's dead. He won't tell Tariq." / "That doesn't mean wreck his stuff.") is genuinely good — it captures grief's tendency to become territorial and the way people fight about objects when they can't fight about the real thing. The exchange about the photographs ("It says preliminary." / "It says condition." / "It doesn't say death trap.") is excellent: it shows Saf's hard-won caution against Jamie's desperate need for certainty, without either character being made a fool. PLOT AND STRUCTURE The chapter follows its plan faithfully.
