She would rather fly through dangerous weather than apologize, repair an engine alone rather than ask for help, or sacrifice something valuable rather than admit she wants to keep it. She protects her self-image through precise distinctions: she arrested the protesters but did not order the seizure; she accepted a bonus but did not request it; she resigned but did not lie at the inquiry. Each statement is true, and together they conceal the truth. She can be controlling under pressure. Eli’s impulsiveness infuriates her because it mirrors the part of herself she suppresses. She mistrusts Sanaa’s secrecy while keeping her own history compartmentalized. She sometimes treats leadership as the right to withhold fear from others, which leaves her crew without information they need. Mara is also proud of her competence. Creed can provoke her by implying she is merely lucky or by using her old rank. She is capable of making a tactically bad choice to avoid appearing subordinate. Her better qualities are inseparable from these flaws. She takes responsibility for immediate dangers, rarely panics, and will place herself between violence and someone less prepared for it. She does not make grand promises. When she says she will do something, she generally does it, even after deciding it was foolish. ## Physicality Mara is of medium height, broad through the shoulders, and stronger than her lean face suggests. She wears her dark hair clipped close at the sides and cuts the rest herself with little concern for symmetry. Her nose was broken during marshal training and healed slightly crooked. The tip of her left ring finger is missing from an old cargo-door accident. Her right palm bears a circular electrical scar. The dust calf is drawn to its altered conductivity, a fact she finds unnerving. She moves economically aboard the ship, catching rails without looking and turning sideways before narrow passages require it. On land, particularly in open spaces, she seems subtly less certain. She stands with her weight balanced and her hands visible, a habit left from marshal work. When angry, she becomes quieter and more formally polite. Her face is not naturally impassive; she has simply trained it. Disapproval appears first as a slight tightening beside her nose. Genuine fear makes her blink rapidly. She rarely smiles broadly, but amusement pulls one corner of her mouth upward before she suppresses it. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships Mara’s immediate motive is to preserve the *Wayward Sun*, keep her crew alive, and remain free of Helix control. These motives conflict as soon as the crate is opened. Her primary allegiance is to her ship because she understands what that means. Her allegiance to Eli and Sanaa is real but harder for her to name. She calls them crew, pays them when she can, and treats continued employment as evidence of mutual regard. She has not noticed that neither has somewhere else to go. She likes Eli’s ingenuity and resents his recklessness. She has begun to rely on him enough that his mistakes feel personal. She is harsher with him when frightened for him. She respects Sanaa’s competence but has always suspected she boarded the ship to escape something. Mara has not asked because she fears reciprocal questions. Sanaa’s concealment of the specimen feels like both a professional betrayal and an exposure of their unspoken arrangement. Mara once admired Creed. He taught her how to survive confrontations and occasionally protected her from more brutal officers. Their bond was never romantic, though other marshals assumed it was. She left him to bear the inquiry alone. Her anger at him contains shame; his anger at her contains a wounded sense of ownership. Mara does not expect forgiveness from Tessa, but part of her still wants it. She initially treats useful action as a substitute for apology. Tessa’s refusal to participate in that exchange unsettles her more than open hatred would. ## Voice Mara speaks in short, concrete sentences and dislikes declarations she cannot enforce. Her internal voice is more evasive, often recasting emotional problems as logistics. > “You drilled air holes into unknown cargo.” > > “Only three,” Eli said. > > “I can see the number, Rook. I’m questioning the philosophy.” > The offer was good. That was the ugly part. Bad offers let a person keep a clean opinion of herself. > “I didn’t come back for forgiveness.” > > Tessa looked at her. > > Mara wished, immediately and uselessly, that she had found a less obvious lie. # Eli Rook ## Background Eli Rook is twenty-eight and serves as the *Wayward Sun*’s mechanic, cargo handler, and occasional copilot. He was born in a Helix extraction camp on Low Mercy, a moon whose labor contracts were inherited as family debt. His mother repaired drilling motors; his father kept inventory until an audit accused him of theft. Eli believes the accusation was false but admits his father did steal tools afterward. At fourteen, Eli began working maintenance shifts in tunnels designed for adults. The company used conductive restraint cuffs on workers accused of tampering with equipment. Eli wore them twice: once for bypassing a safety lock to keep a heater running, and once for striking a supervisor who mocked an injured friend. The pale paired scars around his wrists are why he reacts so strongly to the dust calf’s restraints. He escaped Low Mercy aboard a waste hauler at nineteen. During the next several years, he moved among temporary jobs and acquired a broad, uneven mechanical education. He can rebuild a coolant pump from mismatched parts but cannot explain some of the underlying theory. He joined the *Wayward Sun* after repairing its landing gear in exchange for passage. Mara intended to drop him at the next station. He has remained aboard for four years. ## Personality and Idiosyncrasies Eli is inventive, restless, sociable, and more calculating than his easy manner suggests. He uses humor to test whether people are dangerous. If they laugh, he relaxes; if they do not, he becomes more polite. He likes taking things apart, particularly expensive things marked “no user-serviceable components.” He keeps screws, connectors, and springs in his pockets, then forgets which machine they came from. Mara has instituted a rule that anything removed from a working system must be labeled. Eli follows this rule by writing labels such as “probably galley” or “important, ask later.” He whistles while concentrating but repeats only the first five notes of any tune. Sanaa has threatened him with sedation over this. He eats rapidly, standing up, as if meals may be revoked. He hoards sealed condiment packets and denies doing so despite a drawer full of them. Eli enjoys gambling but dislikes games based solely on chance. He cheats at cards through mechanical tricks and becomes offended when accused of cheating through ordinary sleight of hand. He has an unexpectedly good memory for voices and can imitate the *Wayward Sun*’s soothing alarm system exactly. He is fascinated by animals because Low Mercy had none larger than insects. This does not make him naturally good with them. He mistakes curiosity for rapport and persists after clear warnings. His insistence on calling the specimen a dust calf is partly teasing Sanaa and partly an effort to make the unknown manageable. ## Flaws Eli acts before asking permission, then treats the result as a shared problem. He drills the crate, opens it, and releases the creature because waiting feels too much like obedience. Although his impulse is compassionate, it causes ship damage and places the crew in greater danger. He wants to be the person an unfamiliar creature trusts. This desire leads him to project emotions and intentions onto it. When it remains still, he assumes calm; when it follows him, he assumes attachment. He is slow to accept that he may mean nothing to it. Eli resents authority so reflexively that he sometimes rejects sound instructions. He is also sensitive about education and hears condescension where none is intended. Sanaa’s technical corrections can start arguments even when he knows she is right. Money matters to him. His first thought on learning the crate contains a rare organism is what it might sell for. He feels ashamed of that thought and overcorrects into protectiveness. He has a private reserve of credits hidden inside an empty fire-suppression canister. He tells himself it is an emergency fund for the whole crew, but Mara does not know it exists. Eli is loyal, but his loyalty often expresses itself as interference. He will disobey Mara to save her from a choice he thinks she should not have to make. ## Physicality Eli is tall and narrow, with long arms useful in maintenance spaces and knees perpetually bruised from crawling through them. His brown skin is stippled with small burn marks along his forearms. He wears his black hair in short twists, often with a loose wire or plastic tie holding them back. He has an expressive face that makes deceit difficult unless he can keep talking. His left canine is chipped. He bites the inside of his cheek when worried and taps nearby surfaces to judge their thickness. Eli is rarely still. He rocks on his heels, turns tools through his fingers, and leans against machinery he has been told not to touch. Around authority figures, his posture changes: shoulders rounded, gaze lowered, hands apparently idle. It is an old camp-worker performance, and Mara recognizes when he uses it. His movements around the dust calf begin overly confident and become slower after it bites him. He carries the second bite as an insult before accepting it as information. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships Eli wants durable safety without admitting he wants permanence. He claims he will buy his own repair shop one day but has never investigated a location. What he really wants is a place where nobody can terminate his air, food, or employment through a contract clause. He is loyal to Mara because she pays him a full share when possible and has never threatened to report his false transit documents. He also sees her compromises more clearly than she thinks. He believes she is better than her past, which sometimes places unfair pressure on her to prove him right. He likes Sanaa and delights in irritating her. Her education makes him defensive, but he trusts her hands in a medical emergency. He senses that she is ashamed of something and refrains from pressing because he understands concealed histories. His attachment to the dust calf begins in recognition of its restraints, then becomes tangled with pride. He wants its behavior to validate his decision to free it. Its refusal to offer affection forces him toward a less possessive form of care. He distrusts Creed immediately, not because he knows Creed’s history but because Creed uses the measured tone of supervisors explaining that punishment is procedural. ## Voice Eli’s dialogue is quick, irreverent, and inclined toward analogy. When frightened, he becomes more talkative. His internal thoughts are concrete and occasionally self-mocking. > “Captain, something’s... moving in that crate we picked up.” > > “Define moving.” > > “Changing neighborhoods.” > It had bitten him twice. This was not, Eli admitted, the foundation of the profound interspecies friendship he had briefly imagined. > “You can call it Specimen Twelve if you like, Doctor. It has six legs, lives in dirt, and just ate a cable worth more than me. That’s livestock.” # Dr. Sanaa Vale ## Background Sanaa Vale is thirty-nine and serves as medic aboard the *Wayward Sun*. Her formal training is in xenobiological analytics rather than clinical medicine. Frontier necessity widened her practice: she can set bones, treat decompression injuries, diagnose common infections, and recognize when a patient needs facilities the ship does not have. She grew up in a middle-class Helix habitat where the company was not seen as an occupying power but as the institution that provided schools, air, and professional advancement. Her parents were teachers under corporate contract. Sanaa earned a scholarship and believed for years that careful science could improve frontier life. She worked in a laboratory analyzing samples from Canaan’s Reach. When native filament and tissue indicated a complex ecosystem, her supervisor told her the specimens had been contaminated during collection. Sanaa altered the report to exclude them. She had doubts but wanted to keep her position and trusted the institutional explanation because it was convenient. Later, she discovered repeated samples had been suppressed and living specimens taken into Helix custody. She copied partial records but did not expose them. She resigned under the pretense of burnout, drifted through contract medical work, and eventually joined Mara’s crew. At the abandoned depot, Sanaa encountered the surviving specimen during a security evacuation. She concealed it in the *Wayward Sun*’s salvage because the ship was loading and Helix personnel were approaching. Her action was desperate, poorly planned, and partly driven by a need to reverse something she had helped do. ## Personality and Idiosyncrasies Sanaa is precise, private, patient with pain, and impatient with imprecision. She corrects terminology even when doing so is socially unwise. She objects to “dust calf” because the organism is not taxonomically related to livestock and because no one knows whether it is juvenile. Her corrections only encourage Eli. She washes her hands more often than necessary, especially after lying. She keeps her medical bay meticulously arranged except for one drawer filled with unsorted personal items: old identification cards, broken spectacles, dried tea leaves, and a letter from her mother she has not opened in two years. Sanaa enjoys cheap serialized romances and disguises them on her tablet as pharmacology manuals. Eli knows and occasionally quotes them at her. She has a beautiful singing voice but refuses to sing where anyone can hear. She hums while suturing patients because it steadies her hands. She dislikes food touching other food and carries a folding partitioned plate. She can remain calm while treating a severe injury but becomes flustered by public gratitude. She has a habit of removing her spectacles when she wants more time to answer, polishing already-clean lenses while choosing the least revealing truth. Sanaa’s humor is dry, exact, and usually delivered without a visible smile. She is capable of considerable tenderness but tends to express it through preparation: setting aside painkillers, warming a blanket, memorizing allergies. ## Flaws Sanaa trusts credentials and controlled procedures even after institutions have betrayed that trust. Under stress, she defaults to clinical language because it distances her from responsibility. She says she “excluded anomalous results” rather than “helped erase evidence.” She withholds information until she believes she can present it completely. Since certainty is impossible, this becomes habitual secrecy. Her delay in telling Mara about the specimen and its risks is not only fear; it is an attempt to control how others judge her. Sanaa wants saving the organism to mean she is not the person who altered the report. This can make her treat it as an opportunity for redemption rather than a living being. She is offended when it reacts fearfully to her, although her scent and voice may be associated with laboratory restraint. Her knowledge is partial, but she sometimes speaks with more authority than it warrants. She has examined samples and records, not observed the species in its habitat. Her initial assumptions about its age, behavior, and ecological function may be wrong. She avoids direct conflict until concealment becomes untenable. Once exposed, however, she can swing into reckless honesty, disclosing information without considering who may be harmed by it. ## Physicality Sanaa is short and solidly built, with a round face and a faint crease between her brows from years of squinting at laboratory displays. Her black hair is threaded with premature gray and usually bound in a low knot. She wears rectangular spectacles even though corrective surgery is available; she distrusts elective procedures and likes being able to remove her vision when exhausted. A tremor affects the little finger of her right hand when she is severely fatigued. She hides it by clasping her hands or assigning delicate work to her left. Her posture is upright at a workbench but slumps the moment she believes herself unobserved. Sanaa interacts with objects by arranging them. She squares containers to table edges, coils cables evenly, and automatically turns labels outward. When the ship lurches, she reaches first for another person and only afterward secures herself. The dust calf’s presence unsettles her physically. She holds her breath near it, keeps her hands visible, and avoids blocking exits. It responds to her antiseptic scent with agitation, which she pretends not to take personally. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships Sanaa wants to preserve the organism and its ecological role, but her motives include guilt, scientific awe, and the hope of producing undeniable evidence. These are not always aligned. Evidence might require invasive examination; protection might require accepting uncertainty. Her loyalty to the *Wayward Sun*’s crew has grown through routine rather than declaration. She knows Mara’s old injuries and Eli’s recurring nightmares. She has named them as emergency contacts without telling either one. She respects Mara’s decisiveness while resenting her habit of turning every argument into a command question. Sanaa recognizes Mara’s moral avoidance because it resembles her own. This makes their confrontations unusually sharp. She treats Eli like an irritating younger colleague, not a child. She admires his mechanical intuition and dislikes his contempt for formal expertise. His willingness to touch the creature without certainty both frightens and envies her. Sanaa fears Creed because his authority can transform her past choices into crimes at any moment. She also understands that he may sincerely believe the organism poses a contamination risk; she once helped create the records supporting that belief. ## Voice Sanaa speaks carefully, often qualifying claims. Under pressure, her sentences become more technical. Her internal voice is analytical until emotion breaks through it. > “It is not a calf.” > > “Does it have a better name?” > > “It has a specimen designation.” > > “That’s not better. That’s what you write on a jar.” > Contaminated had been such a useful word. It meant the evidence was wrong, the procedure had failed, and no person had chosen anything. > “I did not know what would happen when I put it aboard.” > > Mara stared at her. > > Sanaa removed her spectacles. “That is not a defense. It is the portion I can prove.” # Tessa Ward ## Background Tessa Ward is forty-three and Dry River’s senior water mechanic, though Helix recognizes her title only as “local systems liaison.” She was born during the settlement’s construction and has never lived elsewhere. Her parents installed the original filtration system, using components rated for fifteen years that are now approaching forty. At twenty-six, Tessa helped organize an occupation of the pumping station after Helix raised water rates and began reclaiming condensation from private dwellings. Mara arrested her during the clearance. Tessa spent seven months in a labor facility and returned to find her mother dead and the pumping station under tighter corporate control. Tessa’s younger brother, Renn, has renal damage caused by childhood exposure to poorly filtered groundwater. He requires regular medication delivered through Helix channels. Tessa has spent years keeping him alive with the same systems she despises. This has cured her of romantic ideas about resistance. She is respected in Dry River because she can keep old equipment operating and because she does not pretend there is a painless option. She is not the mayor, sheriff, or unanimous voice of the town. Some residents consider her too willing to compromise with Helix; others remember that her earlier resistance cost them work and water. ## Personality and Idiosyncrasies Tessa is blunt, unsentimental, and alert to the gap between principles and maintenance schedules. She asks what something will cost, how long it will take, and who will clean up afterward. She keeps obsolete components because “obsolete” often means merely unsupported. Her workshop shelves are arranged by failure type rather than component type: leaks, heat, corrosion, lies. The “lies” shelf contains proprietary parts that are standard hardware inside branded casings. Tessa chews dried mint root to mask the metallic taste of recycled air. She slices it with a small utility knife while thinking. She dislikes chairs and conducts most conversations while working. If someone wants her full attention, they must hand her a tool. She knows the sound of every major pump in Dry River. At night she wakes when one stops, not when an alarm begins. She labels repaired equipment with the date and the initials of whoever helped, including children. Tessa has little patience for symbolic gestures. She would rather receive a gasket than flowers and would regard naming a building after her as an insult if the plumbing remained broken. She enjoys old dance broadcasts in private and knows elaborate steps that Dry River’s narrow corridors cannot accommodate. ## Flaws Tessa’s practicality can become moral fatalism. She has learned to distrust uncertain benefits so deeply that she may defend a harmful status quo because she knows how to survive it. She tells herself that keeping people alive today is the only honest priority, sometimes using this to avoid risks that others may have the right to take. Her protectiveness toward Renn is controlling. She rarely asks what he wants and assumes his survival justifies decisions made on his behalf. Turning over the organism for his medicine is understandable, but it also removes the choice from both him and the town. Tessa can use other people’s guilt as leverage. She does not forgive Mara, but she is willing to let Mara believe forgiveness might be possible if that belief keeps her working. She dislikes this trait in herself and does it anyway. She remembers grievances in exact detail and can mistake memory for fairness. Mara’s return activates an old anger that may distort Tessa’s judgment of present actions. She is capable of dismissing sincere change because accepting it would complicate a hatred that has helped her endure. ## Physicality Tessa is tall, heavyset, and physically powerful from moving equipment in cramped spaces. She has close-cropped gray-brown hair and a burn scar running from beneath her right ear to her collarbone. Her hands are broad, with chemical staining under the nails and swollen joints she ignores. She favors sleeveless work jackets with many narrow pockets. She wears a knee brace under her trousers but removes it when she expects trouble because she hates its restriction, then limps more afterward. Tessa occupies space without apology. She stands close when she wants an honest answer and continues working when she does not consider a conversation important. Her expressions are restrained but not hidden. Disgust pulls down her whole face; amusement appears as a brief closing of the eyes. Around Renn, her movements become gentler and fussier. Around Mara, she deliberately keeps her hands occupied, partly to avoid striking her and partly to deny Mara the dignity of a formal confrontation. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships Tessa’s first allegiance is to Renn, followed by Dry River as a collection of particular people rather than an abstract community. She wants local control of the water system but not at the price of gambling everyone’s immediate survival. She does not trust Helix, yet she negotiates with its representatives because medicine and proprietary parts arrive on its ships. Her opposition is experienced, not ideological. She knows which company agents can be bribed, delayed, flattered, or shamed. She remembers Mara as the composed officer who placed restraints on her and then avoided looking at her during transport. Mara’s confession about the bonus confirms Tessa’s worst assumptions but changes little about the present emergency. Tessa will accept Mara’s labor without granting emotional release. She respects Sanaa’s knowledge but distrusts her uncertainty. She likes Eli more than she wishes to because he treats old machinery as repairable rather than obsolete. She considers his optimism dangerous. Tessa sees the dust calf neither as a sacred being nor as property. It is a living creature and a possible mechanism for change. That does not automatically make its life worth more to her than Renn’s. ## Voice Tessa speaks concretely and dislikes moral abstractions. She often answers an emotional appeal with a material fact. > “You want to say you’re sorry, say it while you’re holding that coupling. I need both hands.” > “A future is useful. So is a dose of renal stabilizer. Only one of those is on the ship outside.” > Mara had returned wanting punishment or forgiveness. Tessa had no spare supply of either, and the west pump was making that noise again. # Marshal Silas Creed ## Background Silas Creed is fifty-two and a senior field marshal for Helix Dominion. He was raised in a succession of temporary frontier camps by parents who followed construction contracts from planet to planet. He learned young that rules changed with each supervisor and that the safest position was beside the person enforcing them. He joined Helix security rather than the military because company service offered better pensions and less chance of deployment to a formal war. He became skilled at ending disputes without public bloodshed, though often by making private threats. His record describes him as restrained, reliable, and effective. Creed trained Mara when she entered the marshal service. He taught her how to read a crowd, how to stand outside arm’s reach, and how to offer people a face-saving route before applying force. He admired her competence and regarded her loyalty as partly his creation. During the Dry River seizure, Creed approved the arrests and managed the operation. When an internal inquiry followed, Mara resigned before giving evidence. Creed remained and defended the action as compliant with company policy. He was cleared but lost a likely promotion. He views Mara’s departure less as moral dissent than as abandonment: she shared the act but left him its consequences. Creed knows the current operation only through Helix summaries. He has been told the organism could contaminate water systems and invalidate valuable land claims. He suspects the second point matters more to his superiors but does not know Sanaa’s full history. ## Personality and Idiosyncrasies Creed is disciplined, observant, vain about his professionalism, and less emotionally controlled than he believes. He rarely raises his voice because he considers shouting evidence that authority has failed. He keeps his uniform immaculate but wears inexpensive civilian boots because official footwear hurts his arches. Mara knows this and once mocked him for it. He cooks elaborate meals aboard his cutter, using fresh herbs grown under the cockpit lights. He becomes genuinely offended when people oversalt food before tasting it. Creed cleans his sidearm when anxious, even if it does not need cleaning. He dislikes silence on a comm channel and may continue talking to fill it, revealing more than intended. He quotes regulations inaccurately but with confidence; deputies rarely correct him. He calls ships by their registered names rather than crew nicknames. The *Wayward Sun* remains the *HS-441 Larkspur* to him because that is the name on its original record. Mara hates this. Creed sends small gifts after disputes: replacement gloves, better coffee, a book mentioned months earlier. He experiences generosity as a way of restoring hierarchy. If the recipient refuses the gift, he feels personally insulted. ## Flaws Creed mistakes predictability for justice. If a rule is consistently enforced, he considers it fair even when the rule itself is exploitative. He values stability partly because instability threatens the professional identity on which he has built his life. He is paternalistic. He believes he understands what frightened workers, settlers, and junior officers need better than they do. When they reject his protection, he interprets it as ingratitude or manipulation by someone else. His pursuit of Mara is not purely professional. He wants her to admit that leaving him was cowardice and that his decision to remain was strength. This personal grievance makes him overcommit to the recovery operation when waiting for specialist support might be wiser. Creed is not omniscient and hates acknowledging uncertainty. He presents Helix’s contamination warning as settled science despite having read only a summary. When new evidence appears, he treats it as a challenge to his authority before considering its content. He is capable of mercy, but his mercy demands recognition. He wants people to understand that he could have hurt them more. This makes even his restraint coercive. ## Physicality Creed is tall but has developed a slight forward stoop from years at shipboard consoles. His face is long and carefully shaved, with deep lines around his mouth. His thinning blond-gray hair is cut to regulation length. He has pale eyes that water in dry air, forcing him to blink during confrontations—a trait Mara once mistook for nervousness. He moves deliberately and keeps one hand near his belt without resting it on his weapon. He takes time removing gloves before serious conversations, arranging them finger to finger. When genuinely angry, he puts them back on. Creed dislikes crawling, cramped tunnels, and getting dirty. In the pumping chamber, his polished control deteriorates as his uniform becomes wet and the confined environment denies him distance. He is physically capable but not a legendary gunfighter; his advantages are preparation, authority, and the willingness to proceed while others hesitate. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships Creed’s allegiance is to Helix because the company has supplied the only durable structure in his life. He knows individual orders may be cynical, but he believes institutions are preferable to improvised morality. He also depends on Helix materially: his pension is near vesting, and failure could cost him the security he has spent decades earning. He wants to recover or destroy the organism, contain Sanaa’s files, and compel Dry River back into compliance. At a less acknowledged level, he wants Mara to submit to his judgment. He once cared for Mara as a protégé and possible successor. He still notices whether she is eating properly and whether her old hand injury has worsened. These observations do not soften his pursuit; they intensify his sense that he retains a claim on her choices. Creed regards Eli as an unruly worker archetype and repeatedly underestimates him. He considers Sanaa a frightened technician who can be pressured into cooperation. Tessa makes him uncomfortable because she remembers a version of him that official records have sanitized. He does not hate the dust calf. He scarcely sees it as an individual. To him it is an unstable biological asset around which irresponsible people have built a crisis. ## Voice Creed speaks with measured courtesy, but irritation appears in repeated names, unnecessary reminders, and small corrections. His thoughts are less coherent than his speech; he revisits old grievances and then reframes them as professional concerns. > “Mara, I am offering you a pump, legal clearance, and the opportunity to stop making this expensive.” > > “You always did know how to dress a threat.” > > “And you always preferred one you could complain about.” > She had left him with the inquiry, the reports, and three executives suddenly unable to remember approving anything. Now she would call this conscience. Mara had always liked a word better when it arrived late. > “I don’t need you to trust Helix. Trust the pressure gauge. Trust the failing filters. Those are not political.”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1 — The Noise The salvage came to twelve thousand credits if Mara lied about the filters. Nine if the buyer tested them. Six after fuel, docking fees, and Eli’s talent for turning minor repairs into open surgery. That left nothing for the coolant pump, which had begun knocking whenever the *Wayward Sun* climbed past half thrust. Mara drew a line beneath the figures. The paper jumped as the ship cleared the research depot’s gravity. “Still doing that,” Eli said from the cockpit hatch. “The arithmetic?” “The pump.” “I know.” “Good. I’d hate to think it was suffering unnoticed.” Eli vanished before she could assign him something unpleasant. Mara added one final expense to the manifest: replacement cargo webbing. The old straps had been certified for another two years, but certification meant only that Helix had accepted a fee to predict the future. Behind her, Canaan’s Reach filled the cockpit glass: a red-brown world wrapped in thin cloud, with the abandoned research depot dwindling against its nightside. Helix had evacuated in a hurry. Cabinets left locked. Meals left in the galley heater. Nine crates of machine parts sitting in an unsecured storage bay as though waiting for an independent hauler with flexible ideas about ownership. Sanaa had called it suspicious. Mara had called it solvent. The intercom clicked. “Captain,” Eli said. Static chewed the next breath. “Something’s... moving in that crate we picked up.” Mara looked at the nine entries on her manifest. “Which crate?” “The long one.” “That describes five.” “The long one I said we shouldn’t take.” “You said that about all of them.” “I was right more broadly than anticipated.” Mara set down her pencil. The pump knocked again through the deck, three quick blows followed by a pause. She had owned the *Wayward Sun* long enough to know all its complaints. This sound was not among them. “Define moving.” “Changing neighborhoods.” The cargo bay smelled of hot dust and the bitter sealant Eli used on everything, including equipment not designed to be sealed. Crates crowded the center deck beneath crossed webbing. Most bore faded Helix inventory marks. The largest had been shoved against the port bulkhead during acceleration. Eli stood six paces from it, holding a drill. Mara stopped in the hatch. “Why do you have that?” “Same reason you brought the pistol.” She looked down. Her right hand had found the coil-pistol at her hip without consulting her. Sanaa waited beside the emergency controls with a medical case open at her feet. She had tied her gray-threaded hair back and removed her spectacles, which usually meant she wanted time to invent a careful answer. The crate scratched. Not the dull slide of loose cargo. A narrow, deliberate rasp came from inside the wall nearest Eli. It stopped. Eli shifted his weight. The scratching resumed opposite him. Mara studied the crate. Two meters long, one high. Matte composite shell. No vents. The cargo label read CERAMIC FILTER ASSEMBLIES. Its mass, according to the depot manifest, was one hundred eighty kilos. “Did it move before we loaded it?” “No.” “Did you scan it?” “Yes.” “With a scanner?” Eli looked injured. “Mostly.” Mara saw three small holes in the crate’s upper panel. She pointed. “Those.” “Ventilation.” “You drilled air holes into unknown cargo.” “Only three.” “I can see the number, Rook. I’m questioning the philosophy.” Something struck the wall from within. Eli stepped back. Sanaa’s hand closed around the emergency decompression lever. Mara said, “Don’t.” “If the container ruptures—” “We are not venting twelve thousand credits.” “Nine if they test the filters,” Eli said. Mara looked at him. He lowered the drill. She circled the crate. Someone had scraped away its original markings, but not thoroughly. Beneath the shipping label, a ghost of black lettering showed through: three interlocking hexagons and a specimen code. Sanaa went still. It was not dramatic. She merely stopped polishing her spectacles. “You know that code,” Mara said. “No.” “You know what kind of code.” Sanaa put her spectacles on. “Biological storage.” Eli glanced at the unventilated shell. “Then my philosophy improves by the second.” Mara touched the crate. A faint vibration traveled into her fingertips. Not an engine resonance. It changed when she applied pressure, gathering beneath her palm like something listening from the other side. She withdrew her hand. “How long can biological storage remain sealed?” she asked. “It depends what is stored.” “Useful.” “Specific questions produce specific answers.” “What’s in the crate?” “I don’t know.” The wording was too clean. Mara filed it away. A recessed latch sat beneath the false label. Mara wiped dust from it. No indicator lights. No pressure warning. “Could be infected,” Sanaa said. “Could be dying,” Eli said. “Those are not mutually exclusive.” The crate scratched twice. Eli, without thinking, tapped the drill handle twice against his thigh. A pause. Then two knocks answered from inside. No one spoke for several seconds. Mara unfastened the retaining strap across the lid. “Sanaa, seal the inner hatch. Eli, get behind me.” “I can open it.” “You already opened it three times.” “Those are holes.” “Behind me.” He obeyed, muttering something about imprecise terminology. Mara drew her pistol and set it to its lowest charge. Shooting inside a pressurized ship was bad practice. Shooting unknown biology was worse. Leaving it sealed had become impossible. Sanaa closed the hatch to the corridor. “If I say shut it, you shut it.” “If it has teeth,” Eli said, “we’ll assume that was implied.” Mara released the first latch. Nothing happened. The second had been welded shut. Eli handed her a cutter over her shoulder. She severed the weld, careful not to heat the shell. The lid lifted a finger’s width. A smell seeped through—not rot, nor antiseptic. Hot stone after rain. Something pale flashed in the gap. A jointed limb unfolded with appalling speed and caught Eli’s metal probe from his hand. He yelped. The limb vanished, dragging the probe inside. The crate erupted in scraping. “Shut it,” Sanaa said. Mara reached for the lid, but a translucent claw punched through the inner lining. It tore out a bright ribbon of conductive mesh and pulled it into the darkness. Eli crouched to peer through the gap. “Don’t,” Mara said. “Can’t see its head.” “That is not an invitation.” A second limb appeared. Then part of a body pressed against the opening: dull glassy plates, fine gray filaments trembling between them. No recognizable head. No eyes Mara could find. Eli extended one gloved hand. The creature struck. Its jaws—or something serving the purpose—closed through the glove between his thumb and wrist. Eli shouted and fell backward. Mara shoved the lid down. The crate hammered once beneath her hands, then went still. Blood ran from Eli’s torn glove. Sanaa dragged him away and cut the material from his wrist. Four punctures welled around old white restraint scars. “Medical bay,” Mara said. “It took my probe,” Eli said through his teeth. “Then you’ve both learned about theft.” Sanaa cleaned the wound. Her hands remained steady, but all the color had left her face. Mara watched her. “You said you didn’t know what was inside.” “I didn’t.” “Yet.” Sanaa pressed a sealant patch over the bite and stared at the closed crate. From within came the soft sound of metal being methodically stripped apart. “I thought they were all dead,” she said.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. **Characterization and Consistency** The chapter establishes Mara with precision. Her financial calculations, her dry humor, her hand finding the pistol without conscious thought, her filing away Sanaa's too-clean denial—all of these are consistent with the profile of a woman who processes emotion through logistics and observation. The detail of her checking the number of air holes and "questioning the philosophy" rather than the count is exactly right for her character. Eli's impulsiveness (drilling holes, extending his hand despite warnings, being more offended that the creature took his probe than that it bit him) is pitch-perfect. Sanaa's stillness when she sees the specimen code, her removal of spectacles, her too-clean denial, and the devastating final line are all consistent with her profile as someone who withholds information while managing how she's perceived. **Dialogue** The dialogue is genuinely good—economical, character-specific, and often funny without being forced. "Changing neighborhoods" is an excellent response to "define moving." The exchange about the air holes ("Only three." / "I can see the number, Rook. I'm questioning the philosophy.") is sharp. The banter has rhythm and reveals character simultaneously. Eli's "I was right more broadly than anticipated" is the kind of line that feels earned rather than performed. The dialogue avoids the common failure of characters explaining things to each other for the reader's benefit. **Prose Quality** The prose is clean and controlled. Concrete nouns dominate. The description of the creature—"dull glassy plates, fine gray filaments trembling between them. No recognizable head. No eyes Mara could find"—is appropriately alien without being overwrought. The smell "Hot stone after rain" is evocative and restrained. The chapter avoids purple prose almost entirely. **Pacing and Plot** The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the discovery, the air holes already drilled, the scratching that responds to Eli's position, Sanaa's recognition of the specimen code, the partial opening, the bite, and the final line. The plan's beats are all hit without feeling mechanical. The creature's behavior—stealing the probe, stripping conductive mesh, biting through the glove—establishes it as genuinely alien rather than cute or symbolic. **Weaknesses** The chapter is very good but not quite masterful. A few observations: The pump-knocking motif (three quick blows, a pause) is introduced but the parallel to the scratching pattern isn't quite developed enough to feel like a deliberate structural choice rather than coincidence. The "nine if they test the filters" callback from Eli is a nice touch but slightly too neat—it risks feeling like a writer's trick rather than natural dialogue. The line "Shooting unknown biology was worse" is slightly clunky in its internal logic—it's not obviously worse than shooting inside a pressurized ship, and the reasoning feels compressed. The chapter is perhaps slightly too comfortable in its wit. The humor is well-executed, but the consistent banter occasionally smooths over what should be more jagged tension. The crew feels slightly too composed for people who have just discovered unknown living cargo. The final line—"I thought they were all dead"—is excellent: it delivers exposition while revealing character and raising stakes without over-explaining. This is the chapter's strongest moment. **Creativity** The chapter avoids the obvious beats of this genre. The creature is not immediately threatening in a monster-movie way; it's methodical, curious about conductivity, and responsive to position. The financial framing at the opening grounds the space-western setting without leaning on clichés. The decision to have Eli already have drilled the holes before reporting is a smart character choice that the plan called for and the execution delivers. **Overall** This is a well-crafted, disciplined chapter that executes its plan faithfully, establishes characters with economy and precision, and avoids most of the pitfalls the writing plan identified. It falls short of masterpiece territory primarily because it plays it slightly safe—the wit is consistent but occasionally too smooth, and the tension could be more genuinely unsettling. But these are minor criticisms of a chapter that does most things right. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2 — Property Sanaa would not name it. “It doesn’t have a common name,” she said. “Then give me the uncommon one.” “I can give you a specimen designation.” Eli sat on the medical bay counter with his bitten hand immersed in a bowl of pink antiseptic. “That’s what you write on a jar.” Mara stood in the doorway, blocking the only exit. “Start with how dangerous it is.” “I don’t know.” “You’re using that answer often.” “It remains accurate.” Sanaa removed Eli’s hand from the bowl and examined the punctures. No swelling yet. The old white scars beneath them made the new wounds look arranged. “What I saw were tissue scans,” she said. “Environmental samples. Preliminary survey data from Canaan’s Reach. The native designation was E-seven-twelve.” “Dust calf,” Eli said. “It is neither associated exclusively with dust nor demonstrably juvenile.” “Six legs, lives in dirt, eats hardware. Livestock.” “It may be fully mature.” “Small livestock.” Mara said, “Helix listed them as extinct?” Sanaa taped a sensor around Eli’s wrist. “Not precisely.” Mara waited. “They listed Canaan’s Reach as possessing no complex indigenous life.” “There’s a complex indigenous life stripping my cargo insulation.” “Yes.” “And you used to work for the people who missed it.” Sanaa tightened the sensor unnecessarily. Eli winced. The bridge alarm chimed with the pleasant discretion of a hotel clerk. “Priority transmission,” the ship announced. “Helix Dominion cutter approaching on intercept. Estimated weapons range: eleven minutes.” Mara closed her eyes. Eli said, “That’ll be about the livestock.” The cutter appeared on the forward display as a hard white point rising from the planet. It had launched from the far side of the abandoned depot and burned at twice the *Wayward Sun*’s acceleration. Its authorization codes reached them before its voice did, ordering their navigation system to prepare for inspection. Mara canceled the command. The comm light flashed again. “Put it through,” she said. A man appeared on-screen in a dark Helix uniform, seated beneath the cutter’s clean blue lights. Silas Creed had lost more hair since Mara had last seen him, but not his conviction that a uniform collar should never touch a human neck. His gloves lay aligned beside the console, finger to finger. “Captain Vance,” he said. “Or is it still Captain?” “Still Marshal?” “Senior Marshal.” “My condolences.” His mouth almost moved. “You’re aboard the HS-441 *Larkspur*.” “Registry says *Wayward Sun*.” “Paint says that.” Behind Mara, Eli whispered, “I like him already.” Creed’s gaze shifted fractionally. He had always been good at locating people from the direction of their breathing. “You removed materials from a secured Helix facility,” he said. “One item was misdirected biological property. Return it, and we can treat the rest as a misunderstanding.” “Misdirected into a sealed crate?” “The facility was under evacuation.” “What from?” A pause, small enough that anyone who had not worked with him might have missed it. “Send your cargo manifest.” “No.” “Mara.” There it was. Not her rank. Worse. Something crashed in the ventilation shaft above the cockpit. The lights dimmed and recovered. Creed heard it. “The specimen is loose?” “Your property seems unclear on the arrangement.” “It may be carrying an uncontained microbial culture. You need to isolate it before it reaches your water recyclers.” Mara looked toward Sanaa. Sanaa did not shake her head. Creed continued, “Prepare to receive a docking collar. I’ll replace your failing coolant assembly, compensate you for the salvage, and cover any medical treatment your crew requires.” The pump knocked under the deck as though summoned. It was a good offer. That was the ugly part. Bad offers let a person keep a clean opinion of herself. “How much compensation?” Mara asked. Eli looked at her sharply. Creed named a figure higher than the salvage was worth even if Mara lied well. The sensor on Eli’s wrist began chirping. Not the sensor, Mara realized. Something inside the walls had matched its frequency. A vibration passed through the cockpit floor. For an instant she tasted metal. Heat pressed against her back. Her limbs—too many, bent wrong—were pinned beneath white light. Antiseptic flooded the air. Something tightened around her body whenever a voice spoke. Then the cockpit returned. Mara’s hand had locked around her scarred palm. Eli was crouched against the medical counter, his face bloodless. Sanaa had dropped her spectacles. The voice still came through the comm. Creed’s voice. The wall beside Eli buckled outward. “Kill the transmission,” Sanaa said. Mara cut the channel. The vibration ceased, leaving her heart racing without permission. A translucent claw tore through the ventilation grille. The creature spilled after it in a confusion of narrow limbs and dull plates. It was smaller than the crate had suggested, no larger than a heavy dog, though its six legs let it spread across half the wall. Gray filaments shivered along its flanks. Eli moved toward it. “Don’t,” Mara and Sanaa said together. The dust calf ripped a cable from the open panel. Sparks snapped across its plates without burning them. It folded the cable beneath its body and fled into the maintenance duct. The cockpit went dark. Emergency lights came on red. The ship announced, “A minor electrical irregularity has been detected.” Eli imitated its gentle tone. “A minor electrical irregularity has eaten navigation.” Mara crossed to the manual board. “Can you get it out?” “Eventually.” “Before it gets into water recycling?” “Less eventually.” The comm flashed again. Creed’s text demand appeared on the backup display: PREPARE FOR DOCKING. RESTRAIN VALE. SECURE HER IN AN ISOLATED COMPARTMENT. Sanaa read it over Mara’s shoulder. “Isolated compartment,” Eli said. “That sounds nicer than airlock.” Mara reopened the channel as audio only. “Your offer includes my whole crew.” “My authority has limits,” Creed said. “Then so does your offer.” “Vale stole a controlled organism and proprietary records. She will be detained.” “For how long?” “That depends on her cooperation.” Sanaa retrieved her spectacles from the floor. “Captain, the contamination risk may be real.” “Helpful timing.” “I won’t let Helix take it.” “You put it on my ship?” Sanaa said nothing. The cutter’s docking instructions forced themselves onto the display. Distance: four minutes. Creed was already closing. “Mara,” he said, “you do not know what she brought aboard.” “Neither do you.” “I know enough to keep it away from a settlement.” The coolant pump struck three times beneath them. Mara saw the figures from her manifest. Nine thousand if tested. Six after expenses. Nothing for the pump. Creed was offering all of it back, with interest. All she had to do was surrender Sanaa and a creature that had bitten Eli twice. “Eli,” she said, “secure the loose cargo.” He studied her for half a second, then ran. “Sanaa, find me the contamination protocol.” Creed exhaled. “Good.” Mara cut the comm and shoved the thrust controls forward. The *Wayward Sun* lurched toward the planet. In the cargo bay, nine inadequately secured crates tore free at once. The impact rolled through the hull like thunder. One by one, the manifest tags winked out. Twelve thousand credits became scrap. The cutter alarm began to howl. Mara strapped herself in as red atmosphere swelled across the glass. “Good,” Creed’s voice repeated from the severed channel, though he was no longer there.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 of this space western demonstrates considerable craft and largely succeeds at what it sets out to do. Let me examine it systematically. **Dialogue and Character Voice** The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. The opening exchange between Sanaa and Eli over naming the creature is economical and character-revealing: Sanaa's precision ("It is neither associated exclusively with dust nor demonstrably juvenile"), Eli's deflating practicality ("Small livestock"), and Mara's impatient interrogation all feel distinct and consistent with their profiles. The Creed-Mara exchange crackles with history—"Or is it still Captain?" / "Still Marshal?" / "Senior Marshal." / "My condolences."—this is sharp, earned banter that reveals their relationship without exposition. The text demand "RESTRAIN VALE. SECURE HER IN AN ISOLATED COMPARTMENT" followed by Eli's "That sounds nicer than airlock" is a good example of humor arising from character and situation rather than being imposed. **Pacing and Structure** The chapter moves efficiently. It opens in the medical bay (aftermath of the bite), transitions to the Creed confrontation, then escalates through the creature's escape and the final decision. The plan called for Creed to offer a deal, reveal he wants Sanaa isolated, and prompt Mara to break contact and burn for the planet—all of which happens. The chapter follows its plan faithfully. **The Sensory Disturbance Sequence** The creature's vibration effect is handled well: "For an instant she tasted metal. Heat pressed against her back. Her limbs—too many, bent wrong—were pinned beneath white light." This is appropriately disorienting and fragmented, not a convenient vision. It's one of the chapter's best moments—showing the creature's distress without making it a psychic exposition device. **The Central Moral Moment** "It was a good offer. That was the ugly part. Bad offers let a person keep a clean opinion of herself." This is the chapter's thematic core, and it lands cleanly. It's not overwritten. Mara's near-acceptance is credible, and the reveal of Creed's demand about Sanaa is the right trigger for her refusal—not heroism but a practical limit on what she'll accept. **Weaknesses** The ship's automated voice ("A minor electrical irregularity has been detected") is a nice touch, and Eli's imitation of it is funny. However, the chapter occasionally leans on the ship's announcements as a structural crutch—they appear twice in quick succession and risk becoming a tic. The line "Good," Creed's voice repeated from the severed channel, though he was no longer there" is the chapter's most overtly literary moment. It's effective but slightly self-conscious—the kind of ending beat that draws attention to itself as a "closing image." It works, but it's the one place where the prose reaches for effect rather than earning it organically. Sanaa's admission "I won't let Helix take it" followed immediately by "You put it on my ship?" is a good confrontation, but Sanaa's line "The contamination risk may be real" / "Helpful timing" is slightly too neat—it feels like the author ensuring both sides of the argument are represented rather than a natural moment. The loss of twelve thousand credits in cargo is handled well—the consequence is immediate and concrete, and the plan specified this should happen. The chapter doesn't moralize about it. **Consistency with Character Profiles** Creed's vanity about his uniform, his measured courtesy, his use of the ship's registered name ("HS-441 Larkspur"), and his personal wound at Mara's departure are all present. Eli's humor under stress, his quick reading of the situation, and his deference to Mara's decision (studying her "for half a second") are consistent. Sanaa's precision, her delayed disclosure, and her physical tells (dropping spectacles) match her profile. Mara's internal calculation, her dry humor, and her decision made through practical limits rather than heroism are all faithful. **Overall Assessment** This is a well-executed chapter that does what it plans to do without overreaching. The prose is clean and controlled. The dialogue is the strongest element. The chapter avoids the common pitfalls of the genre—no purple space-western imagery, no ornamental metaphors, no convenient heroism. The ending is slightly too polished as a closing image, and there are minor moments where the seams show, but these are small complaints against a chapter that consistently delivers on its intentions. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3 — Bad Flying The *Wayward Sun* hit atmosphere sideways. A red sheet swallowed the cockpit glass. The hull groaned, first along the spine and then everywhere at once. Loose salvage battered the cargo bay in a series of expensive collisions. The ship said, “External temperature exceeds recommended limits.” “Recommendations noted,” Mara said. She fired the port thrusters. Nothing happened. “Eli.” His reply came through static and heavy breathing. “Busy.” “With the thruster?” “With the six-legged electrical fault in the wall.” “Do you have to call it that now?” “It’s growing on me.” A warning spread across Mara’s console. The port thruster had lost its control circuit. The *Wayward Sun* rolled deeper, presenting its flank to the atmosphere. Behind them, Creed’s cutter entered cleanly, nose aligned, heat shield bright but balanced. Its narrow silhouette held steady in the turbulence. He had always believed good flying meant refusing to frighten a ship. Mara preferred to learn what it would tolerate. She fired the starboard thruster alone. The freighter rolled harder. At the edge of inversion, she cut thrust and let the air catch the ship’s broad belly. The hull slammed level. A restraining strap snapped somewhere aft. “Cargo integrity compromised,” the ship announced. “That happened earlier,” Mara said. “Multiple cargo containers are no longer responding.” “Neither is the port thruster.” “The port thruster is reporting a control interruption.” “Nice that you two are speaking.” Creed’s cutter fired a warning shot. Blue light passed close enough to turn the red cloud white. The comm activated without permission. “Mara, level out.” “I am level.” “Relative to what?” The channel dissolved into static as she cut it. She checked the heat map. The engines were red, the maneuvering bank orange, the cargo deck climbing toward unsafe. The coolant reserve could serve one system properly, perhaps two badly. Sanaa appeared in the cockpit hatch with one hand braced overhead. “The outer deck is at forty-five degrees.” “Temperature or angle?” “Yes.” “Where’s Eli?” “In the maintenance trunk. He declined medical advice.” “He does that.” “He’s bleeding through the patch.” “Also that.” The heat warning sharpened. Mara routed coolant toward the engines. A second alert appeared: CARGO HABITAT TEMPERATURE CRITICAL. The crate had no habitat controls, but Sanaa had tied its monitor into the cargo sensors. “If that deck gets hotter—” she began. “The engines fail, we all get hotter.” “The organism may not survive.” “The organism is eating my ship.” Sanaa gripped the back of Mara’s chair as turbulence hit. “That does not alter its temperature tolerance.” The screen split the coolant flow into neat blue bars. Mara moved half toward the cargo deck. Engine temperature continued to climb. “Tell me when it reaches fifty,” she said. “It is at fifty-one.” “Then you’re late.” In the maintenance trunk, Eli crawled on one elbow because his injured hand no longer closed properly. The trunk was built for cables, not people. Bundles pressed against his shoulders, warm and vibrating. Ahead, something clicked softly. “I can hear you,” he said. The clicking stopped. “Which isn’t a threat. Just information.” A gray filament withdrew around a bend. Eli edged after it. “I’m going to need that cable back.” The creature had wedged itself around a junction housing, all six limbs clamped to the metal. It had no face he could understand. A seam opened between two plates when it breathed. The stolen cable lay coiled beneath its belly. Eli lowered his voice. “There you are.” Its filaments settled. That meant calm, he decided. He reached for the cable. The creature snapped at him. Its hard mouth closed just short of his fingertips, and Eli struck his head on the trunk ceiling. “Right. Still negotiating.” It was not watching him. Its body remained pressed to the junction, plates aligned with the housing’s faint electrical hum. Warmth, he thought. It had cornered itself in the warmest part of the ship. Not trust. Plumbing. The junction flashed. A wave of heat moved along the trunk. The creature convulsed, tearing the housing loose. The port thruster came alive. In the cockpit, the *Wayward Sun* lurched violently left. Mara caught the spin before it became a tumble. “Eli!” “I may have found the interruption.” “You released an unknown animal into my control conduits.” “Yes.” “It disabled steering.” “I have developed a critique of my earlier decision.” A clang sounded through his channel. “Did it bite you again?” Mara asked. “No.” A pause. “Not successfully.” The creature scrambled deeper into the trunk, trailing the control cable. Sanaa closed her eyes. “I put it aboard.” Mara kept both hands on the controls. “This is poor timing for a confession.” “At the depot. I found it in a containment room during the evacuation. Security was returning. Your cargo hold was open.” “You told me to leave that crate.” “I wanted you to leave before they searched it.” “You could have told me.” “I could have.” “Beacon?” “I didn’t know it had an implant.” “You didn’t scan it?” “I had six minutes and a specimen that was attempting to remove my fingers.” Mara gave her a look. Sanaa glanced toward the maintenance trunk. “It was more successful with Eli.” Creed’s cutter fired again. This time the shot clipped their wake. The *Wayward Sun* shuddered, and the engine heat surged past tolerance. The planet’s cloud layer broke below them. Dry plains stretched to the horizon, divided by black canyons and the pale scars of extraction roads. The navigation display offered one reachable settlement. DRY RIVER POPULATION: 814 HELIX SERVICE CLASS: DEPENDENT Mara’s hands tightened on the controls. Sanaa saw the name. “You know it.” “I know the landing trench.” “We need compatible parts.” “I know.” The jump-drive coolant reservoir blinked beside her hand. Empty it and there would be no leaving the system without a complete refit. Keep it, and the engines would seize before they reached the ground. Creed hailed them again. “Your port drive is shedding material. Climb now and I can tow you.” Mara dumped the jump coolant into the engines. Blue flooded the heat map. The *Wayward Sun* surged forward, leaving a white vapor scar through the rust-colored air. The jump status changed from STANDBY to INOPERABLE. Creed fell behind, unwilling to drive his cutter as hard. “Bad flying,” his voice said through the comm. “Worked.” “It always does until it doesn’t.” Mara shut him out. Dry River’s landing trench appeared between two ridges, a narrow strip roofed in patched pressure fabric. Mara brought the freighter down beyond it on emergency thrusters. The port leg collapsed on contact. The hull struck dirt and plowed a hundred meters before stopping at a sick angle. Silence followed. Then the ship said, “Landing complete.” Mara unfastened her harness. “Eli?” “Alive.” “Sanaa?” “Likewise.” The cargo bay was a wreck of broken crates, cracked filters, and ceramic dust. The long specimen crate sat against the wall with its lid open. Inside, the dust calf had curled around a length of stolen cable. Its plates rose and fell. It had returned there by itself. A fist struck the outer airlock. Mara checked the external camera. A tall, heavyset woman stood in the red dust with a tool case in one hand and a respirator over her face. A pale scar ran from her ear into her collar. Mara searched her memory and found a younger woman kneeling beside a seized pump, wrists bound, staring at Mara as if memorizing her. The outer speaker crackled. “Mara Vance,” Tessa Ward said. “You’ve got nerve landing here.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 of this space western demonstrates considerable craft and largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me examine it systematically. **Dialogue and Voice** The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. The exchanges between Mara and the ship's automated voice are genuinely funny without being forced: "Recommendations noted" and "Nice that you two are speaking" land cleanly. The Mara-Eli banter through the comm ("With the six-legged electrical fault in the wall" / "Do you have to call it that now?" / "It's growing on me") is sharp and character-consistent. The rhythm of short exchanges during crisis feels authentic rather than theatrical. Creed's brief intrusions are well-calibrated: "Bad flying." / "Worked." / "It always does until it doesn't." This is economical and reveals character without over-explaining. The chapter avoids the trap of giving Creed elegant speeches. Tessa's final line—"You've got nerve landing here"—is appropriately blunt and sets up the next chapter without melodrama. **Character Consistency** Mara's decision-making under pressure is consistent with her profile: she prefers physical solutions to emotional ones, she learns what the ship will tolerate rather than following recommendations, and she dumps the jump coolant without ceremony. The detail of her patting the bulkhead is absent here (appropriately—this is crisis, not aftermath), but her voice is right throughout. Eli's behavior in the maintenance trunk is well-rendered. His assumption that the creature's stillness means calm, then the correction ("Not trust. Plumbing."), is exactly the kind of self-aware impulsiveness his profile demands. His line "I have developed a critique of my earlier decision" is perfectly in character. Sanaa's confession is handled well. The timing is deliberately poor, and Mara's "This is poor timing for a confession" acknowledges it without letting Sanaa off the hook. The detail that she had six minutes and the creature was "attempting to remove my fingers" is appropriately unglamorous. **Plot Execution** The chapter follows its plan closely: the damaged ship overheating, Eli crawling after the creature, Sanaa's confession, the jump coolant sacrifice, the hard landing, and Tessa's appearance. The plan's note that "Mara uses coolant meant for the jump drive to survive reentry" and "the ship loses interstellar capability" is executed cleanly. The creature returning to its crate voluntarily is included as planned. The plan specified that Eli "discovers it has merely cornered itself inside the warmest electrical housing" and "bites him again when he tries to pull it free"—both present. The plan also noted "His premature release of the animal has worsened the ship's failures, and Mara says so plainly"—this is handled, though Mara's accusation is somewhat softened by the comic register. **Prose Quality** The prose is clean and concrete, as the plan intended. "A red sheet swallowed the cockpit glass" is vivid without being overwrought. "The hull groaned, first along the spine and then everywhere at once" is effective. The description of the creature—"A seam opened between two plates when it breathed"—is appropriately alien without being purple. The line "He had always believed good flying meant refusing to frighten a ship. Mara preferred to learn what it would tolerate" is the chapter's best piece of characterization-through-action. It's economical and revealing. Some moments are slightly less successful. "Blue flooded the heat map. The Wayward Sun surged forward, leaving a white vapor scar through the rust-colored air" is fine but the "vapor scar" image is a touch decorative for the chapter's otherwise spare register. "The pale scars of extraction roads" is similarly reaching slightly. **Weaknesses** The chapter's main weakness is that it occasionally relies on the comic register to deflect from emotional weight that the plan intended to be present. Mara's accusation that Eli's recklessness worsened the ship's failures is present but softened by the joke structure around it. The plan says "Mara says so plainly"—the chapter has her say it, but the surrounding banter cushions it. The creature's behavior in the trunk is well-observed, but the sensory disturbance element (the creature's low-frequency vibrations causing involuntary sensory impressions) is absent from this chapter. The plan doesn't specifically require it here, but its absence means the creature remains somewhat less strange than the overall plan envisions. The transition from Sanaa's confession to Creed's second shot is slightly abrupt—the emotional weight of the confession doesn't quite land before the action resumes. **Overall Assessment** This is a strong chapter that executes its plan faithfully, maintains consistent character voices, and delivers genuine wit without sacrificing tension. The prose is disciplined, the dialogue earns its humor, and the ending with Tessa is well-positioned. Minor issues with emotional deflection and one or two slightly decorative images don't significantly undermine the chapter's achievement. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4 — What Mara Bought The roof over Dry River had once covered a landing trench. Steel ribs held layers of pressure fabric above a lane barely wide enough for two cargo crawlers to pass. Homes, workshops, and market stalls had been cut into the trench walls. Nothing matched. Every door had been built for a different building first. Condensation ran down plastic gutters overhead. A sign warned: HELIX ATMOSPHERIC RECOVERY ASSET UNAUTHORIZED COLLECTION PROHIBITED Someone had painted a cup beneath it. Tessa Ward walked ahead without checking whether Mara followed. Her tool case struck her left leg each time she limped. Eli and Sanaa remained aboard with the dust calf and a ship full of alarms. “The port leg’s gone,” Mara said. “I saw.” “Coolant manifold cracked on descent.” “Heard that.” “We need a replacement.” Tessa stopped at a pressure door and held out her hand. Mara almost offered to shake it. “Respirator,” Tessa said. Mara gave it back. Tessa dropped it into an open sterilizer beside the door. “You’ve got engine coolant under your hull,” she said. “If it reaches our intake, I’ll tow your ship out with you in it.” “I can isolate the leak.” “Then why are you following me?” “You have parts.” “I have problems. Some may resemble parts from a distance.” A bell rang through the trench: three low notes. Conversations stopped. Residents set containers beneath ration taps built into the walls. The taps opened together, releasing narrow streams. Nobody hurried. They knew exactly how much time they had. Mara stepped around a boy holding two jugs. Tessa said, “You remember the bells?” “Yes.” “No, you remember the alarms. We added bells after.” Her workshop occupied an old cargo office. Shelves covered every wall, labeled LEAKS, HEAT, CORROSION, and LIES. The last held polished Helix casings split open to reveal ordinary valves and connectors. Tessa set down her case. “Manifold model?” “Bryant K-eight.” “Those crack if you look at them in atmosphere.” “I didn’t look at it.” Tessa’s eyes closed briefly. It might once have been laughter. She pulled a dented component from the HEAT shelf and pushed it at Mara. “Hold this.” Mara held it while Tessa measured the intake collar. The weight forced Mara’s injured hand open. Tessa noticed the scar and said nothing. “I should have come back sooner,” Mara said. “For a manifold?” “After the seizure.” Tessa scraped corrosion from the collar. “Turn it.” Mara turned it. “I told myself the arrests kept Helix from firing on the station.” “Did they?” “No.” “They didn’t fire.” “That isn’t what I asked.” Mara looked down at the component between them. “I’m sorry.” “Useful.” “I mean it.” “Then mean it while you hold that coupling. I need both hands.” A chime sounded from Tessa’s pocket. She pulled out a battered medical monitor. The display showed a pulse trace and a dosing schedule. Next delivery: HELIX CUTTER 6, PENDING. “Your patient?” Mara asked. “My brother.” The monitor chimed again, this time with a supply warning. Tessa silenced it. “How long does he have?” “With his current dose? A day before I start dividing what’s left.” Mara thought of Creed’s cutter descending behind them. Tessa reclaimed the component. “Your manifold isn’t here. I can adapt the west pump bypass, but that takes six hours and leaves eight hundred people with one backup.” “I can pay.” “With the filters you scattered across the desert?” Mara’s comm clicked. Eli breathed into it, then said, “Small development.” “Is anything burning?” “Not currently.” “Bleeding?” “Not newly.” Behind his voice came the scrape of metal. Mara and Tessa reached the *Wayward Sun* to find Sanaa crouched beside the open specimen crate. A strip of translucent scale lay on the deck. The crate was empty. “I secured the lid,” Sanaa said. Eli pointed to a jagged hole in the underside. “It disagreed with the definition.” The dust calf had gone through a maintenance drain opened by the crash. Its tracks crossed the leaked coolant, six narrow prints leading toward Dry River. Tessa looked at Mara. “What did you bring here?” Sanaa answered. “Possibly the reason your filters fail.” “That is not a thing. It’s an argument wearing a coat.” They followed the prints into the trench. Residents drew back as the creature passed beneath market tables and water pipes. It did not charge or bare teeth. It ignored food placed in its path. Its six legs moved with a stiff, sideways rhythm, translucent plates dulled by dust. Eli kept three meters behind it. “You’re limping,” Mara said. “It bit the same hand twice. My leg is being sympathetic.” “You believe it trusts you?” “I’m revising the terminology.” The dust calf stopped outside a shuttered pump room. It flattened itself against a floor plate corroded nearly black. Its gray filaments spread across the metal. A vibration passed up Mara’s boots. Her mouth filled with the taste of salt and pennies. Pressure moved around her body—not restraint this time, but depth. Cool darkness. A slow pulse repeated far below. The pipes overhead shuddered. Residents began shouting. Tessa shoved through them and put her palm against the nearest pipe. Her expression changed. “What?” Mara asked. “Flow.” “The ration taps are closed.” “I know what closed sounds like.” The creature hooked its claws beneath the floor plate. Eli started forward, thought better of it, then fetched a pry bar. Together, he and Tessa lifted the plate. Damp air rose from the cavity. Mineral crust coated an old pipe junction. Beneath it lay a mat of pale filaments, dry and brittle as dead roots. Eli crouched. “Water?” “Brackish pocket,” Tessa said. “We’ve known about it since construction. Too much arsenic, selenium, things with longer names. Our filters foul in hours.” Sanaa knelt beside the opening. She did not touch the filaments. “I’ve seen this,” she said. “In the lab?” Mara asked. “In samples Helix marked as contamination.” The dust calf extended one limb into the cavity. The dead mat contracted around its claw. Every light in the trench flickered. A new tone sounded overhead—not the ration bell. The public address system came alive. “Residents of Dry River,” Creed said. “A controlled biological specimen has been brought inside your pressure boundary. Return it to Helix custody immediately.” Tessa’s medical monitor chimed. DELIVERY SUSPENDED. She read the notice twice. Her face revealed nothing. Her thumb rested over her brother’s falling supply estimate. At her feet, the dust calf worried at the dead white filaments, trying to wake them.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 of this space western demonstrates considerable craft and largely fulfills its plan with intelligence and restraint. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is the chapter's most impressive element. The exchange between Mara and Tessa is economical and loaded with subtext. "For a manifold?" as a response to "I should have come back sooner" is exactly the kind of deflection that reveals character without announcing it. The apology sequence — "I'm sorry." / "Useful." / "I mean it." / "Then mean it while you hold that coupling. I need both hands." — is genuinely excellent: it refuses catharsis, keeps the emotional stakes alive, and characterizes Tessa perfectly as someone who converts sentiment into labor. This is the plan's stated intention executed with real skill. The setting is established efficiently. The Helix sign with the painted cup beneath it is a small, precise detail that does real work — it establishes resistance, humor, and the power dynamic without exposition. The shelf labeled LIES containing rebranded proprietary parts is similarly sharp. The ration bells versus alarms distinction ("We added bells after") is a quietly devastating line that implies years of adaptation to deprivation. The creature's behavior is handled well. It doesn't perform gratitude or symbolic meaning. It escapes through a hole it made itself, ignores food, and pursues its own agenda. The sensory disturbance — salt, pennies, pressure, cool darkness — is appropriately disorienting without being convenient exposition. The plan specified these should be "confused fragments" and the chapter delivers that. The chapter follows its plan closely: the manifold problem, Tessa's brother's medicine, the creature escaping and leading them to the floor plate, the discovery of the filament mat, and Creed's announcement suspending delivery. The final image — the creature worrying at dead filaments while Tessa's monitor shows DELIVERY SUSPENDED — is structurally clean and emotionally effective without being melodramatic. Character consistency is strong. Mara's apology is clumsy and self-serving in exactly the way her profile predicts. Tessa refuses the emotional transaction while accepting the labor. Eli's "Not newly" in response to "Bleeding?" is perfectly in character. Sanaa's correction of Tessa's dismissal of the creature ("Possibly the reason your filters fail") is precise and slightly reckless, consistent with her profile.
