The opening establishes that Elias has recently returned from the hospital after being pulled from a river. He tires easily, his ribs hurt from resuscitation, and Mara has moved into the spare room. Despite sleeping elsewhere, she repeatedly enters his bedroom at night. Their conversation is mundane and strained. Elias resents being treated like a patient; Mara resents his refusal to admit how unwell he is. She asks whether he remembers getting up earlier. He does not. She refuses to explain what she means. Elias notices that his house keys are missing from the bedside table. Mara says she put them somewhere safe because his doctor told him not to drive. He accuses her of searching his things. She reminds him that she found him outside two nights ago in bare feet. When Mara leaves to make tea, Elias finds a soft fabric cuff tucked between the mattress and bed frame. One end has been tied to the frame with a clumsy knot. It is not professional equipment; it is part of an old dressing-gown belt. The chapter ends quietly. Mara returns with the wrong mug—the chipped blue one she usually keeps for herself. Elias notices her hands shake hard enough to rattle the spoon, and for the first time wonders whether she is watching him because she expects him to do something. ## Chapter 2 — The Missing Hour The following morning, Elias tries to restore a normal routine. He showers, makes toast, and searches for his keys while Mara dozes at the kitchen table. Her exhaustion makes her irritable rather than mysteriously composed. Elias finds his keys in the pocket of Mara’s coat. He considers leaving but discovers a message on his phone sent to Mara at 3:17 a.m. from his own number: **ARE YOU AWAKE?** He has no memory of sending it, though the phone was beside him all night. He confronts Mara. She admits that he has been walking around during sleep and that she hid the keys after finding the front door open. Elias suggests parasomnia, medication, or a concussion. Mara agrees these are possible, but her agreement feels like appeasement. During the argument, Elias sees a yellowing bruise around Mara’s upper arm. She says she struck the door frame. His instinctive certainty that she is lying frightens him because it arrives before he has considered any evidence. Mara asks him what happened to the blue mug’s missing handle. Elias remembers dropping it during an argument years ago, but Mara says she broke it while laughing so hard she lost her grip. Elias can picture both versions. Neither feels false. He goes upstairs to dress and loses time. He returns to awareness at the closed spare-room door with his hand on the knob. The lower legs of his trousers are damp, although the bathroom floor and stairs are dry. Mara is awake on the other side of the door. She does not open it. She only says his name, slowly, until he steps away. ## Chapter 3 — What She Did Mara finally admits that she tied Elias to the bed once, three nights earlier. She woke to find him standing over her with one hand covering her mouth. His eyes were open, but he did not respond until she said his name. When he woke properly, he was back in his own bed and remembered nothing. Elias is appalled both by what she describes and by her decision to restrain him without telling him. He demands the full truth. Mara shows him where she has been sleeping: the spare-room door is braced at night with a dining chair, and a saucepan has been balanced on the handle as an improvised alarm. The shabby precautions make her fear more credible than polished occult preparations would. They have been improvised by someone exhausted and ashamed. Elias wants to call his doctor. Mara tells him she already did. The doctor advised reducing one medication, arranging an urgent neurological assessment, and calling emergency services if Elias became violent. Mara has not followed the last instruction because she is afraid he will be taken away. Elias points out that this means she has made herself his jailer instead. Their argument turns personal. Mara admits she no longer knows whether his anger is his own. Elias replies that she has treated every feeling he expresses as a symptom. Neither can answer the other. Elias catches an unexpected smell of pond weed on his own skin. He scrubs his hands, but the smell remains under one thumbnail. Wedged there is a tiny piece of green plant matter. That evening, Elias insists Mara sleep while he remains downstairs. She agrees only after placing her phone on a shelf to record him. The chapter ends not with a supernatural shock but with Elias looking into the camera and feeling humiliated by how badly he wants it to prove Mara wrong. ## Chapter 4 — The Video Elias watches the recording with Mara after another lapse. Most of it is uneventful: Elias sits on the sofa, drinks tea, flips through television channels, and occasionally looks toward the camera. The ordinariness makes the missing time worse, because he remembers none of it. Near the end, Mara briefly comes downstairs half-asleep, checks on him, and returns to bed. After she leaves, Elias remains motionless for almost seven minutes. Then he crosses the room and stands beneath the phone, mostly outside its frame. The audio records him whispering. The words are indistinct except for Mara’s name and the phrase “stop looking.” When she calls from upstairs, his posture changes at once. He appears confused and asks why he is standing. The footage does not prove possession. It could show a sleep disorder or dissociative episode. What unsettles Elias most is a small, intimate detail: while waiting beneath the camera, he worries the frayed edge of his wedding ring with his thumbnail. Elias has never had that habit. Mara’s late father did. Mara admits there have been other borrowed gestures: ways of standing, coughing, and touching objects that remind her of people she has known. She has not mentioned them because they sound absurd. Elias asks what she thinks happened in the river. Mara tells him what she said outside the resuscitation room: “I don’t care how. Just bring him back.” She knows it was not an invitation, but she cannot stop hearing it as one. Elias responds cruelly that perhaps she only regrets getting what she asked for. Mara leaves the room. Alone, Elias plays the clip again. Just before his recorded self whispers, the phone picks up the sound of someone taking a long, wet breath. The sound appears to come from directly behind the camera. ## Chapter 5 — The Other One Elias and Mara spend the evening trying to understand the episodes without pretending they possess firm answers. They write down what each remembers. Their accounts contradict one another in small ways. Mara explains why she watches him. During the first episode, saying his name seemed to stop him. During the second, he stopped as soon as she switched on the light and looked directly at him. She does not know whether being seen matters or whether her voice simply wakes him. “The other one” is only what she calls the behavior when she needs to distinguish it from Elias. Elias resists the phrase because it comforts Mara by dividing him into innocent and guilty parts. He cannot be sure the division is real. He remembers flashes from his lapses: cold water around his knees, Mara sleeping with one hand beneath her cheek, and a powerful need to make her look away. He does not know whether these are memories, dreams, or fantasies created after hearing her account. They eat a poor dinner of burnt soup and toast. The ordinary task briefly eases the tension. Mara apologizes for restraining him; Elias apologizes for his cruelty, though neither apology resolves anything. Mara asks whether he wants his wedding ring removed. His swollen finger has made it painful, but he refuses. The argument exposes the real fear beneath their questions: if Elias is not himself, what belongs to him? His name, ring, house, and marriage cannot be settled by a memory test. Mara finally falls asleep at the kitchen table for several seconds. Elias experiences a blank. He returns standing beside her chair, his fingers wet and resting lightly against her eyelids. She wakes and says his name. He recoils before she finishes it, uncertain whether her voice stopped him or whether he stopped himself. ## Chapter 6 — Terms After the kitchen incident, Mara wants to call an ambulance. Elias refuses, fearing sedation will produce a longer lapse and that his violence will be treated only as criminal or psychiatric. Mara tells him fear is not a plan. They negotiate practical terms for surviving until morning, when Elias’s sister can drive him to the hospital. Mara will sleep in the bedroom with the door locked. Elias will remain in the hallway with one wrist secured to the radiator using the dressing-gown belt and a bicycle lock. His phone will record him. A kitchen timer will sound every five minutes to keep him awake. The arrangement is degrading and visibly inadequate. Elias struggles with the lock because his hands shake. Mara wants to help, then hesitates to come within reach. That hesitation hurts more than the restraint. Before she goes into the bedroom, Mara tells Elias she does not know what came back from the river. She also admits that part of her has been using that uncertainty to avoid another possibility: that Elias is still Elias and may never forgive her for being afraid of him. Elias does not offer easy reassurance. He says he understands why she is afraid but does not forgive the lies yet. The honesty gives the exchange more weight than reconciliation would. Mara lies down but keeps speaking through the partly open door. They discuss ordinary memories to stay awake: a disastrous holiday, a neighbor they disliked, the blue mug. Elias remembers Mara breaking it from laughter, then realizes he also remembers dropping it during a later argument. Both memories are true; the handle cracked once and came off years afterward. The earlier confusion was ordinary, not supernatural. The discovery comforts Elias briefly. Then the timer fails to ring. He sees that he has already turned it off, though he cannot remember doing so. ## Chapter 7 — Five Minutes Mara has reached the point where she cannot remain awake. Elias tells her to close and lock the door. She does, but the cheap interior lock could be forced easily. Elias tightens the restraint around his wrist and places the key inside the bedroom, beyond his reach. The chapter follows the next five minutes closely. Elias fights sleep through pain, counting, and conversation through the door. Mara’s answers become slower and eventually stop. The presence arrives not as a voice but as relief. Elias’s fear and pain recede. He becomes acutely aware of Mara breathing on the other side of the door. He experiences an urge to see her eyes and an equally strong conviction that if she looks at him, he will disappear. His body begins working the belt against the radiator. Elias alternates between resisting and assisting without being able to identify which impulses are his. He tears the skin around his thumb trying to slip his hand free. He reaches the bedroom door. The restraint has stretched enough to let his fingers touch the handle but not turn it fully. From inside, Mara murmurs his name in her sleep. Elias freezes. He remembers her at the hospital, though he should not have been conscious: crouched by a vending machine, saying she did not care how he came back. The memory may belong to him, to something that overheard her, or to no one. Rather than struggle toward the bedroom, Elias deliberately pulls backward until the restraint dislocates his thumb. He uses the pain to remain awake and loops the loose end around his neck—not to kill himself, but to make any further movement toward Mara immediately dangerous. It is a frightened, extreme, morally troubling choice rather than a heroic ritual. The door remains shut. Mara sleeps. Elias watches the thin line of darkness beneath it until dawn begins to lighten the hallway. ## Chapter 8 — Morning Mara wakes and finds Elias sitting against the radiator, his injured hand wrapped in the torn belt. He is staring at the bedroom door. For several seconds, she does not know whether she is safe. She unlocks the door but does not approach him immediately. Elias tells her where the key is and asks her to call his sister and the hospital. He does not claim the episode is over or that he defeated anything. They watch the phone recording together. The picture shows Elias struggling at the end of the hall, but the sound is poor. At one point he appears to speak directly toward the bedroom. Neither can make out the words. Mara thinks he said, “Wake up.” Elias thinks he said, “Look at me.” The ambiguity cannot be resolved. Mara tends his hand while waiting for help. Her care is competent but not tender at first. Elias asks whether she believes he chose to stop. She says she believes he is the one asking. It is not proof, but it is what she can honestly give him. Elias realizes that surviving the night has not restored trust. He may be hospitalized, and Mara may be questioned about restraining him. Their marriage may not survive even if he does. The story does not evade these consequences. Exhaustion finally overtakes Elias while they wait. He closes his eyes but does not fully sleep. When he opens them a fraction, he catches Mara staring at him again. She believes he is asleep. This time she is not seated like a supernatural sentinel. She is on the floor opposite him, holding the phone in one hand and watching his injured hand with the other. She looks frightened, angry, and desperately tired. Elias considers telling her he is awake. Instead, he remains still. After a moment, Mara deliberately closes her own eyes, as if testing whether he will move when she stops watching. Elias feels the impulse to reach for her. He keeps his hands where she can see them.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Elias Ward ## Background Elias is thirty-eight and works as an architectural technician for a small firm that specializes in alterations to public buildings: accessible entrances, school extensions, council offices, fire-safety upgrades. He is not the person who dreams up dramatic facades. He measures what is already there, notices where reality differs from the original plans, and finds ways to make imperfect structures usable. He takes quiet pride in being the person who catches the expensive mistake before construction begins. He grew up in a family that treated distress as a practical inconvenience. His father fixed household objects instead of apologizing; his mother expressed concern by making lists and leaving food in the fridge. Elias and his younger sister, Ruth, learned to convert emotion into sarcasm before anyone else could embarrass them by being sincere. Ruth is the one family member who can tell when Elias is frightened because his jokes become more pedantic. Elias met Mara eleven years ago at a friend’s birthday dinner. He remembers her correcting the waiter’s description of a supposedly rare plant printed on the menu. She remembers him sliding the sugar bowl toward her after noticing she had been turning it in circles. Both versions are true, though each considers a different detail to be the beginning. They married at a registry office after four years together. Their marriage is affectionate but not demonstrative. They communicate through routine: Elias warms Mara’s mug before pouring coffee; Mara leaves the hall light on if he is working late; each saves articles the other will complain about. This practical intimacy is precisely what the possession threatens. An intruder could learn anniversaries and addresses more easily than it could learn why Elias cuts sandwiches diagonally for Mara but never for himself. Before the accident, the marriage was under ordinary strain rather than on the verge of collapse. Elias had been working too much, partly because his firm was understaffed and partly because work allowed him to avoid decisions about moving house and whether they wanted children. Mara had begun calling his silence “the pause,” meaning the long interval in which he postponed answering until the question became too exhausting to ask again. Elias drowned six weeks before the story while walking beside the river after work. The exact circumstances remain mundane and unclear. The bank had been damaged by rain; he slipped while climbing down to retrieve a folder dropped by a cyclist. A passerby pulled him out and began CPR. Elias remembers the folder floating open, one page turning in the current, and then nothing reliable. He was clinically unresponsive long enough to sustain concern about neurological damage, though no one gives Mara or Elias a definitive timeline. His convalescence has stripped him of autonomy. He cannot drive, tires while climbing stairs, and has been told to rest without being told how to distinguish recovery from deterioration. He hates needing help with medication because it reminds him that every lapse can be interpreted as incapacity. His resistance to Mara’s supervision is partly reasonable and partly vanity. ## Personality Elias is observant, dryly funny, private, and more sensitive than he likes to appear. He prefers solvable problems and becomes irritable when frightened. He will spend twenty minutes repairing a drawer runner rather than admit that an argument has upset him. He has a strong sense of procedural fairness. Even when Mara’s fear is justified, he resents that she withheld evidence, moved his possessions, and restrained him without consent. He wants rules to apply consistently: if his memory makes him untrustworthy, Mara’s exhaustion should also make her judgment questionable. This insistence can be principled, but it is also how he avoids confronting the more urgent fact that he may be dangerous. He dislikes being watched while performing simple tasks. Even before the accident, he could not type naturally if someone stood behind him. Mara’s surveillance therefore feels like both supernatural containment and an exquisitely personal form of humiliation. Elias’s first instinct is to explain away fear. His second is to become angry with whoever made the explanation necessary. He can be cutting when cornered, especially with Mara because he knows precisely where her shame lies. His line about her regretting “getting what she asked for” is not accidental cruelty; he recognizes the wound and presses it. He is not naturally self-sacrificing. His eventual decision to restrain and injure himself is made through panic, necessity, and stubbornness. He wants to live. He wants to remain married. He also wants to be vindicated, even after vindication has become impossible. ## Idiosyncrasies - Elias straightens crooked frames in waiting rooms but rarely notices clutter on his own desk. - He reads instructions only after attempting something once and failing. - He cuts the labels from the collars of his shirts because he cannot tolerate them touching his neck. - He dislikes overfilled mugs and leaves the last mouthful of tea untouched because it is “mostly tannin and disappointment.” - He checks a locked door by pressing the handle down twice. After the drowning, he sometimes finds himself doing it four times. - He peels apples in one long strip. Mara finds this impressive despite having watched him do it for a decade. - He uses overly exact language when evasive. “I don’t specifically remember that” means he remembers enough to be worried. - He stores screws, washers, and picture hooks in separate jam jars. Mara routinely puts unrelated objects in them to annoy him. - He sleeps with one foot outside the duvet. - He rubs the frayed edge of his wedding ring after the accident. He believes the habit is new; Mara associates it with her late father, which unsettles both of them. - He calls Mara “Mars” only in unguarded moments. During most of the story, he uses her full name, a small measure of distance. - He hates wet sleeves. After the accident, he sometimes fails to notice when his cuffs are damp. ## Flaws and contradictions Elias values honesty but uses silence to control conversations. He rarely tells a direct lie; instead, he delays, qualifies, or answers the easier part of a question. He wants Mara to trust his account of himself while dismissing her account of what she has witnessed. His suspicion of her is partly a defense against the possibility that she is telling the truth. He can mistake autonomy for secrecy. Hiding his medication feels to him like reclaiming control, even though it makes both of them less safe. He believes intentions matter more than impact when defending himself, but more than intentions when judging Mara. He argues that he cannot be blamed for actions he does not remember, while holding her fully responsible for frightened decisions made without sleep. He is capable of tenderness but embarrassed by it. When Mara cries, he tends to offer water, tissues, or a practical solution. If none is appropriate, he may seem cold. ## Physicality Elias is tall but habitually slightly stooped, as though leaning toward a drawing board. He has narrow shoulders, long hands, dark hair beginning to thin at the temples, and a face that looks more severe in repose than he intends. A small pale scar crosses one eyebrow from a childhood fall. After the drowning, he has lost weight. His clothes hang incorrectly, his skin is sallow, and fading bruises from hospital needles remain inside his elbows. CPR left his ribs tender, so he rises from chairs carefully and disguises the pain by pausing to adjust his clothes. Before the accident, he moved economically. Now his body occasionally seems to complete an action before he has decided upon it: reaching for a handle, turning toward Mara’s breathing, touching his ring. These movements should remain slight enough to trouble rather than advertise possession. Elias’s expressions are restrained. Amusement appears at one corner of his mouth. Anger makes him still and precise rather than loud. When frightened, he presses his tongue against the back of his teeth and speaks too carefully. He interacts with rooms structurally. He notices hinges, sightlines, warped doors, the distance between the radiator and the bedroom handle. This professional habit becomes useful during the final restraint, but it should feel like a continuation of character rather than a convenient new skill. ## Motives, allegiances, and relationships Elias’s immediate motive is to regain control of his home, body, and account of reality. Beneath that is a more frightening desire: he wants Mara to recognize him as her husband without reservation. Her uncertainty injures him more deeply than her physical precautions. He is loyal to Mara, but his loyalty is entangled with ownership. He thinks of the house as theirs and the marriage as something he still possesses by continuity. He is slow to consider that Mara may have the right to withdraw from him even if he is the original Elias. He trusts Ruth more readily than doctors because she does not sentimentalize him. He has delayed calling her because involving her would make the situation real and expose the state of his marriage. Ruth will drive him to the hospital after the story, but she need not appear onstage. His relationship with his own body is adversarial after the drowning. He resents its weakness and begins to fear its competence. The more smoothly it acts during missing periods, the less certain he is that clumsiness would be reassuring. His relationship to the possible presence is not a conventional battle. He cannot reliably identify which impulses belong to it. At times, attributing an urge to “the other one” comforts him; at others, he suspects the phrase is a way of evading responsibility. ## Voice samples > “She said she’d moved the keys for my own good, which is a phrase people use when they’ve already decided the argument doesn’t require you.” > “I remembered the mug both ways. That was the trouble. Memory wasn’t a locked room with one person inside it. It was a drawer we’d both been throwing things into for years.” > “Don’t ask me whether I’m still me. Ask me whether I’m going to hurt you. At least that’s a question I might be able to answer.” > “I wanted her to sleep. I wanted her to trust me enough to sleep. Those were not, I had begun to understand, the same thing.” # Mara Ward ## Background Mara is thirty-six and works as an administrator at a community physiotherapy clinic. Her job is less passive than the title suggests. She manages referrals, rearranges appointments, translates clinicians’ vague intentions into instructions patients can follow, and calms people who are frightened by pain or bureaucracy. She knows how to sound composed while improvising. She grew up with her father, Colin, after her mother left when Mara was nine. Colin was affectionate, untidy, and unreliable about practical matters. He could repair an engine but forget to pay the electricity bill. Mara learned early to anticipate problems: pack spare batteries, check closing times, bring cash, write down medication. Her competence developed from anxiety, though she prefers to describe it as common sense. Colin died four years before the story after a stroke. During his final weeks, he sometimes mistook Mara for her mother or spoke to people who were not present. Mara found herself answering whichever version of reality kept him calm. She has never decided whether this was kindness or cowardice. Elias’s altered gestures—especially touching the edge of his ring like Colin—activate an old fear of loving someone whose recognition cannot be trusted. Mara’s relationship with Elias originally appealed to her because he seemed dependable without being controlling. He noticed practical things she had forgotten, while she made decisions he would otherwise postpone. Their habits fit together until the same traits hardened: her preparedness became supervision, and his deliberation became avoidance. Before the accident, Mara wanted them to discuss having a child or make a conscious decision not to. Elias kept saying they should revisit the question when work was quieter. Mara was less wounded by the possibility that he did not want children than by his refusal to state it. This remains unresolved, but it should appear only through small tensions rather than become a subplot. On the day of Elias’s accident, Mara received a call from an unknown number while buying groceries. At the hospital, she waited outside the resuscitation room and repeatedly asked staff what was happening. At some point she said, “I don’t care how. Just bring him back.” She later attached magical importance to the sentence, though she knows rationally that exhausted staff did not obey a supernatural demand. Since Elias came home, Mara has slept poorly. She initially assumed his behavior was medication-related. She moved to the spare room after waking beneath his hand, then began observing him at night after noticing that saying his name interrupted an episode. She has been making decisions in a state of worsening exhaustion and has concealed how frightened she is because she believes fear will provoke either Elias or whatever acts through him. ## Personality Mara is practical, alert, stubborn, and socially adept. She notices what people need before they ask, but she can become resentful when they fail to notice what that anticipation costs her. She dislikes uncertainty and responds by gathering information, creating routines, and controlling the environment. When control fails, she does not become theatrical; she becomes more specific. She asks what time, which door, how many tablets, where the keys are. Her precision can calm other people while disguising her own panic. Mara is capable of sustained care but not limitless patience. Sleep deprivation exposes a sharp temper. She may make tea for Elias, organize his appointments, and then call him selfish because he leaves the cup untouched. She is not naturally superstitious. Her belief that “something” may have returned with Elias embarrasses her. She tries medical explanations first and continues to hold them alongside the supernatural possibility. Calling it “the other one” is not certainty; it is a way to talk about the episodes without directly accusing Elias. She has a tendency to decide what another person can bear and then withhold information accordingly. She frames secrecy as protection. This is how she justifies hiding Elias’s keys, recording him, and failing to tell him about the first restraint. It is also a form of control she learned while caring for her father. Unlike Elias, Mara argues messily. She interrupts, abandons sentences, remembers an older grievance halfway through a current one, and sometimes contradicts herself. After an argument, she often thinks of the precise sentence she wanted while brushing her teeth. ## Idiosyncrasies - Mara drinks tea from the chipped blue mug because its missing handle forces her to hold it in both hands. - She circles appointments on paper calendars despite managing digital schedules for a living. - She carries plasters, painkillers, a pen, and folded shopping bags in every handbag but rarely carries cash. - She reads the ends of novels early if she becomes anxious about a character. - She talks to malfunctioning appliances as if disappointment will shame them into working. - She ties knots badly and compensates by adding more of them. - She cannot sleep with cupboard doors open. - She leaves teaspoons in mugs until the tea is finished, which Elias considers barbaric. - When angry, she cleans one small area with excessive concentration. - She repeatedly presses her thumbnail into the side of her index finger when trying not to cry. - She snorts when genuinely amused and dislikes that Elias can provoke it during arguments. - She calls Elias “Eli” in company but almost never in private. In private, “Elias” can sound affectionate, irritated, or like a command, depending on emphasis. - She saves voicemails from people she loves. She still has one from her father asking which bin goes out on Thursday. - She sleeps curled tightly, one hand beneath her cheek. Elias recognizes this posture even during his lapses. ## Flaws and contradictions Mara believes that anticipating someone’s needs gives her the right to make decisions for them. She can confuse caretaking with authority. She wants Elias to tell her the truth about experiences he may genuinely be unable to remember. When he cannot, she sometimes treats the absence as refusal. She resents Elias for making her his caretaker, even though she repeatedly prevents others from sharing the burden. She has delayed involving Ruth and minimizes the situation to the doctor because she wants control over what happens next. She recognizes that restraint and surveillance violate Elias’s dignity, yet she continues because she is afraid. Her fear is justified, but it does not make every method defensible. She wants a clean division between Elias and “the other one,” but she also suspects no such division exists. The label allows her to comfort him after an episode without confronting the possibility that the same consciousness may be present throughout. Mara can weaponize fragility. When Elias challenges her choices, she sometimes points to her exhaustion or bruises to end the argument rather than answer him. Her suffering is real, but she knows its persuasive power. She is frightened that part of her prefers the recovering Elias because he cannot retreat to work or defer difficult conversations. This shame complicates her care. ## Physicality Mara is of average height with a compact, sturdy build. She has thick brown hair cut just above her shoulders, usually clipped back carelessly while at home. Loose strands collect around her face as the night continues. Her skin flushes visibly when she is angry or embarrassed. She has broad, capable hands and short nails. A pale burn mark crosses the base of her left thumb from grabbing a baking tray years ago. Elias often notices her hands because they are usually doing something—stirring, sorting tablets, checking a lock, worrying a loose thread. Exhaustion alters her posture. Ordinarily she stands squarely and moves quickly. During the story she leans against door frames, misses the edge of chairs when sitting, and rubs her sternum as if trying to keep herself awake. Her eyes are red-rimmed, and concealer from the previous day has gathered beneath them. Mara’s face is expressive despite her attempts at control. Disbelief tightens her upper lip. Fear appears as intense concentration. When she lies, she provides one unnecessary detail and looks at the object she is discussing rather than the person. She does not move like a horror-film sentinel while watching Elias. She shifts, scratches, checks the time, massages her neck, and occasionally startles herself awake. Her physical discomfort should keep the surveillance human. When she says Elias’s name during an episode, her voice is low and firm—the tone she uses at the clinic with frightened patients. The fact that this may interrupt his altered state is unsettling precisely because it is an ordinary skill. ## Motives, allegiances, and relationships Mara’s immediate motive is to keep both herself and Elias alive until she can obtain help she trusts. She does not know what kind of help that is. Medical intervention may protect them but could also reduce Elias to symptoms or expose her own use of restraints. Her delay is driven by fear, guilt, and an exaggerated belief that she alone understands him. She wants Elias to be her husband, but she no longer knows what evidence could satisfy her. Correct memories are insufficient because his body carries gestures from elsewhere. Incorrect memories are also insufficient because ordinary couples remember their shared lives differently. She remains loyal to Elias as a person but is becoming less loyal to the marriage as an institution. If he poses a danger, she may leave even if he is blameless. This possibility frightens her because it contradicts her self-image as someone who does not abandon people. She likes Ruth and trusts her bluntness, but she has avoided calling her because Ruth will insist on hospital care and may accuse Mara of making the situation worse. Mara knows Ruth may be right. Her late father remains an emotional reference point. She loved him through cognitive changes but often resented being required to accept whichever version of him appeared. When Elias uses one of Colin’s gestures, Mara experiences grief before fear. Her relationship with the possible presence is hostile but not mythologized. She does not bargain with it or imagine that she understands its aims. What terrifies her is its attention: it appears to want her awake, looking, and frightened. She worries that calling it “the other one” may already grant it too much separateness. ## Voice samples > “I hid the keys because you opened the front door at two in the morning. I’m sorry I didn’t ask permission while you were doing it.” > “Don’t give me the calm voice, Elias. You only use the calm voice when you’ve decided I’m being unreasonable.” > “I don’t know what I believe. I know what I saw, and those are not always the same thing.” > “You keep asking whether I think you’re you. I’m trying to work out whether I can close my eyes in my own house.” # The Other One The presence should not be treated as a conventional third protagonist with a stable biography. Its lack of a clear profile is part of the story’s unease. Nevertheless, its dramatic function and behavioral character need definition so its appearances feel consistent rather than arbitrary. ## Possible nature The story will not settle whether the other one is: - An external consciousness that entered Elias while he was near death. - A residue or composite of people who died in the same water. - A damaged aspect of Elias formed by neurological trauma. - A dissociated survival mechanism onto which Mara and Elias project agency. - Something that did not enter Elias at all but emerged when his ordinary continuity broke. It should never provide a name, origin, or explanatory monologue. Any apparent memory it carries may belong to Elias, Mara, someone Mara once knew, or information gathered through observation. ## Behavioral character The other one is patient, physically curious, and drawn to Mara’s attention. It does not behave like a raging predator. It approaches slowly, touches eyelids, stands near doors, sends a short message, and waits beneath the phone camera. Its apparent desire is contradictory: it wants Mara to stop looking, yet also wants to force her to look. This should remain unresolved. Possible interpretations include: - Observation suppresses it, so it wants privacy. - Mara’s recognition gives it a more coherent identity. - It wishes to replace Elias in her perception, not merely control his body. - It wants to see itself reflected in her fear. - These impulses belong to Elias, not an entity: he wants freedom from surveillance and reassurance that she still recognizes him. The other one does not speak fluently. Its language is borrowed and fragmentary. It uses Mara’s name, short imperatives, and phrases recently spoken in the house. The phone message—“ARE YOU AWAKE?”—could be a threat, a practical question, or Elias seeking help during a lapse. It appears to reproduce physical habits. The wedding-ring gesture resembles Mara’s father, but the story will not confirm that the presence contains Colin. Elias may have absorbed the habit unconsciously after years of seeing Mara imitate it herself. Its strongest influence is experienced as **relief**. When it nears control, Elias’s pain, fear, and moral hesitation diminish. This makes it seductive without requiring persuasive dialogue. The danger is not only that it overpowers Elias, but that surrendering to it feels easier than remaining himself. ## Physical expression through Elias - His posture becomes less guarded because he no longer protects his injured ribs. - His movements become smooth and economical. - He stands too close without appearing agitated. - He touches objects as though testing their texture. - He looks at Mara’s eyelids rather than her eyes. - He fails to react to damp cuffs, cold floors, or pain. - He uses familiar gestures with no corresponding emotion. - When Mara says his name, he pauses as though listening from another room. The contrast should be subtle. No distorted voice, impossible contortions, black eyes, or theatrical transformation is needed. ## Motives and allegiance The other one has no confirmed allegiance beyond continuation. It appears to protect its access to Elias’s body and resist scrutiny. It may want Mara, but “want” should not be clearly defined as possession, murder, love, recognition, or escape. It should be possible to read Elias’s final restraint as: - Elias overcoming an invasive will. - Elias integrating and controlling a disowned part of himself. - The other one choosing not to harm Mara. - One composite consciousness reaching a decision that cannot be assigned to either side. This ambiguity prevents self-sacrifice from becoming a simple proof-of-humanity test. ## Voice fragments The other one’s verbal presence should remain rare: > *Are you awake?* > *Stop looking.* > *Mara.* Its more characteristic “voice” is a thought that arrives without grammar: > The clean relief of not needing to explain. > Her breathing behind the door; the certainty that her closed eyes could be opened. > A conviction, calm and complete, that the hand already knew what to do.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter One ## Watching I caught her staring at me again. She thought I’d been sleeping. Mara sat in the chair beside the bed, still wearing yesterday’s clothes. There was enough light from the landing to show me the shine on her lower lip and the hollows beneath her eyes. She had both hands around her left knee. Her face was very close to mine. “What are you doing?” She flinched hard enough to knock the chair against the wall. “Checking your breathing.” “You could’ve done that from over there.” “I was over there.” She stood and tugged her blouse straight. It was creased across the stomach, and one side had come untucked. Mara was normally incapable of leaving a cupboard door open. Now she looked as though she had slept in a railway station. I pushed myself onto my elbows. Pain tightened around my ribs. “Don’t,” she said. “I’m sitting up.” “I can see that.” “Then why tell me not to?” She pressed her thumbnail into the side of her finger. “You were making that noise again.” “What noise?” “Like you were choking.” “I wasn’t.” “You wouldn’t know.” That was how most of our conversations went since the hospital. Mara would tell me something I had done while asleep or medicated, and I would have no evidence with which to contradict her. She never raised her voice. She didn’t need to. Every disagreement came with the quiet advantage of a witness. She picked up the glass on my bedside table, sniffed it for some reason, and held it out. “Have some water.” “I’m not thirsty.” “You should drink.” “I’ve done nothing but drink water for six weeks.” “Five.” “What?” “It’s been five weeks.” I took the glass because it was easier. The water had warmed overnight and tasted faintly of the tablets dissolving on my tongue. Mara watched my throat as I swallowed. The doctors had warned us recovery would be uneven. Fatigue, confusion, headaches, poor concentration. They had given Mara a printed sheet with a list of reasons to call the hospital, and she had read it so often the folds had begun to split. I had read it once. Most of the symptoms were also symptoms of being alive on a bad day. “Did you take the evening dose?” she asked. “You handed it to me.” “That isn’t what I asked.” “Yes, Mara.” She reached for the blister pack and counted the empty spaces. I set the glass down harder than I meant to. “Could you stop inventorying me?” “I’m counting tablets.” “That’s what I mean.” She gave me the look she used when patients at the clinic blamed her for the waiting list. Calm, attentive, already tired of the conversation. “Do you remember getting up?” “To use the toilet?” “After that.” “No.” “You’re sure?” “I’d remember getting out of bed. It feels like being kicked in the chest.” Her attention went to my ribs. The pity was worse than suspicion. “What did I supposedly do?” She picked a loose thread from the cuff of her blouse. “Nothing.” “You came in here at three in the morning to stare at me because I did nothing?” “I heard you.” “Choking.” “Yes.” “But I wasn’t choking when you were sitting there.” “No.” “How long were you watching?” “I don’t know.” “You must have some idea.” “I didn’t time it, Elias.” Her use of my full name put something cold between us. She had called me Eli in front of nurses because they liked first names and soft voices. At home, before the river, she usually called me nothing at all. A marriage did away with names. There was only Have you seen my charger? and You left the oven on. I reached for my phone. The charging cable was there, curled beside the lamp, but the phone was gone. “Where is it?” “Downstairs.” “Why?” “You left it down there.” “I didn’t.” Mara looked toward the door. “Then I must have moved it.” “And my keys?” “What about them?” “They were here yesterday.” “I put them away.” “Where?” “Somewhere safe.” I laughed once. My ribs punished me for it. “You’re not supposed to drive.” “I’m not planning to drive.” “Then you don’t need the keys.” “They’re my keys.” “Elias.” There it was again: my name deployed as a restraint. I swung my legs over the edge of the mattress. Mara stepped back so quickly that I stopped. We both looked at the space she had made. “I’m going to the toilet,” I said. “All right.” “Am I allowed?” “Don’t.” “Don’t what?” “Do this.” She rubbed both hands over her face. When she lowered them, the anger was gone. So was whatever had made her step back. She looked merely exhausted. “Two nights ago,” she said, “I found you at the front door.” “You said I was in the hall.” “You were at the door. It was open.” “Why didn’t you tell me that?” “I am telling you.” “After hiding the keys.” “The doctor said confusion can happen.” “The doctor said mild disorientation.” “You were outside in bare feet.” I looked down. A yellow bruise spread across the top of my left foot. I remembered asking about it and Mara saying I must have knocked it against the bed. “How far outside?” “You were on the step.” “What did I say?” “Nothing.” “Mara.” “You came in when I asked.” She turned away before I could ask anything else. “I’ll make tea.” “I don’t want tea.” “I do.” She left the door open. Her footsteps went carefully down the stairs, one tread creaking beneath her. Then the kettle clicked on in the kitchen. I sat still until the pain in my ribs eased. Five weeks ago, I had been pulled from the river by a man whose name I had forgotten twice. Mara had written it on a note in my wallet. Dean Holloway. Red jacket. Two children. As if those details might turn gratitude into memory. I stood and went to the bedside table. No keys. The drawer held receipts, a dried-out pen, lip balm Mara had been looking for since Christmas, and three screws I must once have intended to put somewhere sensible. When I leaned down to check beneath the bed, something brushed my knuckles. A length of grey fabric had been pushed between the mattress and the frame. I pulled it free. It was the belt from my old dressing gown, the one Mara wore to take out the bins. One end had been folded into a loop wide enough for a wrist. The other was tied to the wooden slat in a cluster of bad, hurried knots. I heard the teaspoon before Mara reached the landing, tapping against the inside of a mug. I dropped the belt and pushed it back beneath the mattress. She came in carrying tea in both hands. The chipped blue mug had no handle; she had to cradle it carefully. It was hers, but she offered it to me. “You hate this one,” I said. “I picked up the wrong mug.” Mara never picked up the wrong mug. Her hands were trembling. The spoon rattled against the china. For the first time, it occurred to me that she might not be watching to see whether I was still breathing. She might be waiting to see what I did next.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally accomplished opening chapter. The model has executed its plan with considerable skill, producing prose that is restrained, precise, and genuinely unsettling without resorting to genre clichés. **Strengths:** The dialogue is the chapter's greatest achievement. It is naturalistic, loaded with subtext, and reveals character through what is withheld rather than stated. The exchange about the keys, the tablet counting, and the use of Elias's full name all do double duty—establishing the marital dynamic while building dread. The line "Every disagreement came with the quiet advantage of a witness" is a particularly sharp observation that captures Elias's unreliable-narrator position without announcing it. The correction from "six weeks" to "five weeks" is a small, perfect detail that establishes Mara's precision and Elias's disorientation simultaneously. The chapter follows its plan faithfully and completely. Every beat from the plan appears: the staring, the keys, the dressing-gown belt, the wrong mug, the shaking hands, the revelation about the front door. The final paragraph lands the chapter's intended note—the shift from "she's watching my breathing" to "she's watching what I do next"—with economy and force. Character consistency is excellent. Elias's voice matches his profile precisely: dry, observant, using exact language when evasive ("I don't specifically remember that" energy pervades his narration), converting fear into irritation. His line about the water tasting "faintly of the tablets dissolving on my tongue" is the kind of concrete, unglamorous detail that makes the prose feel lived-in. Mara's physical tells—the thumbnail pressed into her finger, the step backward, the wrong mug—are all drawn from her profile and deployed naturally. The supernatural dread is handled with admirable restraint. The dressing-gown belt is the chapter's horror centerpiece, and it works precisely because it is described so plainly: "a cluster of bad, hurried knots." The horror is in the improvisation, the shame, the domesticity of it. This is exactly what the plan called for. The prose avoids purple passages almost entirely. There is one slightly self-conscious line—"A marriage did away with names"—which is a touch aphoristic, but it earns its place by being true to the character's observational mode. The observation about Dean Holloway's note in the wallet is quietly devastating and establishes backstory without exposition. **Weaknesses:** The chapter is very close to perfect for its intentions, which makes minor criticisms feel pedantic. The line "That was how most of our conversations went since the hospital" is a mild instance of telling rather than showing—the dialogue has already demonstrated this without the summary. The phrase "something cold between us" when Mara uses his full name is the chapter's one moment of slightly generic emotional shorthand. The bruise on the foot is introduced and explained (Mara said he knocked it on the bed) in a way that feels slightly too neat as a planted detail—though it functions correctly. The chapter's restraint is both its greatest virtue and its only potential liability: readers who want more immediate horror may find the pacing slow. But given the story's stated intentions, this is a feature, not a bug. **Overall:** This is sophisticated, controlled literary horror writing. The plan has been executed with fidelity and genuine craft. The dialogue is among the best in the genre—it sounds like real people in crisis, not horror-movie archetypes. The final image (the wrong mug, the shaking hands, the realization) is earned and chilling. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 19 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 20

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Two ## The Missing Hour By nine, Mara was asleep at the kitchen table. Her head rested on one forearm. The other hand still held her tea, thumb hooked through the handle. There was a red pressure mark across her cheek and a smear of ink beneath her little finger. She had been filling in my medication chart, though no one had asked her to make one. I took the mug before it tipped. She woke at once. Her hand closed on empty air. “It’s me,” I said. “I know.” The speed with which she answered made it sound untrue. I put two slices of bread in the toaster. Mara watched me press the lever, as if this were the sort of task during which I might reveal myself. “Go to bed.” “I’m awake.” “You were drooling on my tablet schedule.” “I was resting my eyes.” “You’re turning into your father.” It was something I’d said before. Usually she rolled her eyes. This time her face emptied. “What?” “Nothing.” The toast sprang up. Mara flinched. I buttered both slices and gave her the darker one. She preferred burnt edges, although she claimed she didn’t. We ate standing at opposite sides of the counter. The house looked indecently normal in daylight. Crumbs beneath the toaster. A dead basil plant on the sill. Rain stippling the window, gentle enough that the traffic still hissed along the road. Beyond the back fence, someone was dragging a recycling bin over paving stones. I had spent half the night imagining Mara as a jailer. It was harder to sustain with her hair flattened on one side and toast crumbs stuck to her blouse. Then I remembered the belt. “Where are my keys?” Her chewing slowed. “I told you. I put them away.” “I’d like them back.” “You aren’t driving.” “I need the shed key.” “What for?” “The radiator in the spare room is knocking.” “No, it isn’t.” “Then I’d like to admire it.” She set down her toast. “Don’t be ridiculous.” “Give me my keys.” “I don’t remember where I put them.” It was the unnecessary lie that settled it. Mara remembered where she put everything. She could locate a receipt from four years ago but not her own glasses when they were on her head. I waited until she went upstairs to shower. Her handbag hung by the front door. The keys were not inside. I checked the bowl on the hall table, her coat pockets, and the drawer where we kept batteries, takeaway menus, and keys that no longer opened anything. Mine were in the inside pocket of her green raincoat. I held them in my palm. The front-door key had a shallow bend near the teeth that had annoyed me for years. The shed key still had white paint on it from when I used it to open a tin. Ordinary, indisputably mine. I put them in my trouser pocket. My phone lay face down beside the microwave. It was fully charged. I had no missed calls, but the message icon showed one outgoing text. At 3:17 that morning, I had sent Mara three words. **ARE YOU AWAKE?** I read it several times. Mara’s phone had been upstairs with her. Mine had been downstairs, according to her. Either she had sent the message herself or I had come down and retrieved it. The sentence was in capitals because I never switched off the setting after typing reference codes at work. Even the question mark felt like mine. The shower stopped overhead. I was still holding the phone when Mara entered the kitchen in her dressing gown, rubbing her hair with a towel. “You moved this.” “Yes.” “Why did I text you?” She stopped rubbing. I showed her the screen. “I don’t know.” “You must have received it.” “My phone was on silent.” “You didn’t see it until now?” “I saw it this morning.” “And said nothing.” “I didn’t know what to say.” “You could’ve started with ‘You sent me a message you don’t remember.’” “You were already angry.” “That hasn’t improved.” She dropped the towel onto a chair. “You’ve been walking around at night.” “So you keep saying.” “I found the front door open.” “So you hid the keys.” “Yes.” “At least we’ve reached that stage of honesty.” Her gaze fell to my pocket. The metal had made a visible shape against the fabric. “Give them to me.” “No.” “You can’t drive.” “I don’t intend to.” “You don’t know what you intend to do when you’re asleep.” The words remained between us. I looked at her bare arms. On the inside of her left upper arm was a yellow-purple bruise shaped like four blurred fingertips. “What happened there?” She folded her arms. “The door frame.” “How?” “I walked into it.” “Sideways?” “It’s a bruise, Elias. I don’t have a diagram.” She was lying. I knew it before I thought it. The certainty arrived whole, calm and sourceless. I wanted to ask whether I had put it there. Instead I said, “You should sleep.” Her expression changed, but I couldn’t tell whether she heard concern or dismissal. “I will when you do.” “That makes no sense.” “It does to me.” She picked up the blue mug from the draining board. A hairline crack ran down the side where the handle had once been. “What happened to this?” she asked. “You know what happened.” “Tell me.” “Why?” “Please.” I remembered the kitchen in our old flat. Rain on the skylight. Mara shouting that I could postpone a decision until we were both dead. I had turned too quickly and knocked the mug from the counter. It struck the tiles; the handle broke away. “You were angry with me,” I said. “I dropped it.” Mara stared at the mug. “No. I broke it at Sophie’s. She told that story about the rabbit and I laughed while I was drinking. It slipped.” I could see that too: a crowded table, Mara bent over laughing, the blue mug falling from both hands. The memories sat beside each other without touching. “Maybe you’re remembering another mug.” “We’ve only ever had one like this.” “Then you’re wrong.” She looked as if I had struck her. I went upstairs before she could answer. In the bedroom, I changed my shirt, moving carefully around my ribs. I remember buttoning it. I remember checking the keys were still in my pocket. Then I was standing at the spare-room door. My hand rested on the knob. The house had changed around me. The shower steam had cleared. A strip of sunlight lay across the landing. Downstairs, the toaster clock read 10:42. The last time I’d seen it, it was 9:51. My heart began to hammer. Both trouser legs were damp to the knee. Not soaked—just darkened, as though I had walked through wet grass. I touched the carpet. Dry. The bathroom floor was dry too. I turned the knob. Locked. A chair scraped on the other side. “Mara?” She did not answer at first. I could hear her breathing, too fast and too close to the door. “Mara, open it.” “Step back.” “What?” “Please step away from the door.” I looked at my hand. The knuckles had gone white around the knob. “I’m not doing anything.” She said my name. Not loudly. Not as a warning. Slowly, with a pause between the syllables. “E-li-as.” My hand released the knob. I stepped back before she had finished saying it.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter Two of this possession horror story demonstrates considerable craft and restraint. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions. **Characterization and Voice** Elias's voice is rendered with impressive consistency. The dry, observational quality established in the character profiles comes through naturally: "You were drooling on my tablet schedule" and "Then I'd like to admire it" capture his sarcasm-as-deflection without feeling forced. His instinct to explain away fear while simultaneously becoming angry at whoever necessitated the explanation is dramatized rather than stated. The moment where certainty about Mara's lie "arrived whole, calm and sourceless" is particularly effective—it's doing double duty, suggesting both his perceptiveness and the presence's influence without announcing itself. Mara is rendered with equal care. Her exhaustion is physical and specific: the pressure mark on her cheek, the ink smear, the hand closing on empty air. Her tendency to provide one unnecessary detail when lying ("I don't remember where I put them") is consistent with her profile. The way she asks about the mug—"Tell me," "Please"—reads as a test rather than a question, which is exactly right. **Dialogue** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. It's genuinely good: clipped, evasive, layered with subtext. The exchange about the keys has the rhythm of a long marriage—each line doing work, neither character saying what they mean. "You could've started with 'You sent me a message you don't remember'" is a line that feels earned. The dialogue avoids the trap of becoming a vehicle for exposition while still advancing the plot efficiently. **Plot and Plan Adherence** The chapter follows its plan closely. The text message at 3:17 a.m. is introduced as planned. The bruise on Mara's arm appears. The mug memory discrepancy is handled well—both versions are presented without resolution, and crucially, Elias can "see" both, which is more unsettling than a simple contradiction. The missing time episode and the damp trouser legs are executed as planned. The chapter ends with Mara saying his name slowly and Elias releasing the door handle before she finishes—a strong, quiet climax. **Supernatural Elements** The possession horror is handled with admirable restraint. The damp trouser legs, the missing time, the sourceless certainty about Mara's lie—these are concrete and specific without being overwrought. The moment at the spare-room door is genuinely unsettling because it's described from the inside: Elias doesn't know how he got there, and the white knuckles on the door handle are noticed by him rather than announced to the reader. **Prose Quality** The prose is clean and controlled. "The house looked indecently normal in daylight" is a good line—"indecently" does real work. The detail of the dead basil plant, the recycling bin being dragged over paving stones, the toast crumbs on Mara's blouse—these ground the horror in the domestic without becoming a catalog. The writing avoids purple prose almost entirely. There are minor weaknesses. "The words remained between us" after Mara's line about not knowing what he intends to do when asleep is a slight lapse into the kind of atmospheric announcement the plan explicitly wanted to avoid. The line "The memories sat beside each other without touching" is a touch more poetic than the surrounding prose warrants, though it's not egregious. **Emotional Engagement** The chapter is emotionally effective. The moment where Elias says "You're turning into your father" and Mara's face empties is quietly devastating—it suggests he's said something that lands differently now, possibly because the presence has borrowed Colin's gestures. The reader understands the weight without being told it. The ending, with Mara saying his name in syllables and Elias's hand releasing before he's decided to release it, generates genuine dread. **Creativity** The chapter avoids horror clichés consistently. The supernatural intrudes through a text message, damp trouser legs, and a door handle—not through dramatic manifestations. The mug memory discrepancy is a clever way to introduce the theme of unreliable shared memory without making it feel like a puzzle to be solved. **Minor Issues** The transition "Then I was standing at the spare-room door" is slightly abrupt—the missing time is conveyed, but the shift could be smoother. The observation that Elias "knew before he thought it" about Mara's lie is good, but the phrase "whole, calm and sourceless" is doing a lot of work and might benefit from one more concrete detail to anchor it. Overall, this is a strong chapter that executes its plan faithfully, maintains character consistency, and generates genuine unease through restraint and specificity rather than escalation. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Three ## What She Did Mara kept the door locked while she told me. I sat on the landing with my back against the wall, damp trouser legs cooling against my shins. She remained on the other side. We spoke through two inches of painted wood like people trying not to wake a child. “The first time was three nights after you came home,” she said. “I woke up and you were in here.” “You were sleeping in our room then.” “I’d moved.” “You hadn’t told me.” “You were upset when I suggested it.” “So you waited until I was asleep.” “Yes.” The answer was too tired to be defensive. “What was I doing?” She was silent long enough that I heard a van change gears on the road. “You had your hand over my mouth.” I looked down at my hands. They were long, square-nailed, familiar. A flake of green was lodged beneath my right thumbnail. “Did I hurt you?” “I couldn’t breathe properly.” “The bruise?” “Your other hand.” I touched my own upper arm, finding the place where fingers might close. “Why didn’t you call the police?” “I said your name. You stopped.” “Stopped what?” “You took your hands away. You looked at me like you’d just come into the room. Then you went back to bed.” “And I didn’t wake?” “Not that I could tell.” “You should’ve told me.” “I’m telling you now.” “You keep saying that as if timing doesn’t matter.” “I was frightened.” “So was I. I just didn’t know why.” The lock clicked. Mara opened the door but stayed behind it. The spare room smelled of sleep and the lavender spray she used on linens, with something sharper beneath it—stale sweat, perhaps, or fear if fear had a household smell. The room had become a barricade. The chair from the sewing desk was wedged beneath the door handle. A saucepan lay on the carpet beside it, with two more stacked inside. She must have balanced them on the handle so they would fall if anyone tried to enter. A dining knife had been pushed under the sash window to keep it shut. On the floor beside the mattress were her phone charger, two cereal-bar wrappers and the wooden rolling pin. “You’ve been sleeping like this?” “I haven’t been sleeping much.” I picked up the saucepan. “Did you think this would stop me?” “No. I thought it would wake me.” Her answer left no space for indignation. I put the pan down. “What about the belt?” She closed her eyes. “You found it.” “Were you ever going to mention tying me to the bed?” “It was once.” “That isn’t an answer.” “You’d been at the front door. I couldn’t keep you inside, and I couldn’t stay awake.” “Then call someone.” “I called the doctor.” “When?” “Yesterday.” “And?” “He said it might be the medication. Or trauma. Or a sleep disorder. He reduced the zopiclone and said he’d request an urgent neurological assessment.” “And if I became violent?” Her eyes shifted to the saucepan. “He said to call emergency services.” “But you didn’t.” “No.” “Why?” “Because they’ll take you away.” “That may be what needs to happen.” “You don’t mean that.” I did not know whether I meant it. I knew only that I wanted her not to be the person deciding. “You tied an unconscious man to a bed with a dressing-gown belt.” “I tied your wrist.” “Thank you for the distinction.” “I sat beside you the entire time.” “That makes it better?” “No.” The word came out sharply. Mara picked up the cereal-bar wrappers and crushed them into her palm. “No, Elias. It doesn’t make it better. I didn’t know what else to do.” “You could have woken me.” “I tried.” “You could’ve called Ruth.” “And told her what? That you tried to smother me but it wasn’t really you?” “You don’t know it wasn’t.” Her face tightened. There it was, the thought neither of us had wanted in the room. Mara bent and began stacking the saucepans neatly, one inside another. “You treat everything I say as evidence,” I said. “If I’m angry, that’s a symptom. If I don’t remember, that’s a symptom. If I do remember but not the way you do, apparently that is too.” “What would you like me to call it?” “My life.” “You don’t remember the last hour of your life.” “And you haven’t slept for two days. Why is your version automatically the sane one?” She stood too quickly and caught the edge of the bedside table. The lamp rocked. She steadied it with both hands. “I’m not saying I’m sane.” That stopped me. She took the saucepans downstairs. I went to the bathroom and changed my trousers. When I unbuttoned my shirt, I caught the smell again. Not damp clothing. Not drains. Pond weed. Mud warmed at the edge of stagnant water. I smelled the collar, then my sleeves. Nothing. The smell came from my right hand. I scrubbed it with soap until the skin around my nails reddened. It remained beneath the thumbnail. I used the point of the nail scissors to work out the green flake I had noticed earlier. It was soft and fibrous. A piece of weed no longer than an eyelash. I placed it on a square of toilet paper. There were explanations. I had gone outside. I had touched a plant. Mara had tracked it in. The dressing gown had been in the garden when she took out the bins. Every explanation was possible until I tried to place myself inside it. By evening we had reached a truce built entirely from exhaustion. “I’ll stay downstairs,” I said. “You sleep.” Mara looked at the sofa, then at me. “And if you get up?” “I’ll wake you.” “You won’t know to.” “Record me.” She gave a short, humorless laugh. “You hate being watched.” “I’m adapting.” She set her phone on the bookshelf facing the sofa and plugged it into an extension lead. The camera showed the room badly: half the sofa, the coffee table and a sliver of the doorway. “Happy?” I asked. “No.” “Good. We agree on something.” She hovered with one hand on the phone. “If you feel strange—” “I’ll come upstairs.” “No.” Her answer was immediate. “Call me.” “I’ll call.” “Don’t come into the room.” I stared at her. Mara looked away first. “Please.” She went upstairs carrying her makeshift alarm. A moment later I heard the spare-room door close, then the scrape of the chair and the careful placement of metal against metal. I sat on the sofa. The phone’s small red recording light faced me. For several minutes I watched it back. I wanted the night to pass without incident. I wanted the footage to show me sitting there, bored and resentful, while Mara slept behind a locked door. More than that, I wanted it to prove she had done something unforgivable. That frightened me almost as much as the missing hour.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of domestic horror writing that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Dialogue and Voice** The dialogue is the chapter's greatest strength. It is genuinely accomplished: clipped, asymmetrical, and emotionally loaded without being theatrical. The exchange through the locked door is particularly effective. Lines like "I said your name. You stopped." / "Stopped what?" / "You took your hands away." achieve real menace through understatement. The rhythm of short declarative sentences creates tension without melodrama. Mara's "I'm not saying I'm sane" is an excellent moment—it genuinely stops Elias (and the reader) cold. The dialogue avoids the trap of characters explaining themselves too neatly; instead, they talk past each other in recognizable ways. **Character Consistency** Both characters behave in ways consistent with their profiles. Elias's insistence on procedural fairness ("You don't know it wasn't") and his tendency to use silence and precision as weapons are well-rendered. His final admission—that he wanted the footage to prove Mara had done something unforgivable—is psychologically acute and consistent with his character's self-awareness about his own defensiveness. Mara's practical improvisation (the saucepan alarm, the stacked pans) is exactly right: shabby, human, and more frightening than polished occult preparations. Her "No" when Elias asks if sitting beside him made the restraint better is perfectly in character. **Show vs. Tell** The chapter mostly shows rather than tells. The spare room described as "a barricade" is earned by the specific inventory: the wedged chair, the stacked saucepans, the rolling pin, the knife under the window. These details do the work. The green weed under the thumbnail is handled well—concrete, specific, and allowed to remain unexplained. However, there are a few moments where the narration becomes slightly explanatory: "Her answer left no space for indignation" is a mild instance of telling us how to read the scene rather than trusting the dialogue itself. Similarly, "There it was, the thought neither of us had wanted in the room" is a slight intrusion of authorial commentary. **Prose Quality** The prose is controlled and largely avoids purple passages. The line "fear if fear had a household smell" is a small grace note that works. "A truce built entirely from exhaustion" is apt. The writing is economical without being arid. There are no egregious metaphors or forced poetic leaps. The smell of pond weed is handled with appropriate restraint—described concretely and then subjected to Elias's rationalizing mind. **Pacing and Plot** The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Mara's confession about the restraint, the revelation of her improvised precautions, the argument about calling for help, the smell of pond weed, and the setup for the recording. The escalation is measured and credible. The ending—Elias watching the recording light and realizing he wants it to prove Mara unforgivable—is the chapter's best moment, a genuinely unsettling psychological insight that earns its darkness. **Weaknesses** The chapter is not without flaws. "The room had become a barricade" is slightly too neat as a transitional sentence—it announces what the following description will show, making the description feel like illustration rather than discovery. The exchange about calling Ruth ("And told her what? That you tried to smother me but it wasn't really you?") is good but the parenthetical "the thought neither of us had wanted in the room" slightly over-explains. The van changing gears on the road is a nice grounding detail, but it's the kind of detail that can feel like a workshop exercise in specificity rather than organic observation. These are minor complaints. The chapter is not particularly surprising in its structure—it follows the plan closely and hits expected beats—but the execution elevates familiar material. The horror remains domestic and credible throughout. **Overall** This is accomplished literary horror writing. It respects the reader's intelligence, maintains tonal consistency, and achieves genuine emotional weight through restraint rather than escalation. The characters feel like real people in an impossible situation rather than horror archetypes. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Four ## The Video The recording was two hours and thirteen minutes long. Mara and I watched it the next morning at the kitchen table. She had slept for forty minutes before the saucepans woke her. I remembered hearing them fall. I remembered calling up to ask if she was all right. I did not remember anything after that. On the screen, I sat with my right ankle on my left knee, watching a quiz show with the sound too low to follow. Every few minutes I drank from a mug. I looked ordinary. Tired, perhaps. Badly in need of a haircut. “This proves nothing,” I said. “Keep watching.” “We could skip ahead.” “I did that the first time. I want you to see it all.” So we watched me change channels. We watched me eat half a slice of toast and brush crumbs from my shirt. Once, I glanced directly at the phone, and the Elias at the table flinched. “Why did I do that?” “You knew it was there.” “I don’t remember knowing anything.” Mara rubbed her eyes. “That doesn’t mean you didn’t.” After an hour and thirty-eight minutes, the sitting-room door opened. Mara appeared in the gap wearing her dressing gown. Her hair was flattened against her cheek. On the recording she said, “Are you all right?” I turned toward her. “Fine.” “You’ll call if you need me?” “Yes.” “Promise?” I smiled. It was a patient, fond smile. “Go to bed, Mars.” Beside me, Mara stopped the video. “What?” “You called me Mars.” “So?” “You haven’t called me that since you came home.” “I just did.” “On the recording.” “Still me.” She pressed play. Recorded Mara stayed in the doorway a few seconds longer, then disappeared. The door closed. The screen held me alone. I did not move. At first it seemed like the video had frozen, but the quiz-show lights continued flickering over the wall. My chest rose and fell. The mug steamed between my hands. One minute passed. Then another. By the fifth, I wanted Mara to increase the speed. I didn’t ask. We sat in the kitchen listening to the thin electronic laughter of an audience from the night before. At six minutes and forty-three seconds, I placed the mug on the table. I stood. There was nothing unnatural in the movement. That made it worse. My ribs did not trouble me. I did not brace against the sofa or pause halfway upright. I crossed the room and stopped beneath the bookshelf, too close to the phone to be properly seen. The image showed my chest, the underside of my chin and my left hand hanging at my side. My right hand rose out of frame. “What am I doing?” Mara pointed at the screen. The thumb of my left hand rubbed the rough edge of my wedding ring. Slowly. Back and forth. “Your father did that,” I said. “Yes.” “I’ve seen him do it.” “Not often. He stopped wearing the ring before you met him.” “Then you do it.” “I don’t.” “You must.” “Why must I?” “Because the alternative is ridiculous.” On the phone, I whispered something. Mara raised the volume. Static filled the kitchen, followed by my breathing. There was a wet catch at the end of each exhalation. A word surfaced. “Mara.” Then a longer phrase, blurred by the cheap microphone. Stop looking, I thought it said. Mara had heard the same thing. I could tell from her face. The recording continued. Upstairs, a saucepan struck the floor. “Elias?” Mara called. The hand worrying the ring stopped. My shoulders dropped. I turned my head, staring down at myself as though surprised to find a body attached. Then I backed away from the phone, one hand pressed to my ribs. “Why am I standing?” I called. The video ended nineteen minutes later. Nothing else happened. Mara closed the file. “It could be sleepwalking,” I said. “Yes.” “Or the tablets.” “Yes.” “I don’t like it when you agree like that.” “How would you prefer me to agree?” “You think it’s something else.” She looked at the phone. “I think you were awake.” “I wasn’t.” “Your eyes were open.” “That doesn’t mean anything.” “You answered me.” “People talk in their sleep.” “I know.” I pushed back from the table. “Then say what you mean.” Mara took the blue mug from beside the sink and held it in both hands. There was no tea in it. “There’ve been other things,” she said. “What things?” “The way you stand sometimes. With one foot turned out. Dad used to stand like that when his hip hurt.” “I knew your father.” “You met him twice.” “Twice is enough to see someone stand.” “You cough like Mr Hale.” “Who?” “Our neighbor when I was a child.” I laughed. It came out too loudly. “I’m possessed by everyone you’ve ever met?” “I didn’t say that.” “You didn’t need to.” “That’s why I didn’t tell you. It sounds mad when I say it aloud.” “It sounded mad before.” Mara put the mug down. “Do you know what I said at the hospital?” “No.” “They wouldn’t let me into the room. I could hear people talking, but no one would tell me what was happening. A nurse kept saying they were doing everything they could.” “Mara—” “I said I didn’t care how.
