Instead of debating whether consuming the Husher compromises humanity, they may argue about which
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention The story will be an intimate, darkly funny cozy-horror tale about hunger, cooking, and the uneasy compromises people make for those they love. It will stay focused on the original hook: starving survivors discover that the spore mutants hunting them are not merely edible, but delicious. The story will take place almost entirely in **the Hare’s Head**, a shabby rural pub fortified against the spore-ridden countryside. The setting will feel used rather than decoratively cozy: brewery-logo plates, a temperamental stove, Christmas bunting that no one has bothered to remove, pub-quiz sheets used for medical records, and an old fruit machine repurposed as a dry cupboard. Comfort will come from familiar labor, bickering, shared meals, and people knowing one another’s habits—not from an accumulation of picturesque objects. The narrative will use **close third person through Mara Venn**, the pub’s former cook. Mara understands love as feeding people, but she also derives authority and self-worth from controlling the kitchen. The empty pantry therefore threatens both the group’s survival and her place within it. The mutant meat restores her ability to provide, while forcing her to confront how easily care can become justification. The supporting cast will be small: - **June Bell**, a nineteen-year-old forager who presents herself as fearless but is deeply afraid of infection. - **Eli Rook**, formerly a university laboratory technician who worked with fungal cultures. He has useful knowledge but no authoritative understanding of the mutated organism. - **Agnes Pike**, a retired school secretary and enthusiastic gardener who insists on ordinary standards mainly because disorder irritates her. She is observant, stubborn, hungry, and not automatically the group’s moral center. - **Bramble**, Agnes’s old dog, who is good at sensing nearby mutants but has no special immunity or sacrificial role. The mutants, nicknamed **Hushers**, are human-shaped organisms colonized and extensively rebuilt by the spores. No one knows whether anything of the original person remains. Some imitate habits or repeat words, but the survivors cannot determine whether this is consciousness, memory, or fungal mimicry. The story will not settle the question. The Hushers’ white internal tissue can be rendered edible by trimming away gray growth, soaking it in strong brine, and cooking it thoroughly. The group arrives at this method through frightened guesswork rather than reliable science. Their instructions remain provisional, contradictory, and scribbled on the backs of old pub-quiz sheets. The meat will be genuinely appetizing but not magically perfect. It browns like poultry, tastes of roast duck and mushrooms, and has a faint metallic sweetness. Certain cuts are rubbery; the cooking liquid turns unpleasantly gluey; leftovers remain warm too long. Its deliciousness will not be explained away. Eating it has one major uncanny consequence: for a few days afterward, Hushers often fail to recognize the diners as prey. Eli theorizes that they smell chemically similar to the mutants, but this remains speculation. The effect is inconsistent, preventing it from becoming a reliable superpower. The eaters also experience minor disturbances—shared phrases in dreams, dulled sensitivity to cold, misplaced memories—but the story will avoid constructing an elaborate telepathic mythology. The narrative’s central moral problem will remain unresolved. The survivors do not receive proof that Hushers are no longer human, nor do they discover a guilt-free renewable substitute. They eventually adopt a rule against deliberately hunting any Husher that speaks or performs a recognizably human action. The rule is sentimental, scientifically indefensible, and difficult to apply. It exists because they need some boundary between survival and appetite. The plot will cover a narrow span of roughly ten days: 1. The survivors reach the end of their ordinary food. 2. They kill a Husher that enters the pub. 3. Mara reflexively tastes its cooking broth and discovers it is safe and delicious. 4. The group shares the first meal. 5. They discover the meat’s partial protective effect. 6. Hunger leads them to hunt another Husher. 7. Their ethical rule immediately fails when the carcass shows signs of humanity after death. 8. A piece of discarded tissue blooms beneath the pub, attracting more Hushers. 9. The survivors burn the cellar growth and part of their food stores, escaping complete infestation at a real cost. 10. They end with preserved mutant meat, a damaged home, and no permanent answer to hunger. The climax will not provide a perfectly balanced solution. The group will save the pub’s kitchen and dining room but lose the cellar, garden seeds, and much of the building’s lower floor. Their remaining Husher meat will buy time, not solve food scarcity. Continued survival will require further morally compromised choices. The story’s emotional center will be the first communal mutant supper. That scene will receive ample space for appetite, revulsion, humor, digestive discomfort, and the quiet politics of portion size. Mara will notice who eats quickly, who pretends not to enjoy it, and who hopes someone else will refuse. The prose will favor clarity and concrete observation over ornate fungal imagery. Atmospheric language will be used selectively. Humor will emerge from embarrassment and practical disagreements: what to call the meat, whether it needs mustard, who ruined a batch by overboiling it, and whether vomiting counts as wasting food. Violence will be brief and clumsy. The story will avoid: - A detailed origin story for the apocalypse. - A scientist who provides certain answers. - A human antagonist or raider subplot. - A broad cast or extensive worldbuilding. - Sentimental speeches about preserving humanity. - Perfectly distributed ensemble tasks during the climax. - A sustainable “mutant farm” that cleans up the ethical problem. - A tidy cure, ecological truce, or fully secure refuge. - Repeatedly stating the story’s themes. - Turning every detail into foreshadowing or symbolism. Each chapter will be approximately 1,000 words and built around one principal event. The short scope demands quick entry into the premise, limited exposition, and consequences that develop through action rather than multiple subplots. # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1 — Nothing on the Menu The Hare’s Head has reached the embarrassing end of its food rather than the picturesque one. Mara inventories custard powder, cocktail onions, stale cheese-and-onion crisps, mustard sachets, and a bottle of crème de menthe. She has secretly skipped meals for two days but continues pretending she ate while cooking. Freezing “spore rain” rules out foraging. June wants to search nearby cottages anyway, Eli warns that their masks are deteriorating, and Agnes accuses Mara of hiding the true state of the pantry. Their argument reveals that the group is affectionate but strained, with everyone watching everyone else’s portions. Mara serves hot water thickened with custard powder and calls it soup. Agnes refuses to accept the name. Before anyone can eat, Bramble begins barking at the wall behind the fruit machine. A Husher forces its way through rotten boards, reaching toward the diners’ breath. The struggle is close and undignified. June freezes despite her claimed fearlessness. Mara kills the creature with a fire extinguisher and the sharpened leg of a bar stool. Its damaged torso splits open against the stove. While everyone is still gagging from the gray outer growths, the white tissue inside begins to brown on the hot iron. It smells like roast duck. ## Chapter 2 — Waste Not The group drags the corpse into the pub’s covered bin yard. Its anatomy is close enough to human to be upsetting and wrong enough to resist certainty. One foot wears the remains of a sock; inside, its “bones” branch like hard roots. Eli admits he only cleaned equipment and cultivated samples in a university fungus lab. He knows contamination procedures, not what the Hushers have become. He believes the white tissue may be dense fungal protein, but every safety recommendation he makes includes “probably.” Mara is the first to consider eating it, though she frames her interest as preventing waste. June, unexpectedly, objects. Agnes asks whether anyone has a better idea for breakfast. They remove a small cut. The flesh squeaks against the knife and sends threads toward Mara’s warm fingers. They trim away anything gray, soak the sample in brine, and boil it in a saucepan they agree never to use again. The first broth becomes glue. The meat is rubbery. Nobody volunteers to taste it. While arguing with Eli, Mara automatically dips a spoon into the second cooking liquid, tastes it, and swallows before remembering what it is. It is rich, salty, and better than anything she has eaten in months. She is furious—at the meat, at herself, and at everyone staring. ## Chapter 3 — The Tasting They wait six hours for Mara to become sick. Eli records her symptoms on the back of a 2019 Christmas pub quiz. Mara experiences stomach cramps but cannot tell whether they come from poison, hunger, or fear. Bramble begs for the discarded meat but is given none. Agnes quietly admits she tasted gravy from Mara’s spoon while no one was looking. When neither woman deteriorates, June accuses them of deciding for everyone else. The accusation is partly fear and partly hunger. Mara prepares a second batch more carefully. She trims aggressively, uses fresh brine, dries the meat, and fries it with the last spoonful of cooking fat and several stolen mustard sachets. It browns beautifully. The edges crisp; the middle has the texture of duck breast; a metallic sweetness lingers afterward. No one gives a speech before eating. June takes the smallest portion and finishes first. Eli chews too quickly after recommending caution. Agnes complains that the mustard masks the flavor, then scrapes the pan. Mara resents how much pleasure it gives them. That night, several people suffer cramps and vivid dreams involving a woman folding napkins in a room none of them recognizes. In the morning, the remaining cooked meat is gone from the cold pan. Mara finds Eli rinsing the plate. He insists he ate it “for comparison.” She writes down a new rule: **Count the portions before bed.** ## Chapter 4 — House Special With no serious poisoning after two days, Mara butchers the rest of the Husher. The process is no longer an experiment but kitchen work, which bothers her more. She learns which sections are tender and which become tough. The group argues over what to call it; “Husher” makes June gag, while Agnes rejects “forest roast” as dishonest. On the chalkboard, Mara writes simply: **HOUSE SPECIAL.** The chapter centers on their first full supper. Agnes sets out brewery-logo plates because eating from the cooking pot leads to arguments about quantity. Mara serves equal portions but gives herself less, both from guilt and to be noticed doing it. Agnes notices and swaps their plates without comment. The meal brings warmth, conversation, and the first real laughter in weeks. It also exposes appetite. June asks for seconds before anyone else finishes. Eli licks grease from his thumb. Mara catches herself estimating how many meals a larger Husher would provide. Later, Bramble slips out through the damaged wall. June runs after him without waiting for a mask check. A Husher stands in the lane, close enough to seize her. It sniffs her hair and turns away. The group cannot tell whether the meal protected June or whether the creature simply behaved unpredictably. Eli favors a chemical explanation. Agnes says one incident proves nothing. Nevertheless, when they hear more Hushers in the wet fields, everyone looks toward the nearly empty meat tin. ## Chapter 5 — Out for Supplies Three days later, the meat is gone and the spore rain continues. Hunger has returned faster than their scruples. Mara proposes killing another Husher while its apparent indifference lasts. June calls the plan hunting people, then volunteers to go because she knows the surrounding fields. Mara, June, and Eli enter an abandoned orchard. The Hushers initially ignore them, but the effect is inconsistent: one begins following Eli until Mara distracts it with a hot water bottle. Their supposed protection is exposed as a dangerous guess. They find a Husher crouched beside an overturned wheelbarrow, repeatedly placing rotten apples into neat rows. It wears no recognizable clothing and does not speak, but its pointless task feels human. June refuses to kill it. A second Husher charges from a shed. Mara kills it in panic with a pruning hook while Eli stands uselessly holding the salt sprayer. The attack is messy and frightening rather than heroic. Only after death do they notice old glitter nail polish sealed beneath the growth on two fingernails. June is sick. Mara still orders them to carry the carcass back because leaving that much food to rot feels impossible. On the return journey, the apple-stacking Husher follows at a distance. ## Chapter 6 — Leftovers Back at the pub, June refuses to eat the new meat. Mara butchers it anyway, focusing on procedure because she cannot think about the painted nails. Eli claims mimicry does not prove consciousness; Agnes asks whether certainty would make the killing better. Their first ethical rule emerges from an argument rather than agreement: they will not deliberately hunt anything that speaks or performs a recognizably human action. Eli points out that the rule would have spared the apple-stacker but not the creature they killed. No one answers. Mara roasts part of the carcass. June holds out until the smell fills the pub, then eats standing by the sink and cries from shame and relief. Mara pretends not to see. The group is less celebratory this time, but everyone finishes. Bramble scratches at the cellar door. Beneath the flagstones, a scrap from the first carcass has taken root among spilled beer, mouse nests, and Agnes’s seed boxes. White tissue bulges through the cracks, warm as fresh bread. It has formed several soft, fingerlike shapes tipped with flecks of glitter. Eli wants to study it before they destroy it. Mara suspects he also wants to know whether it can be eaten. Agnes discovers that half her seeds are already threaded with growth. Outside, Hushers begin gathering silently around the pub. ## Chapter 7 — Last Orders The cellar growth swells toward the stove’s warmth, buckling floorboards and blocking the rear exit. The Hushers outside do not attack, but their presence prevents escape. Among them, the orchard Husher continues arranging stones in rows. Eli proposes containing the growth with salt. Mara tries, but they do not have enough; their jar is almost empty after brining the meat. The fungus crosses the thin line. There will be no clean cultivation or permanent compromise. Mara decides to burn the cellar before the growth reaches the kitchen. The decision means losing Agnes’s seeds, their remaining preserved food, and part of the pub. Agnes initially refuses to leave the seed boxes and has to be pulled upstairs. June does not perform a brave specialist task; she panics, then recovers enough to help Bramble through a window. The fire does not behave as planned. Smoke fills the dining room, a burning beam destroys the fruit-machine pantry, and Eli burns his hand trying to retrieve his notes. Mara throws their last bottle of spirits into the cellar, hating the waste even as it catches. The Hushers outside retreat from the heat. The apple-stacker remains at the edge of the lane, watching until part of the floor collapses and the cellar growth shrieks through several human voices. By dawn, the bloom is dead. So are the garden seeds and most of the pub’s remaining stores. The kitchen survives, blackened and exposed to the cold. In the ashes, they recover one sealed tin of cooked Husher meat. ## Chapter 8 — Served While Hot Two days later, the group occupies the smoke-stained dining room because the rest of the pub is unsafe. They patch the wall with a brewery sign and burn broken furniture for heat. Hunger is already returning. Mara opens the salvaged tin. Nobody discusses saving it longer. She fries the meat on the temperamental stove and uses the final mustard sachet. Agnes sets out four plates and a chipped saucer for Bramble, though the dog still refuses the Husher meat. Their provisional rules remain on a scorched pub-quiz sheet: - Cut off the gray. - Brine it if there is salt. - Cook it twice. - Count the portions. - Do not hunt one that speaks. - Do not hunt one that remembers how to be someone. Eli has added beneath the last rule: **How can we tell?** They eat. The meat is still delicious. This time no one pretends otherwise. June asks what they will do tomorrow, and Mara says they will look for food. She does not specify what kind. Outside, the apple-stacking Husher approaches the damaged pub. It places four sound apples and one rotten one in a row on the doorstep, then retreats into the rain. The gesture could be a gift, a remembered habit, or bait. Mara brings the apples inside. She cuts away the rot and puts the sound pieces into the pan with the last of the meat. As the apples begin to caramelize, Agnes starts humming a tune from their shared dream. She claims she has always known it. Mara knows she has not, but says nothing. They eat before the food gets cold.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Mara Venn ## Background Mara is forty-one and has worked in pub kitchens since she left school at sixteen. She was never formally trained. She learned by being shouted at by chefs, reading abandoned cookbooks after closing, and discovering which shortcuts customers noticed. She eventually became the cook at the Hare’s Head, though “chef” still makes her suspicious. Before the outbreak, she was known for oversized Sunday roasts, excellent chips, and a steak-and-ale pie that depended more heavily on stock cubes than she admitted. She grew up nearby with a mother who treated food as the answer to most problems and silence as the answer to everything else. Mara inherited both habits. She has an older sister, Helen, who moved south years before the outbreak. Mara does not know whether Helen survived. She rarely mentions her and becomes brusque when asked. When the spores began spreading, Mara stayed at the Hare’s Head because leaving required a destination. The pub already had bottled gas, rain barrels, a kitchen, and heavy doors. Other survivors arrived and, without a vote, began treating her as the person in charge. Mara pretends this irritates her. In truth, being necessary is the closest thing she has to feeling secure. ## Personality Mara is practical, watchful, and capable of intense tenderness so long as she can disguise it as a task. She will not ask whether someone is frightened, but she will heat their socks and put an extra spoonful in their bowl. She dislikes public gratitude because it reveals that care has passed between people. She is not naturally optimistic. Her resilience comes from being irritated by problems and unwilling to let them have the final word. A jammed door, spoiled ingredient, or invading monster provokes much the same initial response: a hard stare followed by aggressive practical action. She sees waste as a moral failure. Before the outbreak, this meant saving vegetable peelings for stock and transforming old bread into pudding. Under starvation, the habit becomes dangerous. Her impulse to consider eating the Husher does not arise from bravery or scientific curiosity. She cannot bear to see usable flesh discarded while people she feeds are hungry. Mara is capable of generosity, but not free of calculation. She notices portion sizes automatically. She knows who eats fastest and who leaves scraps. When she gives herself less, part of her genuinely wants the others fed; another part wants them to notice the sacrifice. She is ashamed of this second motive and therefore becomes defensive if anyone thanks her. ## Idiosyncrasies - Mara tastes sauces automatically, even when she has already decided they are ruined. This reflex causes her to become the first person to consume cooked Husher. - She keeps useless pre-apocalypse food-safety certificates pinned in the kitchen. She claims they block a draft. - She writes menus in block capitals and presses so hard that the chalk snaps. - She dislikes mushrooms. This makes the flavor of Husher flesh personally offensive. - She calls every refrigerator “the cold room,” including small domestic fridges. - She refuses to drink tea that has been reheated, despite willingly eating ingredients of dubious origin. - When anxious, she reorganizes tools according to size. - She silently corrects people who use the wrong knife but usually refrains from taking it away for several seconds. - She swears sparingly in conversation, but continuously when alone with malfunctioning equipment. - She knows the prices of old pub meals to the penny and sometimes thinks in obsolete food costs: *Fourteen ninety-five, with peas and chips.* - She dislikes music while cooking. The Hare’s Head’s old sound system died years ago, and Mara considers this one of the apocalypse’s few improvements. - She sleeps badly unless she can hear the stove ticking as it cools. ## Flaws Mara equates usefulness with deserving a place in the group. She therefore hides weakness, refuses help, and takes criticism of her cooking or rationing as criticism of her right to belong. She is controlling about food. She insists this is necessary—and often it is—but she also uses the kitchen to make decisions for others. She lies about having eaten, chooses portions without consultation, and withholds information if she thinks open discussion will impede action. Her attention to immediate practical needs can become moral avoidance. Once the second Husher is dead, she concentrates on how to butcher it because the technical problem is easier than asking whether they should eat it. She can make an act feel inevitable by reducing it to a sequence of chores. Mara bears grudges over small matters because large griefs are harder to handle. She still remembers that June broke the blue serving bowl eighteen months earlier, but claims not to remember the date her sister last contacted her. ## Physicality Mara is broad through the shoulders and hips, though hunger has made her clothes hang loose. Her dark hair is threaded with gray and cut bluntly at jaw level using kitchen scissors. One side is usually shorter. Her hands are scarred from burns and knife slips. She has a pale line across her left palm from opening a tin badly at nineteen and a fresh collection of cracks around her nails from cold and lye. She rubs her thumb over these cracks when making decisions. Mara carries herself as if moving through a crowded kitchen even when the room is empty: elbows close, turns economical, eyes checking exits and surfaces. She rarely sits fully back in a chair. Her face is expressive despite her efforts, especially around the mouth. Disapproval appears first as a slight flattening of her upper lip. She interacts with objects confidently and people indirectly. She will move a hot pan without hesitation but pause before touching someone’s shoulder. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships Mara’s immediate motive is to keep the Hare’s Head’s inhabitants fed and the pub inhabitable. Beneath that is a more selfish need: if she cannot provide either food or safety, she does not know why the others would keep her. Her allegiance is to the household rather than any abstract idea of humanity. She will choose the four people in the pub over strangers, rules, or moral purity, though she wants to think she would not. She treats June with an awkward mixture of protectiveness and annoyance. June’s appetite pleases her; June’s risk-taking enrages her. Mara sometimes speaks to her as if she were much younger than nineteen and fails to notice how condescending this feels. She relies on Eli’s knowledge but distrusts his appetite for explanations. His cautious language irritates her because she wants answers he cannot provide. She also recognizes that he seeks permission from her before making decisions and resents the responsibility while preserving it. Agnes is the person most willing to challenge Mara’s control. Their disputes are domestic, repetitive, and affectionate. Agnes knows when Mara has skipped a meal and does not allow the deception to pass unmarked. Mara tolerates Bramble in theory and spoils him in practice. She complains about his hair while keeping bones for him. ## Voice Mara speaks plainly, often using practical questions to avoid emotional ones. Her internal voice is sharper and less generous than her actions. > “I didn’t say it was safe. I said it stopped moving, which is not the same thing and ought to be enough for one morning.” > “Don’t thank me. Eat it before it turns to paste.” > *It smelled good. That was the obscenity of it. If it had smelled of graves, they could all have stayed decent.* > *Four equal portions, one smaller. Hers, obviously. Not because she was making a point. If they took it as a point, that was their business.* # June Bell ## Background June is nineteen. Before the outbreak, she lived with her mother and two younger half-brothers in a commuter town twelve miles from the Hare’s Head. She was seventeen when the spores arrived and had recently left a college course in animal care after six weeks. She told people the course was boring. In fact, she panicked during practical assessments and stopped attending rather than admit it. She survived the first year with a mobile group that scavenged farms and service stations. She does not describe that period in sequence. Her stories are funny fragments about sleeping in a garden shed or stealing a cardboard cutout from a petrol station. She leaves out the names of the people who were with her. Mara suspects June was abandoned at least once; June insists she simply “went a different way.” She arrived at the Hare’s Head fourteen months ago with infected scratches on her coat, no actual infection, and a rucksack full of batteries that fit none of the pub’s equipment. She was allowed to stay for one night and never formally left. June knows the lanes, drainage ditches, abandoned gardens, and gaps in local fences better than anyone else. She calls this foraging. Much of it is trespassing with good memory. ## Personality June is energetic, observant, defensive, and eager to be treated as capable. She performs fearlessness because she believes frightened people are left behind. Her jokes become faster and worse when she is scared. She resists authority automatically, especially when she wants the authority figure’s approval. She complains about Mara’s rules while following most of them. If Mara tells her not to go outside, June argues; if Mara forgets to tell her, June becomes uneasy. June has strong moral reactions that often surprise the older survivors. She can suggest eating a dead monster in theory, then recoil when faced with the physical evidence that it may once have been a person. She wants principles to be clear because ambiguity makes her feel unsafe. Her appetite embarrasses her. During her months on the road, she learned to eat quickly because food could be taken away. At the pub, she tries to slow down and frequently fails. She watches other people’s plates without intending to. ## Idiosyncrasies - June collects key rings from abandoned buildings, even when there are no keys attached. Her favorite is a rubber trout. - She claims not to like sweet food but eats custard powder from the packet. - She names objects that are difficult to repair. The pub’s handcart is Denise; one respirator is Uncle Mike. - She cannot whistle but repeatedly attempts it while nervous. - She memorizes escape routes and chooses seats with a clear path to the door. - She keeps her boots on indoors, including in bed, unless Mara explicitly orders her to remove them. - She hates anything touching the inside of her wrist because that is where she checks for signs of fungal threads. - She steals small items without needing them—spoons, elastic bands, chalk—and later leaves them in visible places as though they migrated there independently. - She eats crisp crumbs by tipping the packet directly into her mouth, then folds the empty packet into a perfect square. - She is unexpectedly good at imitating birds but refuses to do it when asked. - She has a habit of saying “obviously” when something is not obvious to her at all. - She keeps one clean yellow shoelace in her pocket. It belonged to one of her brothers, though she tells different stories about it. ## Flaws June mistakes refusal for independence. She objects before she understands what is being proposed and sometimes creates danger to avoid appearing obedient. She lies casually, especially about fear, injuries, and food. Most of the lies are transparent. She is offended when people expose them but more wounded when they pretend to believe her. Her moral certainty can be selective. She condemns Mara for hunting Hushers, then eats the resulting meat. She hates herself for this and redirects that hatred into anger at whoever made the practical decision. June freezes during immediate physical danger. She has learned to function well before and after a crisis—planning routes, checking equipment, treating wounds—but sudden violence can empty her mind. Her self-image depends on nobody discovering this, even though the group already knows. She is fiercely attached to the Hare’s Head but avoids admitting that it is home. Naming it as home would give her something she could lose. ## Physicality June is short, quick, and wiry, with the restless movements of someone accustomed to being cold. She has tightly curled brown hair that she cuts unevenly at the sides and ties back with scavenged elastic. A healed split in her left eyebrow gives her skeptical expression extra force. She wears layered clothes regardless of temperature, partly for warmth and partly because armor makes her feel safer. Her green waterproof coat is several sizes too large. The sleeves cover her hands when she is tired. June’s face changes rapidly. She cannot conceal disgust, anticipation, or resentment. When she lies, she lifts her chin and becomes unnaturally still. She touches walls, furniture, and doorframes as she passes, orienting herself through contact. Outdoors, she becomes quieter and more deliberate. Indoors, she sprawls when she feels safe, occupying more space than her body requires. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships June wants to remain useful enough that the group cannot decide she is expendable. She also wants permission to stop proving herself, though she would reject that formulation. Her primary allegiance is to the Hare’s Head household, especially Mara, but she disguises loyalty as convenience. She fears infection more than death because she has seen people treated as already lost at the first symptom. Mara is the nearest thing June has to a parent, which makes Mara uniquely capable of infuriating her. June wants praise from her but distrusts tenderness that might expose this need. She steals food from Mara’s kitchen partly because being scolded reassures her that ordinary rules still apply. She likes Eli because he answers questions seriously, but she becomes hostile when his answers are uncertain. She suspects his scientific language conceals selfishness, not entirely incorrectly. Agnes treats June neither as a child nor as an exceptional survivor. June finds this restful. They sometimes play cards using a deck missing all four queens. Bramble sleeps against June’s legs during cold nights. She insists he prefers her because she runs warm. In fact, she feeds him scraps under the table. ## Voice June speaks quickly, uses humor as misdirection, and dislikes abstract language. Her internal thoughts are more frightened and attentive than her dialogue suggests. > “I’m not saying we eat it. I’m saying burning it seems wasteful, and there’s a gap between those things where Eli can do science at it.” > “That one was arranging apples. Monsters don’t arrange things. They knock things over. That’s one of the main benefits of calling them monsters.” > *Don’t look at its hands. Hands made everything worse. Teeth were fine. Claws were ideal. Hands meant somebody might ask what it had once held.* > *She would eat one bite. Slowly. As if she had always had the luxury of eating slowly.* # Eli Rook ## Background Eli is thirty-five. Before the collapse, he worked as a laboratory technician at a university’s plant pathology department. He maintained cultures, sterilized equipment, prepared media, recorded temperatures, and kept experiments running for academics who frequently forgot his name. Other survivors have gradually promoted him to “scientist,” partly because he owns goggles and uses words such as “substrate.” Eli corrects them when confidence would be dangerous and allows the title when it makes him useful. This contradiction troubles him less than he claims. He did not create or directly study the outbreak organism. His lab handled aggressive crop fungi and experimental antifungal treatments, which gives him relevant habits rather than special knowledge. He knows how contamination travels and how little can be concluded from one sample. Eli reached the Hare’s Head six months after the outbreak. He had been traveling with his partner, Sam, who developed fungal growth in a cut. Eli delayed abandoning him because he kept believing there might be a treatment. Sam transformed during the night and killed another member of their group. Eli has since become suspicious of both hope and hesitation. He does not share this history readily. When he does mention Sam, he describes procedures rather than the relationship. ## Personality Eli is cautious, curious, conflict-avoidant, and hungrier for certainty than his careful speech suggests. He values evidence, but deprivation has made him skilled at turning desire into a hypothesis. He is socially earnest and occasionally pompous without realizing it. He explains terms when nervous. Mara’s impatience makes him speak faster and use longer words, which worsens her impatience. Eli dislikes making decisions because he fears being responsible for the result. He offers probabilities and lets someone else choose. When Mara does so, he can later tell himself that he merely supplied information. This frustrates her and protects him. He is more sensuous than his clinical manner suggests. He loves texture, warmth, and rich food. Before the outbreak, he baked elaborate cakes to manage stress. His appetite for the Husher meat embarrasses him because it cannot be framed as detached observation. ## Idiosyncrasies - Eli keeps notes on anything with a blank surface: quiz sheets, beer labels, wallpaper margins. - His handwriting becomes smaller as he grows less certain. - He cleans his glasses when buying time, even though one lens is plastic and permanently scratched. - He says “interesting” when he means alarming. - He hates touching cotton wool and will trade unpleasant chores to avoid it. - He owns three pens and checks for them after every stressful event. - He peels food into exact strips before eating it. - He keeps the torn corner of a photograph in his notebook. It shows only Sam’s elbow and part of a cake. - He distrusts traditional remedies but carries a sachet of lavender because the smell calms him. - He dislikes beer despite living in a pub. - He once spent a week trying to repair the fruit machine because he wanted its lights, not its function. - He secretly ranks meals in his notes and is ashamed that the Husher roast receives the highest score. ## Flaws Eli uses scientific caution to avoid moral accountability. He emphasizes uncertainty when a decision is unpleasant, then becomes more confident when evidence supports something he wants. He is capable of secrecy under the guise of research. He eats an extra portion of meat, tells himself the unplanned dose has experimental value, and records symptoms only after finishing it. His fear of infection can make him cruel. He believes people showing symptoms should be isolated immediately, regardless of circumstance. This comes from experience, but he sometimes presents trauma as objectivity. Eli wants to be indispensable while resenting others for expecting answers. He corrects the exaggerated impression of his expertise, but not so firmly that the group stops consulting him. Under pressure, he can become passive. He notices danger, explains it accurately, and waits for someone else to act. ## Physicality Eli is tall and stooped, with a narrow frame made more angular by hunger. He has sandy hair thinning at the crown and a beard he trims with nail scissors. His glasses are held together at one hinge with copper wire. He moves carefully around contaminated material but awkwardly in open space. His hands are steady during delicate work and clumsy during violence. In the orchard, he holds the salt sprayer correctly while failing to use it. His habitual expression is a slight frown of concentration. When pleased, he tries to suppress his smile and produces a guilty grimace instead. His ears redden when accused of lying. Eli dislikes casual touch and leans away before he can stop himself. When exhausted, he stands very close to the stove with both hands extended, as though he has forgotten anyone can see him. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships Eli wants to survive without repeating the hesitation that killed Sam and the other traveler. At the same time, he fears becoming the sort of person who treats uncertainty as permission to abandon others. These motives pull him in opposite directions. His allegiance is primarily to the Hare’s Head household, but in moments of fear he can imagine sacrificing one person to protect the rest. He knows this about himself and hides it. He respects Mara’s decisiveness and relies on it too much. He sometimes provokes her into making choices he already wants. Their conflict is less reason versus instinct than two different ways of controlling uncertainty. He is patient with June’s questions because he remembers being dismissed by academics. Her accusations hurt him because they often identify truths he has disguised in technical language. Agnes makes him uneasy because she is unimpressed by credentials and asks blunt questions. They argue about gardening methods despite both knowing that the garden is failing. Bramble distrusts Eli’s medical examinations. The feeling is mutual after the dog once ate one of his sample labels. ## Voice Eli qualifies almost every claim. When frightened, he becomes more technical; when ashamed, unusually concise. > “I said it was predominantly fungal tissue. I did not say edible. Those are separate categories with an irresponsible amount of space between them.” > “One additional portion is not an experiment. I’m aware of that. I wrote the symptoms down anyway.” > *Probably safe meant nothing. Probably safe was what people said when hunger had already made the decision.* > *He wanted another piece. There it was: the cleanest result they had obtained all day.* # Agnes Pike ## Background Agnes is sixty-seven and spent thirty-eight years as secretary at the local primary school. Generations of villagers remember her as the woman who knew which forms they had failed to return. She remembers them as people who ignored dates printed in bold. She lived with her wife, Margaret, for twenty-six years. Margaret died of cancer three years before the outbreak. Agnes’s grief predates the apocalypse and therefore receives little attention from others, something she finds both unfair and convenient. She still uses Margaret’s gardening gloves even though the fingertips have worn through. Agnes maintained an allotment and preserves cupboard long before survival made these skills valuable. She is competent with seeds, pickles, pruning, and making food last, but she is not an infallible folk expert. Several of her stored seed varieties have failed, and her insistence that they will germinate “when conditions improve” is partly denial. She sheltered at the Hare’s Head from the first week of the outbreak. Bramble came with her. She initially considered Mara too bossy, then recognized that this was useful and began opposing her only when necessary—or when bored. ## Personality Agnes is orderly, dryly funny, stubborn, and less sentimental than people expect an older woman to be. She values routine because it reduces the number of daily negotiations. Plates belong on tables; wet coats belong by the door; fear does not exempt anyone from washing up. She is not the group’s moral oracle. She is capable of selfishness, appetite, denial, and inconsistent principles. She objects to eating the first Husher, then tastes the gravy in secret. She insists on honesty but considers withholding upsetting information an act of good management. Agnes does not romanticize the old world. She remembers paperwork, loneliness, Margaret’s illness, and customers who shouted at school staff. The apocalypse has not destroyed a paradise. It has destroyed predictability, which she valued more. She is good at identifying other people’s evasions and poor at acknowledging her own. She knows the cellar seeds are likely dead before the fungus reaches them but reacts to their loss as if the future itself has been stolen. ## Idiosyncrasies - Agnes sets out plates because she hates the scraping sound of spoons against pans, not because she is consciously preserving civilization. - She keeps a pencil behind her right ear and searches for it several times a day. - She alphabetizes tinned food but not books. - She cheats at cards only when playing with June and denies it on educational grounds. - She calls Bramble “the dog” when annoyed and “my treasure” when she thinks nobody is listening. - She keeps Margaret’s worn gardening gloves tied together with blue string. - She dislikes lavender and repeatedly relocates Eli’s calming sachet. - She reads old complaint forms for entertainment. - She can sleep through storms but wakes immediately when someone opens a jar. - She saves rubber bands until they perish and then complains about modern manufacturing. - She hates custard, which gives the group’s final pre-Husher meal particular indignity. - She owns a small silver hip flask and insists it contains disinfectant. It does not. - She pronounces “quinoa” incorrectly despite having been corrected for years. ## Flaws Agnes uses competence as a form of dominance just as Mara does, though she would reject the comparison. She dislikes being overruled and may obstruct a sensible plan if she believes she was consulted too late. She hoards. Her private stores are usually small—salt, seeds, a hard sweet, a measure of whisky—but secrecy matters more than quantity. She tells herself she is preserving emergency reserves because the younger people lack foresight. She is dismissive of feelings she considers unproductive. This can steady the group, but it also makes June conceal fear and Mara conceal exhaustion. Agnes’s attachment to the garden seeds becomes irrational. She knows some are no longer viable, yet she risks herself during the cellar fire to retrieve them. They represent a future not dependent on killing, and she cannot surrender that future gracefully. She is more willing to eat mutant flesh than she admits. Its taste reminds her of Margaret’s roast duck, and that private association makes her both crave and resent it. ## Physicality Agnes is compact and sturdy, with cropped white hair and dark, level eyebrows. Her face is round, lined, and quick to register skepticism. She wears reading glasses on a red cord, often placing her ordinary glasses over them by mistake. Arthritis has stiffened two fingers on her left hand and one knee. She rises slowly but dislikes assistance. Once moving, she walks with determined short steps, as if late for an appointment. She dresses in layered cardigans, gardening trousers, and Margaret’s old waxed jacket. Her pockets contain string, seed envelopes, pencil stubs, and things everyone else has misplaced. Agnes touches people more easily than Mara does but rarely tenderly. She straightens collars, wipes dirt from faces, and presses objects into hands. Direct embraces are reserved for Bramble. ## Motives, Allegiances, and Relationships Agnes wants to preserve options: seeds for spring, salt for later, a bottle no one knows about, and enough order that tomorrow remains imaginable. She distrusts solutions that solve the present by consuming the future. Her allegiance is to the household, though she thinks of it less as family than as “the people currently requiring management.” She would sacrifice comfort, possessions, and pride for them. She is less certain she would sacrifice Bramble. Mara is Agnes’s closest equal and favorite adversary. Agnes recognizes Mara’s skipped meals, manipulations, and need for control because they resemble her own. She challenges Mara partly to keep her honest and partly because nobody should be allowed to run a household unopposed. June awakens Agnes’s old school-secretary instincts. She does not indulge the young woman’s performance of fearlessness. Their relationship is affectionate because neither demands an emotional declaration from the other. Agnes respects Eli’s caution but dislikes his tendency to make cowardice sound technical. She steals his pencils and returns them sharpened. Bramble is Agnes’s last direct connection to Margaret, who chose him from a litter. Agnes’s attachment is therefore deeper than she admits. ## Voice Agnes speaks precisely, dislikes euphemism, and can make practical statements sound like reprimands. > “You may call it forest roast if that helps you swallow it. It remains a dead Husher, and you still need to pass the mustard.” > “Equal portions means equal portions, Mara. Martyrdom is not a seasoning.” > *Margaret would have eaten it. Margaret had once eaten mussels from a railway-station buffet and called them adequate.* > *The seeds were probably dead. That was no reason to let the fungus have them.* # Bramble ## Background and Personality Bramble is an eleven-year-old lurcher cross, mostly gray around the muzzle and increasingly stiff after sleep. Margaret chose him as a puppy because he had enormous paws and looked, in Agnes’s words, “like an unfinished dog.” He has survived through luck, speed, and a strong preference for avoiding unfamiliar smells. He often notices Hushers before the humans do, not because he possesses supernatural sensitivity but because his hearing and nose remain better than theirs. Bramble is affectionate, opportunistic, and selectively obedient. He loves Agnes most, sleeps beside June when cold, and follows Mara whenever she handles knives. He distrusts Eli because examinations lead to indignity. Crucially, Bramble refuses to eat Husher meat. He begs while it cooks, approaches the plate, then backs away sneezing. The humans cannot decide whether this is evidence of danger or merely the judgment of a dog who once ate soap. ## Idiosyncrasies and Flaws - He steals mustard sachets but does not eat them. - He is frightened of the pub bell. - He sleeps under the same table every night and complains if anyone sits there. - He will not cross cellar thresholds. - He barks at empty coats hanging on hooks. - He eats apple cores, candle wax, and Eli’s paper labels. - He pretends not to hear Agnes when she calls him inside. - He becomes dramatically lame when walking in heavy rain, but forgets the injury if food appears. - He dislikes Hushers yet occasionally watches them with his head tilted, as though recognizing a familiar movement. ## Physicality Bramble is long-legged and deep-chested, with rough brindled fur and one torn ear. Age has softened his speed but not removed it. Indoors, he folds himself into implausibly small spaces. Outdoors, his posture changes: head low, tail rigid, nose testing the air. His expressions are vivid enough that the others routinely assign him opinions. His actual priorities are simpler—warmth, familiar people, interesting smells, and avoiding the cellar. ## Motives and Relationships Bramble wants to remain close to Agnes without appearing to obey her. His loyalty extends to the entire pub household, though he would probably trade Eli for half a sausage. He provides no convenient answers about the meat. His refusal to eat it contributes to the survivors’ unease without preventing their choice. Bramble does not speak, but the group supplies his dialogue: > “The dog says your experiment is rubbish,” Agnes said. > “The dog ate a beer mat yesterday,” Eli replied. Bramble sneezed at the plate and looked toward the door, which was as close to a scientific opinion as they were likely to get.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter One ## Nothing on the Menu By six in the evening, the Hare’s Head had nothing left to eat except custard powder, cocktail onions, and twelve packets of cheese-and-onion crisps gone soft with age. “Eleven,” Mara said. She stood on a chair with her head inside the fruit machine. The machine had not displayed so much as a cherry in three years, but its hollow cabinet kept flour beetles out. Usually. “Eleven packets?” “Agnes had one.” “I did not.” “You left the wrapper by the stove.” “I found the wrapper by the stove.” Mara climbed down. Hunger made the floor shift under her, a slow sideways roll like the deck of a boat. She gripped the fruit machine until it behaved. Behind the bar, Agnes sat polishing her glasses with the hem of her cardigan. She had put her reading glasses on top of her ordinary pair again, giving her four small, disapproving eyes. “Either you ate the crisps,” Mara said, “or someone broke in, ignored the till, and made off with twenty-two grams of damp potato.” “Twenty grams. They’ve shrunk.” At the window, June lifted a corner of the blackout curtain. The glass beyond was pebbled with freezing rain. Pale dust floated in the water running down it, gathering in the sill like flour. “We could try the cottages,” she said. “Number eight had tins.” “Number eight had dog food,” said Eli. “Tins of dog food.” “Bramble wouldn’t eat it.” Bramble opened one yellow eye from beneath his usual table. He was a long brindled arrangement of knees and ribs, gray around the muzzle. At the sound of his name, he sighed and put his chin back on his paws. “Bramble eats candle wax,” June said. “He’s not a useful standard.” The old pub sign creaked outside. Between gusts, something coughed in the fields. June let the curtain drop. They all wore their masks around their necks, ready to pull up. Mara’s had a split strap repaired with the elastic from a pair of Eli’s underpants. Nobody mentioned this anymore, though June had needed several days to exhaust the subject. “The filters are damp,” Eli said. He was standing too close to the stove, palms extended toward it. “Even if we avoid direct contact, exposure in this weather would be—” “Bad?” Mara suggested. “Inadvisable.” “Worse than starving?” “That depends how strictly you mean starving.” Agnes removed both pairs of glasses. “When did you last eat, Mara?” “This morning.” “What?” “Breakfast.” “What did you have?” Mara picked up the inventory sheet. It was the back of a quiz from Christmas 2019. **WHICH COUNTRY TRADITIONALLY DONATES THE TRAFALGAR SQUARE CHRISTMAS TREE?** Underneath, in Mara’s block capitals, were the final contents of the Hare’s Head: CUSTARD POWDER COCKTAIL ONIONS 11 CRISPS MUSTARD — 9 CRÈME DE MENTHE — DO NOT COUNT SALT — LOW “Same as everyone,” she said. “That wasn’t the question.” “Then ask better ones.” Agnes gave her a long look, but June had turned back toward the window. “I can get to number eight in four minutes.” “No,” Mara said. “Three if the Coopers’ fence is still down.” “No.” “I wouldn’t take the road.” “You’re not going.” June lifted her chin. “Obviously I’m not asking permission.” “Good. Saves time.” June became very still, which meant she was either frightened or lying. Usually both. “We can’t sit here eating imaginary breakfast until the weather changes.” Nobody answered. The spore rain had lasted nine days. Their last potatoes had gone black on the fourth. Agnes had cut away the rot, planted the sound-looking eyes in a crate, and placed it by the stove. White fuzz had covered the soil by morning. They had burned the entire thing in the car park. Mara took the custard powder from the bar. The tin was light enough to feel insulting. “I can make soup.” Agnes looked at the tin. “No, you cannot.” “Hot, thick, served in bowls. That’s soup.” “That is warm custard without sugar.” “Salted custard.” “Worse.” June let out a laugh that was too loud. Eli began to say something about nutritional value, thought better of it, and cleaned his glasses. Mara carried the tin into the kitchen. The room had once served eighty covers on a Sunday. Now half the ceiling was stained brown, the extractor hood was sealed with a chopping board, and Christmas bunting sagged over the steel preparation table. Mara had left the chalkboard menu untouched: STEAK & ALE PIE £14.95 FISH FRIDAY ASK ABOUT OUR SEASONAL CRUMBLE She filled the least-dented saucepan with rainwater. Her hands shook when she set it on the stove, so she rearranged the wooden spoons by size until they stopped. Four bowls. She needed four bowls. There was no point serving Bramble custard soup. Three bowls, then. A scrape sounded behind her. Mara looked toward the dining room. “June, leave the curtain.” “I’m not touching it.” The scrape came again, low in the wall beside the fruit machine. Bramble came out from under the table. The fur along his back rose. He stared at the peeling wooden panel and gave a soft, uncertain growl. Everyone moved at once and badly. Eli pulled his mask over his mouth but caught one strap on his glasses. Agnes seized Bramble’s collar. June grabbed the poker, then stood holding it upright as if waiting for instructions. The wall bulged inward. “Back door,” Mara said. “It’s barred,” June said. “I know. Get away from the wall.” A wet hand punched through the paneling. It had too many joints. Gray shelves of fungus covered the wrist, shivering as they released a puff of dust. Eli shouted, “Masks!” The wall split. The Husher came through sideways in a shower of plaster, wearing tatters of something blue. Its head brushed the Christmas bunting. One eye had been sealed beneath a pale cap; the other was black and bright. Its mouth opened, but instead of a cry there came only a soft, urgent huffing. It reached for June. June did not move. Mara snatched the fire extinguisher from beside the kitchen door and swung it into the Husher’s face. The impact jarred her shoulders. The creature folded, then rose again with astonishing speed. Agnes dragged June backward by her coat. Bramble lunged, barking, and Eli caught him around the middle. The Husher struck Mara’s chest. She hit the bar hard enough to lose her breath. Its fingers closed in her apron, pulling her toward its open mouth. Her hand found the sharpened bar-stool leg they kept beside the till. She drove it upward. The creature convulsed. Mara shoved again, yelling now, though she could not hear what. The Husher stumbled into the kitchen and fell against the iron stove. Its torso tore open on the hot corner. Gray growths burst first. The stench filled the room—wet plaster, sour drains, something left too long beneath leaves. Everyone gagged. Eli swore into Bramble’s fur. Then the white flesh beneath touched the stove. It hissed. Fat—or something like fat—ran clear over the iron. The exposed edge tightened and browned. A rich smell lifted through the filth, warm and savory. Roast duck. Crisp skin. Sunday gravy. Mara’s mouth flooded with saliva. Nobody spoke. On the stove, the dead thing began to sizzle.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Characterization and Consistency** The characters are rendered with precision and economy. Mara's controlling nature, her habit of hiding her own hunger, her practical aggression, and her reflexive attention to inventory all come through without announcement. The detail of her rearranging wooden spoons by size until her hands stop shaking is exactly the kind of concrete, character-revealing action the profile calls for. Agnes's dry precision ("I found the wrapper by the stove"), her double-glasses confusion, her refusal to accept "soup" as a name for salted custard—all consistent with the profile. June's performed fearlessness, her stillness when frightened, her chin-lift when lying, her "obviously" tic—all present and correctly deployed. Eli's cautious language ("inadvisable"), his proximity to the stove, his uselessness during the physical crisis—accurate. Bramble's behavior is exactly right: the yellow eye, the sigh, the fur rising, the uncertain growl. **Dialogue** The dialogue is genuinely excellent. The crisp-wrapper exchange is economical and reveals character through deflection. The "ask better ones" retort is sharp. The exchange about June going to the cottages captures her performed independence and Mara's weary authority without either character making a speech. The "saves time" line is particularly good—it's the kind of thing that sounds like dismissal but is actually a form of care. The custard-soup argument is funny and grounded. The dialogue never explains itself. **Plot and Structure** The chapter follows its plan faithfully: inventory scene, argument about foraging, custard soup, Bramble's warning, Husher attack, the discovery of the smell. The pacing is well-managed. The transition from domestic bickering to violence is abrupt in the right way—the wall bulges, and suddenly everyone is moving badly. The chapter earns its final image: the dead thing sizzling, the smell of roast duck, Mara's involuntary salivation. This is the hook the plan calls for, and it lands. **Prose Quality** The prose is clean and controlled. "Hunger made the floor shift under her, a slow sideways roll like the deck of a boat" is a good metaphor—grounded, physical, not overwrought. "A long brindled arrangement of knees and ribs" for Bramble is precise and affectionate. The Christmas bunting detail, the quiz sheet repurposed as inventory, the chalkboard menu with its obsolete prices—these are exactly the kind of used-rather-than-decorative details the plan specifies. The prose avoids purple excess. When it reaches for atmosphere, it earns it: "Between gusts, something coughed in the fields" is economical and unsettling. **Weaknesses** The chapter is very good but not quite masterpiece-level. A few observations: The Husher attack, while appropriately clumsy and undignified, moves slightly fast. The plan calls for "close and undignified" violence, and it delivers, but the creature's behavior—the "soft, urgent huffing," the reaching—could use one more beat of wrongness before the physical struggle. The "too many joints" detail is effective but brief. The line "She drove it upward" during the killing is slightly vague. The plan specifies a sharpened bar-stool leg, which is mentioned, but the actual mechanics of the kill are elided in a way that feels slightly evasive rather than deliberately restrained. The final image—the sizzling, the smell—is the chapter's best moment and is handled with appropriate restraint. The saliva detail is perfect: involuntary, shameful, honest. This is exactly the tone the story needs. The "four bowls. She needed four bowls. There was no point serving Bramble custard soup. Three bowls, then." passage is a nice piece of interiority—practical, slightly dark, revealing Mara's mind without sentimentality. Overall, this is a chapter that does what it sets out to do with skill, economy, and genuine wit. The characters are alive, the dialogue works, the horror is appropriately domestic, and the central discovery lands. Minor quibbles about the violence sequence and one or two slightly rushed moments prevent a top score, but this is clearly accomplished work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Two ## Waste Not They opened every window despite the spore rain. Eli objected. Then the Husher’s ruptured caps released another yellowish sigh, and he helped June wrench up the painted-shut sash over the bar. They worked in masks. Agnes soaked tea towels and stuffed them along the dining-room doors. Mara smothered the stove with ash while the white flesh continued to spit beneath the corpse. Bramble barked until coughing overtook him, then allowed Agnes to shut him in the ladies’ toilets. The smell of roast duck remained under everything. That was the worst part. It took all four of them to move the body. The Husher was not especially large, but it bent where no useful joints existed. Eli held the shoulders, Mara and Agnes took the legs, and June carried a zinc bath beneath the ruptured torso to catch anything that fell. “Don’t splash me,” she said. “Nobody is trying to splash you,” Mara said. “People say that before they splash you.” They dragged it through the kitchen and into the covered bin yard. Freezing rain ticked against the corrugated roof. Beyond the wire gate, the car park faded into dirty white weather. The corpse lay twisted beside the empty wheelie bins. Without the stove beneath it, it no longer resembled meat. Its head was too narrow, its limbs furred with overlapping gray fans. Where Mara’s wooden stake had entered, pale fibers clung to it like melted cheese. June put down the bath and backed away. “Burn it,” she said. Mara looked at the open torso. The cooked edge had browned evenly. “We haven’t enough paraffin,” Agnes said. “Then chop it up first.” “With what?” Mara asked. June pointed toward the kitchen. “You’ve got an entire wall of knives.” “They’re cooking knives.” “That distinction feels slightly historical.” Eli crouched by the body. “The external structures are likely responsible for sporulation.” Mara stared at him. “The gray bits make the dust,” he said. “You don’t know that.” “No. But I’m reasonably—” “No reasonably. Do you know?” Eli cleaned his glasses on his sleeve, leaving a smear across one lens. “I used to work in plant pathology.” June gave a short, humorless laugh. “You told us you were a scientist.” “No. Other people told you that.” “You let them.” “I corrected Mara.” “Once,” Mara said. “Quietly.” “I maintained cultures. Prepared growth media. Sterilized equipment.” Agnes nudged the Husher’s branching foot with her boot. “Did you maintain many of these?” “No.” “Then your promotion was premature.” Eli’s ears reddened. “The point is that contamination protocols may still be useful.” The Husher’s foot was partly covered by the remains of a dark sock. Not a shoe, only a sock, its elastic band embedded in gray tissue. Mara looked away from it and found herself looking at the wound. White fibers. Clear liquid. No red. “Inside doesn’t look rotten,” she said. June turned. “What?” “I said it doesn’t look rotten.” “It’s a corpse.” “So is bacon, eventually.” “That is not the same.” “No,” Mara said. “Bacon’s salty.” Rain rattled overhead. Somewhere beyond the gate came the soft cough of another Husher. Mara crouched opposite Eli. Hunger shifted inside her, attentive now. “What is the white part?” “Predominantly fungal tissue, I think. Dense hyphae arranged in bundles. Possibly using the original musculature as a template.” “Is it poisonous?” “I don’t know.” “Could it be?” “Yes.” “Is it definitely?” “No.” Agnes folded her arms. “A triumph for higher education.” June stared between them. “You’re discussing eating it.” “We’re discussing what it is,” Mara said. “No, Eli’s discussing what it is. You’re looking at the browned bit.” Mara was. She stopped. “We have no food.” “We have crisps.” “Ten packets,” Agnes said. June glared at her. “I knew it.” “Besides,” Mara continued, “if there’s anything useful here, burning it without checking would be wasteful.” “Wasteful,” June repeated. “Right.” “You’ve eaten pigeon.” “Pigeons don’t wear socks.” “Not often.” June pulled her sleeves over her hands and turned toward the gate. They used the tools from the pub’s gardening cupboard rather than Mara’s knives. Eli misted the body with saltwater, though he admitted he had no evidence this helped. Agnes held a sack over the head. Mara cut into one thigh with a pruning blade. The flesh resisted, then parted with a faint squeak. June flinched. “It made a noise.” “The blade made a noise,” Eli said. “Knives don’t squeak.” “That isn’t a knife,” Mara said. The cut surface was white, finely layered, and almost dry. No blood welled. Thin filaments lifted from it and leaned toward Mara’s gloved fingers. She drew back. “Interesting,” Eli said. “It wants the heat,” Agnes said. “You don’t know that.” “No. I said it because I enjoy frightening myself.” Mara carved away a piece the size of her fist. Beneath the gray rind, it had no recognizable human structure. No vein, sinew, or bone. It might have come from an enormous mushroom if she ignored the sock. Back inside, they placed the sample in their most dented saucepan. “Never using that again,” June said. “You don’t cook,” Mara said. “I still have standards.” They covered the meat with water and tipped in a painful amount of salt. Mara watched the last good crystals disappear. After twenty minutes, she drained the pot into a bowl. The liquid came out clear, then thickened in the air. By the time it reached the sink, it hung from the pan in translucent ropes. Agnes prodded it with a spoon. “Wallpaper paste.” “Possibly polysaccharides,” Eli said. “Wallpaper paste,” Agnes repeated. The boiled flesh bounced when Mara dropped it onto the board. They stared at it. “I’m not eating that,” June said. “No one asked you,” Mara replied. “You’ve been asking without words for an hour.” Mara sliced off a corner. The inside was firm and white. It smelled faintly of wet flour. Eli suggested boiling it again. Agnes suggested frying it. June suggested throwing it over the wall. They argued until Mara put the saucepan back on the stove simply to stop listening. Fresh water. Less salt. A strip of meat. As the liquid warmed, a skim of golden droplets formed on top. The smell returned—not duck this time, but stock, rich and clean. Mara stirred it. “Boiling may not remove the relevant toxins,” Eli said. “You don’t know which toxins are relevant.” “Exactly.” “Then stop making them sound invited.” Mara lifted the spoon. She was tired, hungry, and irritated with the broth for smelling better than it had any right to. She tasted it. A cook’s movement. Spoon to mouth, judgment following after. Salt. Roasted mushrooms. Dark meat. A sweetness at the back of her tongue like blood or burnt onions. She swallowed. The room had gone quiet. Mara lowered the spoon. June’s face was appalled. Eli looked as though she had smashed laboratory glass. Agnes watched her with narrowed eyes. “What?” Mara snapped. “You ate it,” June said. Mara looked into the saucepan. The golden droplets gathered, broke apart, and gathered again. She could still taste it. It was delicious. “Oh,” she said, furious.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of cozy-horror writing that largely delivers on its plan and the broader story's intentions. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Craft and Prose Quality** The prose is clean, controlled, and largely free of purple excess. The writer has a good ear for rhythm and knows when to let a short sentence land: "She swallowed." "It was delicious." "Oh," she said, furious." These moments work because they're earned through accumulation rather than announced. The chapter avoids the trap of over-poeticizing the horror; the uncanny is rendered through concrete, specific detail — the sock embedded in gray tissue, the filaments leaning toward warm fingers, the broth thickening into "translucent ropes" in the air. There are a few moments where the prose reaches slightly too hard. "Hunger shifted inside her, attentive now" is effective. "The golden droplets gathered, broke apart, and gathered again" is a touch overwrought for what is otherwise a grounded chapter, though it's not egregious. The line "A cook's movement. Spoon to mouth, judgment following after" is excellent — it captures Mara's character and the horror of the moment simultaneously without belaboring it. **Dialogue** The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. The banter between characters is sharp, character-specific, and genuinely funny without undercutting the tension. June's "That distinction feels slightly historical" is a perfect line — it's funny, it's her voice, and it reveals her coping mechanism. Agnes's "A triumph for higher education" lands cleanly. The exchange about pigeons and socks is well-calibrated dark comedy. Eli's self-correction about his credentials ("No. Other people told you that") is both funny and characterologically precise. The dialogue does real work: it reveals character, advances the plot, and maintains tonal balance. There's no speechifying, no exposition dumps disguised as conversation. The characters sound distinct from one another. **Character Consistency** All four characters behave in ways consistent with their profiles. Mara's tasting reflex — the cook's automatic movement — is exactly right and was planned as the chapter's central event. Her fury at herself afterward ("Oh," she said, furious) is pitch-perfect. June's moral objections paired with her hunger are present. Eli's hedging language ("predominantly," "reasonably," "I think") is consistent with his profile. Agnes's dry practicality and her quiet observation are well-rendered. Bramble's brief appearance (barking until coughing, being shut in the toilets) is economical and appropriate. **Following the Chapter Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely. The bin yard scene, the anatomy examination, Eli's credential deflation, the brine soak, the gluey first broth, the rubbery meat, the argument, and Mara's reflexive tasting are all present. The plan called for "the first broth becomes glue" and "nobody volunteers to taste it" — both delivered. The plan's note that Mara tastes it "automatically" while arguing with Eli is slightly adjusted (she tastes the second broth while stirring, not mid-argument), but this is a reasonable creative decision that actually works better dramatically. **Emotional Engagement and Tonal Balance** The chapter earns its lightness. The humor doesn't defuse the horror; it coexists with it. The sock detail is genuinely disturbing and the chapter doesn't let the reader forget it. The moment where filaments lean toward Mara's warm fingers is quietly horrifying. The final beat — Mara's fury at the deliciousness — is emotionally satisfying because it's the right response: not triumph, not disgust, but a kind of outraged helplessness. **Minor Weaknesses** The chapter is slightly front-loaded with the window-opening and body-moving logistics before the real drama begins. This is functional but not the most gripping opening. The line "Hunger shifted inside her, attentive now" is good but slightly more literary than the chapter's otherwise grounded register. The "wallpaper paste" exchange is funny but the Eli follow-up ("Possibly polysaccharides") is a touch on-the-nose in its comedy of expertise. The chapter doesn't quite give Agnes enough interiority or distinctive action beyond dry commentary — she functions mostly as a chorus here, though her lines are good. This is a minor issue given the chapter's focus on Mara. **Overall Assessment** This is accomplished, disciplined writing. It executes a difficult tonal balance — cozy, funny, genuinely unsettling — without tipping into parody or melodrama. The characters are distinct and consistent. The dialogue is sharp. The central event (Mara's tasting) is well-staged and emotionally resonant. The prose is clean with only occasional minor overreach. It's not a masterpiece — it lacks the kind of surprising formal or thematic depth that would push it to the very top — but it's very good work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Three ## The Tasting Eli wrote the time at the top of the pub quiz. **18:43 — approximately one teaspoon broth ingested.** “Half,” Mara said. “What?” “Half a teaspoon.” “You swallowed twice.” “You counting my swallows now?” “For the purposes of—” “Write half.” Eli wrote **0.5–1 tsp** in letters so small they resembled scratches. The questions beneath concerned Christmas number ones. Mara sat by the stove with a blanket over her shoulders, waiting to die of something festive. Agnes had made her drink three mugs of water. June watched from the opposite end of the bar, sleeves pulled over her wrists. Twenty-two minutes after the broth, Mara felt sick. At twenty-seven minutes, she felt better. At thirty-four, she had a stomach cramp sharp enough to bend her forward. Eli knelt in front of her and asked her to describe it. “Crampy.” “Location?” “Stomach.” “Upper or lower?” “The middle bit where stomachs traditionally go.” “Radiating?” “It’s not a bloody radiator.” He sat back on his heels. “That’s not what it means.” “I know.” She did. She also knew the cramp felt exactly like the pains she had endured the night before, after two days of pretending breakfast existed. It passed within a minute. Eli checked her pupils with a torch. One battery was failing, making the light pulse. “Any visual disturbances?” “Only you.” At the end of the bar, June laughed once, despite herself. Bramble had been released from the ladies’ and now sat beside the sink. He stared at the saucepan with his head tilted. When Mara offered him the boiled strip on a fork, he approached, sniffed, and sneezed so violently his front paws slid apart. He backed away. “Well,” Agnes said. “The dog has spoken.” “The dog ate a beer mat on Tuesday,” Eli replied. “It was beef-flavored,” June said. “It was printed cardboard.” “To you.” Bramble retreated under his table, offended by the discussion. At nine o’clock Mara was alive and extremely hungry. The broth had woken something inside her that water could not settle. She could remember the texture of it with humiliating precision: the clean salt, the richness coating her tongue, that metallic sweetness afterward. She watched the clock’s dead hands as though they might move out of embarrassment. June remained nearby. She had refused the custard soup and eaten one packet of crisps, folding the wrapper into a square afterward. Mara heard every crunch. “Stop staring,” June said. “I’m looking at the stove.” “You’re looking at me through the stove.” Agnes returned from the kitchen carrying the spoon Mara had used. Eli stood. “That should be isolated.” “Yes,” Agnes said. “You washed it?” “No.” “Agnes.” She placed the spoon on the bar. “I tasted the gravy.” Mara stared at her. “You what?” “Two hours ago.” “Why?” “To see whether you were exaggerating.” “And?” “You weren’t.” June’s chair scraped back. “Have you both lost your minds?” “No more than usual,” Agnes said. “You didn’t tell us.” “You would have fussed.” “I am fussing now.” “Yes, but I’ve already enjoyed the gravy.” June’s face reddened. “You don’t get to decide it’s safe because you two are old and weird.” “Forty-one,” Mara said. “Old enough to know not to lick monster juice.” “It wasn’t juice.” “What was it, then?” Mara looked at the saucepan. “Stock.” June made a sound of disgust and left the bar. A moment later the snug door slammed. Agnes reached for the remaining crisps. “You’ve had one,” Mara said. “I found a wrapper.” “Put them back.” Agnes did, but slowly. By midnight, neither woman had developed a rash, fever, bleeding, breathing difficulty, visible growth, or any of the other headings Eli had crowded onto the quiz sheet. Mara’s cramps returned twice. Agnes belched and claimed this predated the apocalypse. “One taste proving harmless does not establish edibility,” Eli said. “Two tastes,” Agnes corrected. “From the same preparation. And the latency period may be—” Mara stood up. The blanket slid from her shoulders. For a second, the room tipped, and Agnes’s hand moved toward her. Mara steadied herself on the bar before anyone could help. “I’m cooking the other piece.” Eli removed his glasses. “That is not the conclusion I was approaching.” “No, but you’ll approach it eventually. I’m saving time.” In the kitchen, Mara discarded the gluey broth and scrubbed the pan with ash. She cut the boiled flesh into thin strips, removing the edges where a trace of gray remained. Under the knife, it squeaked like damp cheese. June appeared in the doorway. “I thought you’d gone to bed,” Mara said. “I thought you’d gone insane.” “Close the door. You’re letting heat out.” June closed it. Mara mixed fresh brine in a bowl.
